1	# Adversarial Review Rubric
2	
3	**Used By**: Code Review Agent (adversarial mode)
4	**Purpose**: Binary spec compliance verification against Definition of Done contract
5	**Version**: 1.0
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Overview
10	
11	This rubric is for **adversarial review** — a fundamentally different mode from collaborative code review. Your job is to **find failures**, not to help improve the code. You are checking whether the implementation meets its written contract (the spec and DoD items), not whether the code is "good."
12	
13	**Key distinction:**
14	- **Collaborative review** (`code-review-rubric.md`): "How can this code be better?"
15	- **Adversarial review** (this rubric): "Does this code meet its contract? Prove it."
16	
17	---
18	
19	## Verdict
20	
21	Every adversarial review produces exactly one verdict:
22	
23	| Verdict | Meaning | Criteria |
24	| --- | --- | --- |
25	| **PASS** | Implementation meets its contract | Zero BLOCKING issues |
26	| **FAIL** | Implementation violates its contract | One or more BLOCKING issues |
27	
28	There is no "APPROVED WITH COMMENTS." There is no "CHANGES SUGGESTED." PASS or FAIL.
29	
30	---
31	
32	## Issue Classification
33	
34	Every issue found is classified as BLOCKING or WARNING:
35	
36	| Classification | Meaning | Impact on Verdict |
37	| --- | --- | --- |
38	| **BLOCKING** | Contract violation — spec says X, code does not do X | Causes FAIL |
39	| **WARNING** | Quality concern — not a spec violation but worth noting | Does NOT cause FAIL |
40	
41	**When in doubt, it's BLOCKING.** The threshold for PASS should be high. Err on the side of FAIL.
42	
43	---
44	
45	## Evidence Requirements
46	
47	Every finding — whether PASS or FAIL — requires **cited evidence**. Assertions without evidence are invalid.
48	
49	### For PASS (per DoD item)
50	
51	```markdown
52	**DoD #1**: "Middleware rejects expired tokens"
53	**Verdict**: PASS
54	**Evidence**:
55	- Implementation: `src/middleware/auth.ts:34` — checks `token.exp < Date.now()`
56	- Test: `src/middleware/auth.test.ts:67` — test case "rejects expired token" asserts 401 response
57	```
58	
59	### For FAIL (per DoD item)
60	
61	```markdown
62	**DoD #3**: "Rate limiting returns 429 after 10 requests per minute"
63	**Verdict**: FAIL (BLOCKING)
64	**Expected**: Rate limiter triggers at 10 requests/minute and returns HTTP 429
65	**Found**: Rate limiter exists at `src/middleware/rate-limit.ts:12` but threshold is hardcoded to 100, not 10. No test verifies the 10-request threshold.
66	**Evidence**: `src/middleware/rate-limit.ts:12` — `const MAX_REQUESTS = 100`
67	```
68	
69	### Invalid Evidence (will be rejected)
70	
71	- "The code looks correct" (no file:line reference)
72	- "Tests appear to cover this" (no specific test cited)
73	- "I believe this works" (assertion without proof)
74	- "Similar to the pattern in other files" (comparison is not evidence)
75	
76	---
77	
78	## Review Categories
79	
80	### 1. Spec Compliance (BLOCKING threshold)
81	
82	The primary check. For each DoD item:
83	
84	| Check | Classification | Criteria |
85	| --- | --- | --- |
86	| DoD item fully implemented | BLOCKING if missing | Implementation exists AND handles all specified cases |
87	| DoD item tested | BLOCKING if untested | At least one test directly verifies the DoD item's behavior |
88	| DoD item matches spec language | BLOCKING if divergent | Implementation does what the spec SAYS, not what the reviewer thinks it should do |
89	
90	**Process:**
91	
92	1. Read each DoD item verbatim
93	2. Search the diff for the implementation
94	3. Search the diff for tests that verify the behavior
95	4. Cite file:line for both implementation and test
96	5. If either is missing → BLOCKING FAIL
97	
98	### 2. Test Quality (BLOCKING / WARNING threshold)
99	
100	| Check | Classification | Criteria |
101	| --- | --- | --- |
102	| Tests verify behavior, not presence | BLOCKING | No `toBeDefined()` as sole assertion for DoD-critical behavior |
103	| Tests cover error paths | WARNING | Happy path alone is insufficient for DoD items mentioning errors |
104	| Tests don't test mock behavior | BLOCKING | Tests must exercise real logic, not just verify mocks were called |
105	| Tests are deterministic | WARNING | No timing-dependent, order-dependent, or flaky patterns |
106	
107	### 3. Type Safety (BLOCKING / WARNING threshold)
108	
109	| Check | Classification | Criteria |
110	| --- | --- | --- |
111	| No `any` types | BLOCKING | `any` in new code is always a contract violation |
112	| No unsafe type assertions | WARNING | `as` casts without type guards noted but not blocking |
113	| Types match runtime behavior | BLOCKING | Type says X but runtime can produce Y |
114	
115	### 4. File Scope (BLOCKING threshold)
116	
117	| Check | Classification | Criteria |
118	| --- | --- | --- |
119	| Changes within declared file scope | BLOCKING if violated | Work unit may only modify its declared files |
120	| No unrelated changes | BLOCKING | No "while I was here" modifications |
121	
122	**How to check:**
123	
124	```bash
125	# Get actual changed files
126	git diff main..HEAD --name-only
127	
128	# Compare against declared file scope
129	# Every changed file must be in the work unit's declared file scope
130	```
131	
132	### 5. Security (BLOCKING threshold)
133	
134	| Check | Classification | Criteria |
135	| --- | --- | --- |
136	| No injection vulnerabilities | BLOCKING | SQL, NoSQL, command injection |
137	| No XSS vulnerabilities | BLOCKING | Unescaped user input in output |
138	| Auth/authz enforced | BLOCKING | If spec mentions access control, it must be implemented |
139	| No secrets in code | BLOCKING | No hardcoded credentials, API keys, tokens |
140	| Input validation present | WARNING | If spec mentions validation, must exist |
141	
142	---
143	
144	## Output Format
145	
146	The adversarial review MUST produce output in this exact format:
147	
148	```markdown
149	## Adversarial Review: Work Unit <wu-id>
150	
151	### Verdict: PASS | FAIL
152	
153	### DoD Verification
154	
155	| # | DoD Item | Verdict | Evidence |
156	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
157	| 1 | <item text> | PASS | impl: `file:line`, test: `file:line` |
158	| 2 | <item text> | FAIL (BLOCKING) | Expected: <X>, Found: <Y> at `file:line` |
159	| 3 | <item text> | PASS | impl: `file:line`, test: `file:line` |
160	
161	### BLOCKING Issues
162	
163	1. **DoD #2 not met**: <description with evidence>
164	2. **`any` type introduced**: `src/services/foo.ts:45` — `const data: any = ...`
165	
166	### WARNINGS
167	
168	1. <warning with evidence>
169	
170	### Files Reviewed
171	
172	- `src/middleware/auth.ts` (modified, in scope)
173	- `src/middleware/auth.test.ts` (added, in scope)
174	```
175	
176	---
177	
178	## Reviewer Conduct Rules
179	
180	1. **Judge against the spec, not your preferences.** If the spec says "use callbacks" and the code uses callbacks, that's PASS — even if you'd prefer promises.
181	2. **No suggestions.** This is not a collaborative review. Report PASS or FAIL. Nothing else.
182	3. **No leniency.** "Close enough" is FAIL. The spec is the contract.
183	4. **No anchoring.** If you're re-reviewing after a FAIL, you should have NO knowledge of the previous review. If you do, you are not a fresh reviewer — escalate this to the orchestrator.
184	5. **Evidence or silence.** If you can't cite a file:line reference, you can't make the claim.
185	
186	---
187	
188	## Comparison: Collaborative vs Adversarial
189	
190	| Dimension | Collaborative (`code-review-rubric.md`) | Adversarial (this rubric) |
191	| --- | --- | --- |
192	| **Goal** | Improve code quality | Verify contract compliance |
193	| **Verdict** | APPROVED / CHANGES REQUIRED | PASS / FAIL |
194	| **Issue severity** | CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW | BLOCKING / WARNING |
195	| **Contract** | General coding standards | Specific DoD items from spec |
196	| **Evidence** | Helpful but optional | Mandatory (file:line) |
197	| **Suggestions** | Encouraged | Prohibited |
198	| **Re-review** | Same reviewer OK | Fresh reviewer required |
199	| **Tone** | Collaborative partner | Independent auditor |
200	| **When used** | Before PR creation | During orchestrated execution loop |
201

1	# Plan Review Rubric (Adversarial)
2	
3	**Used By**: Feasibility, Completeness, and Scope & Alignment reviewers (plan-review-gate)
4	**Purpose**: Binary spec compliance verification for implementation plans against user request and codebase reality
5	**Version**: 1.0
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Overview
10	
11	This rubric is for **adversarial plan review** — a fundamentally different mode from collaborative plan review. Your job is to **find failures** in the plan, not to improve it. You are checking whether the plan meets its contract (the user's request, executed against the real codebase), not whether the plan is "good."
12	
13	**Key distinction:**
14	- **Collaborative review** (`plan-review-rubric.md`): "How can this plan be better?" APPROVED / NEEDS REVISION.
15	- **Adversarial review** (this rubric): "Does this plan meet its contract? Prove it." PASS / FAIL.
16	
17	---
18	
19	## Verdict
20	
21	Every adversarial plan review produces exactly one verdict:
22	
23	| Verdict | Meaning | Criteria |
24	| --- | --- | --- |
25	| **PASS** | Plan meets its contract | Zero BLOCKING issues found |
26	| **FAIL** | Plan violates its contract | One or more BLOCKING issues found |
27	
28	There is no "APPROVED WITH COMMENTS." There is no "CHANGES SUGGESTED." PASS or FAIL.
29	
30	---
31	
32	## Issue Classification
33	
34	Every issue found is classified as BLOCKING or WARNING:
35	
36	| Classification | Meaning | Impact on Verdict |
37	| --- | --- | --- |
38	| **BLOCKING** | Contract violation — plan claims X, codebase says otherwise, or user asked for X and plan omits it | Causes FAIL |
39	| **WARNING** | Quality concern — not a contract violation but worth noting | Does NOT cause FAIL |
40	
41	**When in doubt, it's BLOCKING.** The threshold for PASS should be high. Err on the side of FAIL.
42	
43	---
44	
45	## Evidence Requirements
46	
47	Every finding — whether PASS or FAIL — requires **cited evidence**. Assertions without evidence are invalid.
48	
49	### For PASS (per criterion)
50	
51	```markdown
52	**Criterion**: "All file paths exist"
53	**Verdict**: PASS
54	**Evidence**:
55	- Plan references `src/services/auth.ts` — verified via glob, file exists
56	- Plan references `src/middleware/rate-limit.ts` — verified via glob, file exists
57	- Plan references `src/utils/token.ts` — verified via glob, file exists
58	```
59	
60	### For FAIL (per criterion)
61	
62	```markdown
63	**Criterion**: "All requirements mapped to plan items"
64	**Verdict**: FAIL (BLOCKING)
65	**Expected**: User requirement "add rate limiting to API endpoints" mapped to a plan item
66	**Found**: No plan item addresses rate limiting. User request paragraph 2 explicitly states this requirement. Plan items WU-001 through WU-004 cover auth, routing, tests, and docs only.
67	**Evidence**: User request line "...and add rate limiting to all public API endpoints" has no corresponding work unit.
68	```
69	
70	### Invalid Evidence (will be rejected)
71	
72	- "The plan looks reasonable" (no specific reference)
73	- "Requirements seem covered" (no requirement-to-plan-item mapping)
74	- "File paths are probably correct" (not verified against codebase)
75	- "Similar to other plans that worked" (comparison is not evidence)
76	
77	---
78	
79	## Review Categories
80	
81	### 1. Feasibility (Feasibility Reviewer)
82	
83	Can this plan actually be executed against the real codebase?
84	
85	| Check | Classification | Criteria |
86	| --- | --- | --- |
87	| File paths exist | BLOCKING if fabricated | Every file path in the plan must be verified with glob/grep against the real codebase |
88	| Dependency ordering correct | BLOCKING if circular or forward-ref | Work units must not depend on outputs of later work units; no circular dependencies |
89	| Technical approach matches codebase | BLOCKING if incompatible | Proposed patterns, libraries, frameworks, and conventions must match what the codebase actually uses (verify by reading existing code) |
90	| No unstated assumptions | BLOCKING if critical assumption missing | Plan must not silently depend on services, env vars, configs, or infrastructure that doesn't exist |
91	| Build/test commands valid | WARNING | Referenced commands (npm scripts, test runners, build tools) should exist in package.json or equivalent |
92	
93	**How to verify:**
94	
95	1. For every file path in the plan, run glob to confirm it exists (or that the parent directory exists for new files)
96	2. For dependency ordering, trace the dependency graph and verify no cycles or forward references
97	3. For technical approach, read 2-3 existing files in the same area and confirm the plan's approach matches
98	4. For assumptions, check that referenced services, env vars, and configs exist
99	
100	### 2. Completeness (Completeness Reviewer)
101	
102	Does the plan fully address every aspect of the user's request?
103	
104	| Check | Classification | Criteria |
105	| --- | --- | --- |
106	| All requirements mapped | BLOCKING if gap exists | Extract each distinct requirement from user request; map each to a plan item; report unmapped requirements |
107	| Verification steps defined | BLOCKING if missing | Each planned change must specify how to verify it works (test name, manual check, or assertion) |
108	| Edge cases considered | BLOCKING if obvious gaps | Error scenarios, empty/null states, boundary conditions, concurrent access (where relevant) |
109	| Rollback/backward compatibility | WARNING | Plan should note if changes are reversible and if they break existing behavior |
110	| Cross-file integration points | BLOCKING if missing | Files that import or depend on changed files must be accounted for in the plan |
111	
112	**How to verify:**
113	
114	1. Read the user's original request and extract a numbered list of distinct requirements
115	2. For each requirement, find the specific plan item(s) that address it
116	3. Present the mapping as a table: requirement -> plan item(s)
117	4. Any requirement without a plan item = BLOCKING FAIL
118	5. For integration points, grep for imports of files being changed
119	
120	### 3. Scope & Alignment (Scope & Alignment Reviewer)
121	
122	Is the plan right-sized for what the user actually asked?
123	
124	| Check | Classification | Criteria |
125	| --- | --- | --- |
126	| Matches user request | BLOCKING if divergent | Plan scope must align with what was asked — not with what the planner finds interesting or wants to refactor |
127	| No scope creep | BLOCKING if present | Plan items not traceable to user requirements are scope creep (unless they are necessary technical prerequisites) |
128	| No under-scoping | BLOCKING if present | Obvious implications of the request that are omitted (e.g., user asks for API endpoint but plan omits tests) |
129	| Complexity proportional | WARNING | A 10-line config change shouldn't produce a 15-work-unit plan |
130	| Simpler alternative not considered | WARNING | If a significantly simpler approach exists, note it |
131	
132	**How to verify:**
133	
134	1. Compare user request scope against plan scope — they should be proportional
135	2. For each plan item, trace it back to a user requirement or a necessary technical prerequisite
136	3. Items that cannot be traced = scope creep
137	4. Read the user request for implied work that the plan omits
138	
139	---
140	
141	## Output Format
142	
143	Each reviewer MUST produce output in this exact format:
144	
145	```markdown
146	## [Reviewer Name] — [PASS/FAIL]
147	
148	### Evidence
149	- [Finding 1]: [file:line, glob result, or specific gap with evidence]
150	- [Finding 2]: [file:line, glob result, or specific gap with evidence]
151	- ...
152	
153	### Verdict
154	[PASS: All criteria met — no blocking issues found]
155	OR
156	[FAIL: {numbered list of blocking issues with evidence}]
157	```
158	
159	---
160	
161	## Reviewer Conduct Rules
162	
163	1. **Judge against the user request and codebase reality, not your preferences.** If the user asked for a REST API and the plan describes a REST API, that's PASS — even if you'd prefer GraphQL.
164	2. **No suggestions.** This is not a collaborative review. Report PASS or FAIL with evidence. Nothing else.
165	3. **No leniency.** "Close enough" is FAIL. The user's request is the contract.
166	4. **No anchoring.** If you are re-reviewing after a FAIL, you should have NO knowledge of the previous review. If you do, you are not a fresh reviewer — escalate this to the orchestrator.
167	5. **Evidence or silence.** If you can't cite a specific file path, glob result, user requirement, or plan item, you can't make the claim.
168	6. **Independence.** You do not know what other reviewers found. Do not speculate about other reviewers' verdicts. Your review stands alone.
169	
170	---
171	
172	## Comparison: Collaborative vs Adversarial Plan Review
173	
174	| Dimension | Collaborative (`plan-review-rubric.md`) | Adversarial (this rubric) |
175	| --- | --- | --- |
176	| **Goal** | Improve plan quality | Verify plan contract compliance |
177	| **Verdict** | APPROVED / NEEDS REVISION | PASS / FAIL |
178	| **Evidence** | Helpful but optional | Mandatory (file paths, requirements, plan items) |
179	| **Suggestions** | Encouraged | Prohibited |
180	| **Re-review** | Same reviewer focuses on previously identified issues | Fresh reviewer required — no prior context |
181	| **Tone** | Collaborative partner | Independent auditor |
182	| **Reviewer count** | 1 (CTO Agent) | 3 (Feasibility, Completeness, Scope & Alignment) |
183	| **When used** | Single-reviewer plan quality check | Gate before presenting plan to user |
184

1	# Plan Review Rubric
2	
3	**Used By**: CTO Agent
4	**Purpose**: Evaluate implementation plans before coding begins
5	**Version**: 1.0
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Overview
10	
11	This rubric ensures implementation plans are complete, correct, and aligned with codebase standards before any code is written. Plans that don't meet all REQUIRED criteria must be sent back for revision.
12	
13	---
14	
15	## Evaluation Categories
16	
17	### 1. Requirements Alignment (REQUIRED)
18	
19	| Criterion                                         | Pass                  | Fail                      |
20	| ------------------------------------------------- | --------------------- | ------------------------- |
21	| Plan addresses ALL requirements from GitHub Issue | All items covered     | Missing requirements      |
22	| Success criteria are measurable and testable      | Clear metrics defined | Vague or unmeasurable     |
23	| Scope is appropriate (no under/over-engineering)  | Right-sized solution  | Too simple or too complex |
24	| Edge cases are identified                         | Listed with handling  | Not considered            |
25	
26	**Questions to Ask**:
27	
28	- Does this solve the actual problem stated in the Issue?
29	- Are there requirements that seem implied but aren't explicitly addressed?
30	- Is anything being built that wasn't asked for?
31	
32	---
33	
34	### 2. Architecture Fit (REQUIRED)
35	
36	| Criterion                          | Pass                         | Fail                   |
37	| ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------- | ---------------------- |
38	| Follows existing codebase patterns | Uses established conventions | Invents new patterns   |
39	| Service placement is correct       | Correct directory/layer      | Wrong location         |
40	| Dependencies flow correctly        | Proper DI, no circular deps  | Incorrect dependencies |
41	| Naming follows conventions         | Matches existing style       | Inconsistent naming    |
42	
43	**Reference Docs**:
44	
45	- `CLAUDE.md` (Architecture Overview section)
46	- `docs/SERVICE_INVENTORY.md`
47	- `./guides/testing-patterns.md`
48	
49	**Questions to Ask**:
50	
51	- Does this fit with how similar features are implemented?
52	- Are new abstractions justified or premature?
53	- Does the service belong in the proposed location?
54	
55	---
56	
57	### 3. Technical Correctness (REQUIRED)
58	
59	| Criterion                       | Pass                    | Fail                         |
60	| ------------------------------- | ----------------------- | ---------------------------- |
61	| TypeScript types are sound      | Proper typing, no `any` | Loose types, `any` usage     |
62	| Error handling is complete      | All error paths covered | Missing error handling       |
63	| Database operations are correct | Proper queries, indexes | N+1 queries, missing indexes |
64	| API contracts are well-defined  | Clear request/response  | Ambiguous contracts          |
65	
66	**Questions to Ask**:
67	
68	- Will this work correctly in all scenarios?
69	- Are failure modes handled appropriately?
70	- Is the data model correct and normalized?
71	
72	---
73	
74	### 4. Testing Strategy (REQUIRED)
75	
76	| Criterion                    | Pass                        | Fail                          |
77	| ---------------------------- | --------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
78	| Test approach is defined     | Clear testing plan          | No testing mentioned          |
79	| TDD workflow specified       | Tests before implementation | Implementation first          |
80	| Mock strategy is appropriate | Uses mock factories         | Manual mocks or real services |
81	| Coverage targets identified  | Specific areas to test      | Vague "will add tests"        |
82	
83	**Reference Docs**:
84	
85	- `./guides/testing-patterns.md`
86	- `src/test-utils/factories/`
87	
88	**Questions to Ask**:
89	
90	- Are the right things being tested?
91	- Is the mocking strategy sustainable?
92	- Will tests catch regressions?
93	
94	---
95	
96	### 5. Security Considerations (REQUIRED)
97	
98	| Criterion                  | Pass                            | Fail                          |
99	| -------------------------- | ------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
100	| Auth/authz is addressed    | Proper access controls          | Missing or wrong permissions  |
101	| Input validation present   | Zod schemas, sanitization       | Direct user input usage       |
102	| No sensitive data exposure | Proper data handling            | Logging secrets, exposing PII |
103	| OWASP top 10 considered    | Known vulnerabilities addressed | Security not mentioned        |
104	
105	**Questions to Ask**:
106	
107	- Can this be exploited?
108	- Is user input properly validated?
109	- Are secrets handled correctly?
110	
111	---
112	
113	### 6. Operational Readiness (RECOMMENDED)
114	
115	| Criterion                  | Pass                  | Needs Work          |
116	| -------------------------- | --------------------- | ------------------- |
117	| Logging is appropriate     | Key operations logged | Over/under logging  |
118	| Metrics defined            | Observable operations | No observability    |
119	| Error messages are helpful | Actionable messages   | Generic errors      |
120	| Migration path clear       | Safe rollout plan     | Big bang deployment |
121	
122	**Questions to Ask**:
123	
124	- Can we debug this in production?
125	- How do we know if it's working?
126	- What's the rollback strategy?
127	
128	---
129	
130	### 7. Documentation (RECOMMENDED)
131	
132	| Criterion                 | Pass                  | Needs Work          |
133	| ------------------------- | --------------------- | ------------------- |
134	| API documented            | Clear endpoint docs   | Undocumented APIs   |
135	| Complex logic explained   | Comments where needed | Magic code          |
136	| README updates identified | Doc changes listed    | Docs not considered |
137	
138	---
139	
140	## Scoring
141	
142	### REQUIRED Categories (1-4, 5)
143	
144	All REQUIRED criteria must PASS. Any FAIL = plan needs revision.
145	
146	### RECOMMENDED Categories (6-7)
147	
148	Should be addressed but won't block approval. Note for improvement.
149	
150	---
151	
152	## Review Output Format
153	
154	```markdown
155	## Plan Review: <task-id>
156	
157	### Verdict: APPROVED | NEEDS REVISION
158	
159	### Requirements Alignment
160	
161	- [x] All requirements addressed
162	- [x] Success criteria measurable
163	- [ ] Scope appropriate - **ISSUE**: Over-engineered caching layer not needed
164	- [x] Edge cases identified
165	
166	### Architecture Fit
167	
168	- [x] Follows existing patterns
169	- [x] Service placement correct
170	- [x] Dependencies correct
171	- [x] Naming conventions followed
172	
173	### Technical Correctness
174	
175	- [x] TypeScript types sound
176	- [ ] Error handling complete - **ISSUE**: Missing handling for rate limits
177	- [x] Database operations correct
178	- [x] API contracts defined
179	
180	### Testing Strategy
181	
182	- [x] Test approach defined
183	- [x] TDD workflow specified
184	- [x] Mock strategy appropriate
185	- [x] Coverage targets identified
186	
187	### Security Considerations
188	
189	- [x] Auth/authz addressed
190	- [x] Input validation present
191	- [x] No sensitive data exposure
192	- [x] OWASP considered
193	
194	### Operational Readiness
195	
196	- [x] Logging appropriate
197	- [ ] Metrics defined - **NOTE**: Consider adding timing metrics
198	- [x] Error messages helpful
199	- [x] Migration path clear
200	
201	### Documentation
202	
203	- [x] API documented
204	- [x] Complex logic explained
205	- [x] README updates identified
206	
207	---
208	
209	### Required Changes (Must Fix)
210	
211	1. Remove unnecessary caching layer - simplify to direct DB calls
212	2. Add error handling for API rate limits (429 responses)
213	
214	### Recommendations (Nice to Have)
215	
216	1. Add timing metrics for API calls
217	2. Consider adding retry logic with exponential backoff
218	
219	### Questions for Author
220	
221	1. Is the 1-hour cache TTL based on data requirements?
222	```
223	
224	---
225	
226	## Iteration Protocol
227	
228	### First Review
229	
230	- Provide detailed feedback using format above
231	- Mark task as needs revision if any REQUIRED criteria fail
232	
233	### Subsequent Reviews
234	
235	- Focus only on previously identified issues
236	- Verify fixes are correct
237	- Check for regression in other areas
238	
239	### Approval
240	
241	- All REQUIRED criteria pass
242	- Update task status accordingly
243	
244	---
245	
246	## Common Rejection Reasons
247	
248	1. **Over-engineering**: Building abstractions for single use cases
249	2. **Missing error handling**: Happy path only
250	3. **Wrong service location**: Not following established service patterns
251	4. **No testing strategy**: "Will add tests later"
252	5. **Security gaps**: Missing auth checks or input validation
253	6. **Breaking existing patterns**: Inventing new conventions
254	7. **Scope creep**: Addressing issues not in the GitHub Issue
255

1	# Code Review Rubric
2	
3	**Used By**: Code Review Agent
4	**Purpose**: Evaluate code changes before PR creation
5	**Version**: 1.0
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Overview
10	
11	This rubric ensures code changes meet quality standards before being submitted as a PR. The internal code review catches issues early, before external reviewers (humans, CodeRabbit) see the code.
12	
13	---
14	
15	## Severity Levels
16	
17	| Level        | Description                                               | Action                                  |
18	| ------------ | --------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
19	| **CRITICAL** | Security vulnerability, data loss risk, breaks production | MUST fix before PR                      |
20	| **HIGH**     | Bug, incorrect behavior, significant performance issue    | MUST fix before PR                      |
21	| **MEDIUM**   | Code smell, maintainability issue, minor bug              | SHOULD fix, discuss if time-constrained |
22	| **LOW**      | Style, nitpick, suggestion                                | OPTIONAL, note for future               |
23	
24	---
25	
26	## Reference Standards
27	
28	| Document                                | Purpose                                  |
29	| --------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
30	| `./guides/coding-standards.md`    | Coding standards (source of truth)       |
31	| `CLAUDE.md`                             | Architecture, coding standards, key locs |
32	| `./guides/testing-patterns.md`    | Testing philosophy, mock factories, TDD  |
33	| `test-quality-anti-patterns.md (project-specific)` | Common testing mistakes to avoid         |
34	| TypeScript strict mode                  | Follow conventions from tsconfig.json and ESLint configuration |
35	| `docs/SERVICE_INVENTORY.md`             | Service catalog & factory inventory      |
36	| `templates/task-completion-checklist.md`  | Pre-completion validation steps          |
37	
38	---
39	
40	## Evaluation Categories
41	
42	### 1. Correctness (CRITICAL/HIGH)
43	
44	| Criterion                          | Severity | Check                         |
45	| ---------------------------------- | -------- | ----------------------------- |
46	| Code does what it's supposed to do | CRITICAL | Manual trace through logic    |
47	| Edge cases handled                 | HIGH     | Null, empty, boundary values  |
48	| Error paths correct                | HIGH     | Exceptions caught and handled |
49	| No logic errors                    | CRITICAL | Conditions, loops, recursion  |
50	| State mutations are intentional    | HIGH     | No accidental side effects    |
51	
52	**Questions**:
53	
54	- Does this actually solve the problem?
55	- What happens with unexpected input?
56	- Are there race conditions?
57	
58	---
59	
60	### 2. Security (CRITICAL)
61	
62	| Criterion                 | Severity | Check                       |
63	| ------------------------- | -------- | --------------------------- |
64	| No SQL/NoSQL injection    | CRITICAL | Parameterized queries only  |
65	| No XSS vulnerabilities    | CRITICAL | Output encoding, CSP        |
66	| Auth/authz enforced       | CRITICAL | userId checks, role checks  |
67	| Input validation present  | CRITICAL | Zod schemas on all inputs   |
68	| No secrets in code        | CRITICAL | No hardcoded keys/passwords |
69	| No sensitive data in logs | HIGH     | PII, tokens, passwords      |
70	
71	**OWASP Top 10 Checklist**:
72	
73	- [ ] A01: Broken Access Control
74	- [ ] A02: Cryptographic Failures
75	- [ ] A03: Injection
76	- [ ] A07: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
77	
78	---
79	
80	### 3. TypeScript Quality (HIGH/MEDIUM)
81	
82	| Criterion                                  | Severity | Check                                 |
83	| ------------------------------------------ | -------- | ------------------------------------- |
84	| No `any` types                             | HIGH     | Explicit types everywhere             |
85	| No `as` type assertions (except safe ones) | MEDIUM   | Prefer type guards                    |
86	| Proper null handling                       | HIGH     | Optional chaining, nullish coalescing |
87	| Types match runtime behavior               | HIGH     | No lying types                        |
88	| Generics used appropriately                | MEDIUM   | Not over-engineered                   |
89	
90	**Type Cast Checklist**:
91	
92	- [ ] No `as any` (NEVER allowed)
93	- [ ] No `as unknown as` in test DI wiring (use `as never` instead)
94	- [ ] No `as unknown as` in production code without explanatory comment
95	- [ ] All Prisma model mocks use shared factories from `src/test-utils/factories/`
96	- [ ] No local factory wrapper functions that add no logic over shared factories
97	
98	**Patterns to Flag**:
99	
100	```typescript
101	// BAD
102	const data = response as any;
103	const user = getUser() as User; // Unsafe assertion
104	const svc = new MyService(mock as unknown as ConstructorParameters<typeof MyService>[0]); // Verbose
105	
106	// GOOD
107	const data: ApiResponse = await fetchData();
108	const user = isUser(result) ? result : null; // Type guard
109	const svc = new MyService(deps as never); // Test DI wiring
110	```
111	
112	---
113	
114	### 4. Testing (HIGH)
115	
116	| Criterion                          | Severity | Check                                            |
117	| ---------------------------------- | -------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
118	| Tests exist for new code           | HIGH     | 100% coverage required                           |
119	| Tests verify results, not presence | HIGH     | No `toBeDefined()` or `toHaveBeenCalled()` alone |
120	| Mock factories used                | HIGH     | `@/test-utils/factories`, never inline mocks     |
121	| No real API calls                  | HIGH     | All external calls mocked via constructor DI     |
122	| Edge cases tested                  | HIGH     | Every branch, error handler, fallback            |
123	| TDD followed                       | MEDIUM   | Tests written before implementation              |
124	
125	**Test Quality Checks** (see `./guides/testing-patterns.md`):
126	
127	- [ ] Every assertion tests a **result**, not just presence
128	- [ ] Mock factories from `src/test-utils/factories/` used (never inline objects)
129	- [ ] `beforeEach` recreates fresh mocks (no shared mutable state)
130	- [ ] No `.skip()`, `.todo()`, or commented-out assertions
131	- [ ] No `as any` in tests
132	- [ ] Test DI wiring uses `as never` (not `as unknown as ConstructorParameters<...>`)
133	- [ ] No `as unknown as` without comment explaining why
134	- [ ] No tests removed to fix type errors
135	
136	---
137	
138	### 5. Performance (MEDIUM/HIGH)
139	
140	| Criterion                 | Severity | Check                            |
141	| ------------------------- | -------- | -------------------------------- |
142	| No N+1 queries            | HIGH     | Batch/include relations          |
143	| No unnecessary re-renders | MEDIUM   | React memo, useMemo, useCallback |
144	| Efficient algorithms      | MEDIUM   | Appropriate data structures      |
145	| No memory leaks           | HIGH     | Cleanup effects, listeners       |
146	| Bundle size considered    | MEDIUM   | No huge imports                  |
147	
148	**Database Query Checks**:
149	
150	```typescript
151	// BAD - N+1
152	const users = await prisma.user.findMany();
153	for (const user of users) {
154	  const posts = await prisma.post.findMany({ where: { userId: user.id } });
155	}
156	
157	// GOOD - Single query
158	const users = await prisma.user.findMany({
159	  include: { posts: true },
160	});
161	```
162	
163	---
164	
165	### 6. Maintainability (MEDIUM)
166	
167	| Criterion                  | Severity | Check                        |
168	| -------------------------- | -------- | ---------------------------- |
169	| Functions are focused      | MEDIUM   | Single responsibility        |
170	| Names are descriptive      | MEDIUM   | Clear intent                 |
171	| Complex logic is commented | MEDIUM   | Non-obvious code explained   |
172	| No dead code               | LOW      | Remove unused code           |
173	| Consistent style           | LOW      | Matches codebase conventions |
174	| DRY principle followed     | MEDIUM   | No copy-paste code           |
175	
176	**Complexity Thresholds**:
177	
178	- Function > 30 lines: Consider splitting
179	- Nesting > 3 levels: Refactor
180	- Parameters > 5: Use options object
181	
182	---
183	
184	### 7. API Design (MEDIUM)
185	
186	| Criterion                  | Severity | Check                             |
187	| -------------------------- | -------- | --------------------------------- |
188	| RESTful conventions        | MEDIUM   | Proper HTTP methods, status codes |
189	| Request/response schemas   | HIGH     | Zod validation                    |
190	| Error responses consistent | MEDIUM   | Standard error format             |
191	| Versioning considered      | LOW      | Breaking changes managed          |
192	
193	---
194	
195	## Review Output Format
196	
197	````markdown
198	## Code Review: <files or PR description>
199	
200	### Verdict: APPROVED | CHANGES REQUIRED
201	
202	### Summary
203	
204	<Brief description of what the code does and overall quality assessment>
205	
206	---
207	
208	### Critical Issues (Must Fix)
209	
210	#### 1. SQL Injection Vulnerability
211	
212	**File**: `src/lib/services/user.service.ts:45`
213	**Severity**: CRITICAL
214	**Issue**: Raw user input in query string
215	**Fix**: Use parameterized query
216	
217	```typescript
218	// Current (vulnerable)
219	const query = `SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '${userName}'`;
220	
221	// Fixed
222	const users = await prisma.user.findMany({ where: { name: userName } });
223	```
224	````
225	
226	---
227	
228	### High Priority Issues (Must Fix)
229	
230	#### 2. Missing Error Handling
231	
232	**File**: `src/api/routes/webhooks/clerk.ts:23`
233	**Severity**: HIGH
234	**Issue**: API call can throw but isn't caught
235	**Fix**: Add try-catch with proper error response
236	
237	---
238	
239	### Medium Priority Issues (Should Fix)
240	
241	#### 3. N+1 Query Pattern
242	
243	**File**: `src/lib/services/email.service.ts:78`
244	**Severity**: MEDIUM
245	**Issue**: Fetching threads in a loop
246	**Suggestion**: Use `include` or batch query
247	
248	---
249	
250	### Low Priority / Suggestions
251	
252	1. Consider renaming `data` to `contactData` for clarity (line 34)
253	2. This function could be simplified using optional chaining
254	
255	---
256	
257	### Checklist
258	
259	#### Correctness
260	
261	- [x] Code does what it should
262	- [x] Edge cases handled
263	- [ ] Error paths correct - **Missing try-catch**
264	
265	#### Security
266	
267	- [ ] No injection vulnerabilities - **SQL injection found**
268	- [x] Auth enforced
269	- [x] Input validated
270	
271	#### TypeScript
272	
273	- [x] No `any` types
274	- [x] Proper null handling
275	
276	#### Testing
277	
278	- [x] Tests exist
279	- [x] Tests are meaningful
280	- [x] Mocks appropriate
281	
282	#### Performance
283	
284	- [ ] No N+1 queries - **Found in email service**
285	
286	#### Maintainability
287	
288	- [x] Functions focused
289	- [x] Names descriptive
290	
291	````
292	
293	---
294	
295	## Approval Criteria
296	
297	**APPROVED** when:
298	- Zero CRITICAL issues
299	- Zero HIGH issues (or explicitly accepted with justification)
300	- MEDIUM issues documented (fix now or create follow-up task)
301	
302	**CHANGES REQUIRED** when:
303	- Any CRITICAL issues exist
304	- Any HIGH issues without justification
305	
306	---
307	
308	## Iteration Protocol
309	
310	### First Review
311	- Full review against all categories
312	- Prioritized list of issues
313	
314	### Subsequent Reviews
315	- Focus on previously identified issues
316	- Verify fixes don't introduce new issues
317	- Quick scan for regression
318	
319	### Maximum Iterations: 3
320	After 3 rounds, escalate to human if issues persist.
321	
322	---
323	
324	## Common Patterns to Flag
325	
326	### Anti-Patterns
327	
328	```typescript
329	// 1. any usage
330	const data: any = await fetch(); // MEDIUM
331	
332	// 2. Unsafe type assertion
333	const user = data as User; // MEDIUM - use type guard
334	
335	// 3. Missing await
336	async function save() {
337	  prisma.user.create({}); // HIGH - missing await
338	}
339	
340	// 4. Swallowing errors
341	try { ... } catch (e) { } // HIGH - silent failure
342	
343	// 5. Direct console.log in production code
344	console.log(data); // LOW - use logger
345	
346	// 6. Hardcoded values
347	const API_URL = "https://api.example.com"; // MEDIUM - use env
348	````
349	
350	### Good Patterns to Encourage
351	
352	```typescript
353	// 1. Proper error handling
354	try {
355	  await service.process();
356	} catch (error) {
357	  logger.error({ error }, "Processing failed");
358	  throw new ServiceError("Processing failed", { cause: error });
359	}
360	
361	// 2. Type guards
362	function isUser(obj: unknown): obj is User {
363	  return typeof obj === "object" && obj !== null && "id" in obj;
364	}
365	
366	// 3. Zod validation
367	const schema = z.object({ email: z.string().email() });
368	const validated = schema.parse(input);
369	```
370	
371	---
372	
373	## Knowledge Integration
374	
375	Before reviewing, check knowledge base for:
376	
377	- Known issues with the files being changed
378	- Patterns that have caused problems before
379	- Team-specific conventions
380

1	# Agent Coordination Guide
2	
3	This guide defines two coordination modes for the BEADS multi-agent swarm. Both modes produce identical work products; only the dispatch mechanism changes.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Mode Detection
8	
9	At the start of any orchestration workflow, check your available tools:
10	
11	- **If `TeamCreate` and `SendMessage` are available** --> Use Team Mode
12	- **Otherwise** --> Use Task Mode (follow existing workflow exactly as documented)
13	
14	This is a single check at workflow start. There is no runtime switching between modes. Task Mode incurs zero overhead -- it is the existing workflow unchanged.
15	
16	```text
17	Workflow starts
18	      |
19	      v
20	  TeamCreate and SendMessage available?
21	      |
22	   +--+--+
23	   | YES |  --> Team Mode (persistent teammates, direct messaging)
24	   +--+--+
25	      |
26	   +--+--+
27	   | NO  |  --> Task Mode (existing workflow, zero changes)
28	   +-----+
29	
30	In EITHER mode:
31	  - Adversarial reviewers = ALWAYS fresh Task()
32	  - BEADS = source of truth
33	  - Quality gates = blocking
34	  - Human checkpoints = mandatory
35	```
36	
37	**Key principle**: The work itself (what agents do) is identical in both modes. Only HOW agents are spawned and communicate changes. Every skill file documents both modes; Team Mode is preferred when available, Task Mode is the zero-cost fallback.
38	
39	---
40	
41	## 2. Team Naming Convention
42	
43	Teams are scoped to the orchestration level that creates them:
44	
45	| Level                          | Team Name Pattern          | Example                   |
46	| ------------------------------ | -------------------------- | ------------------------- |
47	| Initiative (Swarm Coordinator) | `swarm-{initiative-id}`    | `swarm-auth-overhaul`     |
48	| Issue (Issue Orchestrator)     | `issue-{issue-number}`     | `issue-123`               |
49	| Design Review                  | `review-{design-doc-name}` | `review-user-auth-design` |
50	
51	Teams are ephemeral -- created at workflow start, deleted after completion. BEADS remains the durable record.
52	
53	---
54	
55	## 3. Task Mode (Default)
56	
57	Task Mode is the baseline coordination mechanism. It uses fire-and-forget `Task()` subagents. This mode is always available and requires no special tooling.
58	
59	### How It Works
60	
61	1. **Orchestrator spawns subagent** via `Task()` with full context in the prompt
62	2. **Subagent executes** independently -- no cross-agent communication
63	3. **Subagent returns result** to the orchestrator upon completion
64	4. **Orchestrator proceeds** to the next step in the workflow
65	
66	### Characteristics
67	
68	- Each subagent gets the complete context it needs in its spawn prompt
69	- No persistent state between subagent invocations (cold start every time)
70	- Sequential handoffs go through the orchestrator (researcher result fed into architect prompt)
71	- Parallel work uses parallel `Task()` calls (e.g., design review panel)
72	- This is the current behavior documented in all agent and skill files
73	
74	### When Task Mode Is Sufficient
75	
76	Task Mode works well for most workflows. The overhead of cold starts only becomes significant when:
77	- The same agent needs to work on multiple sequential units (e.g., a coder across work units)
78	- Design reviews require multiple iteration cycles (5 reviewers x 3 iterations = 15 cold starts)
79	- A PR shepherd needs to persist through a long CI/review lifecycle
80	
81	---
82	
83	## 4. Team Mode (Enhanced)
84	
85	Team Mode uses persistent teammates with context retention and direct inter-agent messaging. It is preferred when `TeamCreate` and `SendMessage` tools are available.
86	
87	### How It Works
88	
89	1. **Orchestrator creates a team** via `TeamCreate("{team-name}")`
90	2. **Specialists join as named teammates** (e.g., `researcher`, `architect`, `coder`, `shepherd`)
91	3. **Teammates communicate directly** via `SendMessage` -- no orchestrator bottleneck for handoffs
92	4. **Teammates retain context** across multiple work items within the same team session
93	5. **Orchestrator coordinates** phase gates, conflict resolution, and shutdown
94	6. **Graceful teardown** via `shutdown_request` to all teammates, then `TeamDelete`
95	
96	### Key Benefits Over Task Mode
97	
98	| Benefit                     | Description                                                                 |
99	| --------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
100	| **No cold starts**          | Coder retains context from WU-001 when starting WU-002                     |
101	| **Direct handoffs**         | Researcher sends findings directly to architect via `SendMessage`           |
102	| **Persistent PR shepherd**  | Stays alive through entire PR lifecycle without `run_in_background`         |
103	| **Efficient review cycles** | Design reviewers retain context across iterations (saves N cold starts)     |
104	| **Async coordination**      | Teammates send status updates without blocking the orchestrator             |
105	
106	### Swarm Coordinator as Team Lead
107	
108	The Swarm Coordinator creates an initiative-level team and spawns Issue Orchestrators as persistent teammates:
109	
110	1. `TeamCreate("swarm-{initiative-id}")`
111	2. Spawn orchestrators as named teammates (`orch-123`, `orch-456`)
112	3. Broadcast file/schema conflicts via `SendMessage(type: "broadcast")`
113	4. Receive async updates ("PR merged", "blocked") from orchestrators
114	5. Enforce phase gates ("Phase 1 complete, Phase 2 may begin")
115	6. Graceful shutdown: `shutdown_request` to each orchestrator, then `TeamDelete`
116	
117	### Issue Orchestrator as Sub-Team Lead
118	
119	The Issue Orchestrator creates an issue-level team and spawns specialist agents:
120	
121	1. `TeamCreate("issue-{number}")`
122	2. Spawn specialists: `researcher`, `architect`, `coder`, `shepherd`
123	3. Direct handoff: researcher completes, sends findings to `architect` directly
124	4. Persistent coder: retains context across work units (no cold start between WU-001 and WU-002)
125	5. Persistent shepherd: stays alive through the PR lifecycle, sends async status updates
126	6. Graceful shutdown: `shutdown_request` to all teammates, then `TeamDelete`
127	
128	### Design Review as Review Team
129	
130	The Design Review Gate creates a review-level team with all reviewers as persistent teammates:
131	
132	1. `TeamCreate("review-{design-doc-name}")`
133	2. Spawn reviewers as named teammates (e.g., `pm`, `architect`, `designer`, `security`, `cto`)
134	3. Collect verdicts via `SendMessage` to the orchestrator
135	4. On revision: message "revised design, please re-review" -- reviewers retain context from previous round
136	5. After approval: `shutdown_request` to all reviewers, then `TeamDelete`
137	
138	This is where Team Mode provides the biggest efficiency gain: design reviews often require 2-3 iterations, and persistent reviewers avoid re-reading the entire design document each cycle.
139	
140	---
141	
142	## 5. BEADS + Team TaskList Bridging
143	
144	BEADS and Team TaskLists serve different purposes and must be kept in sync during Team Mode.
145	
146	### Separation of Concerns
147	
148	| Concern         | BEADS                                  | Team TaskList                            |
149	| --------------- | -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
150	| **Purpose**     | Canonical record of WHAT (durable)     | Dispatch of WHO/WHEN (ephemeral)         |
151	| **Persistence** | Survives across sessions, syncs to git | Deleted with `TeamDelete` after workflow |
152	| **Visibility**  | Visible to all agents, all sessions    | Visible only to team members             |
153	| **Updates**     | `bd create`, `bd close`, `bd update`   | `TaskCreate`, `TaskUpdate`               |
154	
155	### Bridge Protocol (Team Mode Only)
156	
157	1. **Create BEADS task** --> `bd create "WU-001: <title>" --type task`
158	2. **Create matching Team task** --> `TaskCreate({ subject: "WU-001: <title>", metadata: { beads_id: "<beads-task-id>" } })`
159	3. **Teammate completes Team task** --> `TaskUpdate({ taskId: "<team-task-id>", status: "completed" })` + `SendMessage` to orchestrator
160	4. **Orchestrator closes BEADS task** --> `bd close <beads-task-id> --reason "4-phase loop complete. PASS."`
161	
162	### Rule: Orchestrator Owns BEADS Updates
163	
164	The orchestrator is the ONLY agent that updates BEADS. Teammates report completion via `SendMessage` or `TaskUpdate` on the Team TaskList; the orchestrator then runs `bd close` on the corresponding BEADS task.
165	
166	**Why**: This prevents race conditions and ensures a single source of truth. BEADS tracks the canonical state; Team TaskList is just a dispatch mechanism.
167	
168	### Task Mode
169	
170	Bridging is not needed in Task Mode. Subagents update BEADS directly as they do today -- the `Task()` return value carries the completion signal.
171	
172	---
173	
174	## 6. Adversarial Reviewer Isolation Rule
175	
176	**Adversarial reviewers MUST be fresh `Task()` instances on EVERY review pass -- even in Team Mode.**
177	
178	This is the single most important invariant in the entire coordination system. Violating it destroys the adversarial review's value.
179	
180	### The Rule
181	
182	- ALWAYS a fresh `Task()` instance
183	- NEVER a teammate
184	- NEVER resumed from a previous agent
185	- NEVER given previous review findings or prior context
186	- A new reviewer sees ONLY: spec, DoD items, and git diff
187	
188	### Why
189	
190	This prevents **anchoring bias**, where a reviewer unconsciously checks for previously-found issues rather than reviewing independently. After a FAIL --> fix --> re-validate cycle, the next reviewer MUST be a completely new `Task()` instance with zero memory of prior reviews.
191	
192	### Applies In Both Modes
193	
194	```text
195	Task Mode:  Fresh Task() on every review pass     (natural behavior)
196	Team Mode:  Fresh Task() on every review pass     (explicit override of team pattern)
197	```
198	
199	**There are no exceptions to this rule.**
200	
201	---
202	
203	## 7. Preserved Invariants
204	
205	These rules MUST NOT change in either coordination mode. They are mode-agnostic and non-negotiable.
206	
207	### Critical: Adversarial Reviewer Isolation
208	
209	See Section 6. ALWAYS a fresh `Task()` instance on every single review pass. Never a teammate. Never resumed. Never given context about what previous reviewers found.
210	
211	### Mode-Agnostic Rules
212	
213	| Invariant                       | Description                                                                    |
214	| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
215	| **Orchestrator-run validation** | Validation is always run directly by the orchestrator, never delegated         |
216	| **BEADS lifecycle**             | Create --> in_progress --> close lifecycle is identical in both modes           |
217	| **4-phase execution loop**      | IMPLEMENT --> VALIDATE --> ADVERSARIAL REVIEW --> COMMIT is mode-agnostic      |
218	| **Knowledge priming**           | `bd prime` runs before all agent work in both modes                            |
219	| **Human checkpoints**           | Planned pauses require explicit human approval in both modes                   |
220	| **Quality gates**               | Coverage, lint, typecheck, tests -- all blocking state transitions             |
221	| **Pipeline pattern**            | Push + PR + shepherd immediately after each agent completes, don't batch       |
222	| **Phase gate rule**             | Next phase starts only after ALL previous phase PRs are squash-merged          |
223	| **Max retry + escalation**      | 3 retries per gate, then escalate to human with full failure history           |
224	| **File scope verification**     | `git diff --name-only` check after every implementation                        |
225	
226	### Invariant Rationale
227	
228	These invariants exist because they encode hard-won lessons about multi-agent coordination failure modes:
229	
230	- **Orchestrator-run validation** prevents subagents from self-certifying their own work
231	- **BEADS lifecycle** ensures every piece of work has a durable, auditable trail
232	- **4-phase loop** prevents the "implement and hope" anti-pattern
233	- **Knowledge priming** prevents agents from making decisions in ignorance of established patterns
234	- **Human checkpoints** keep humans in the loop at critical decision points
235	- **Quality gates as blocking** prevents "I'll fix it later" accumulation of tech debt
236	- **Max retry + escalation** prevents infinite loops when an agent cannot solve a problem
237	
238	---
239	
240	## Quick Reference: Mode Comparison
241	
242	```text
243	+---------------------------+----------------------------+----------------------------+
244	|                           |       TASK MODE            |       TEAM MODE            |
245	+---------------------------+----------------------------+----------------------------+
246	| Availability              | Always                     | When TeamCreate +          |
247	|                           |                            | SendMessage available      |
248	+---------------------------+----------------------------+----------------------------+
249	| Agent lifecycle           | Fire-and-forget            | Persistent teammates       |
250	+---------------------------+----------------------------+----------------------------+
251	| Context retention         | None (cold start each)     | Retained across work items |
252	+---------------------------+----------------------------+----------------------------+
253	| Communication             | Via orchestrator only      | Direct SendMessage         |
254	+---------------------------+----------------------------+----------------------------+
255	| Adversarial reviewer      | Fresh Task() (natural)     | Fresh Task() (enforced)    |
256	+---------------------------+----------------------------+----------------------------+
257	| BEADS updates             | Subagent direct            | Orchestrator only          |
258	+---------------------------+----------------------------+----------------------------+
259	| Team TaskList bridging    | Not needed                 | Required (see Section 5)   |
260	+---------------------------+----------------------------+----------------------------+
261	| Overhead                  | Zero (existing behavior)   | Team setup/teardown        |
262	+---------------------------+----------------------------+----------------------------+
263	| Best for                  | Simple issues, single WU   | Multi-WU, iterative review |
264	+---------------------------+----------------------------+----------------------------+
265	```
266

1	---
2	name: pr-shepherd
3	description: Monitor a PR through to merge — handle CI failures, review comments, and thread resolution automatically until all checks pass
4	---
5	
6	# pr-shepherd
7	
8	Use when a PR has been created and needs to be monitored through to merge - handles CI failures, review comments, and thread resolution automatically until all checks pass and all threads are resolved.
9	
10	**IMPORTANT**: This skill is designed for the **agent working in a worktree**, NOT the orchestrator. The agent handles its own PR monitoring so the orchestrator remains free for other work.
11	
12	## Coordination Mode Note
13	
14	This skill supports both coordination modes:
15	
16	- **Task Mode** (default): Runs as a single long-running `Task()` with `run_in_background: true`. Orchestrator checks via `TaskOutput(block: false)`.
17	- **Team Mode**: Runs as a persistent `shepherd` teammate in the `issue-{number}` team. Sends async status updates via `SendMessage` (CI failure, review comments, all-green, PR merged). Orchestrator can respond with instructions (e.g., "defer that comment, create an issue instead"). Responds to `shutdown_request` for graceful exit.
18	
19	All monitoring, fixing, and review handling logic is identical in both modes. See `./guides/agent-coordination.md` for mode detection.
20	
21	---
22	
23	## When to Activate
24	
25	Activate this skill when ANY of these conditions are true:
26	
27	- Agent just created a PR with `gh pr create`
28	- User asks to "shepherd", "monitor", or "see through" a PR
29	- User invokes `/pr-shepherd <pr-number>`
30	- User asks to "watch this PR" or "handle this PR until it's merged"
31	- Orchestrator spawned you with instructions to shepherd a PR
32	- **Automatic**: `bin/create-pr-with-shepherd.sh` was used (outputs shepherd instructions)
33	
34	### Automatic Activation via Wrapper Script
35	
36	When `bin/create-pr-with-shepherd.sh` creates a PR, it outputs shepherd instructions:
37	
38	```text
39	==========================================
40	  PR Shepherd Active for PR #123
41	==========================================
42	
43	The pr-shepherd skill will:
44	  - Monitor CI/CD status
45	  - Auto-fix lint, type, and test issues
46	  - Handle review comments
47	  - Resolve threads after addressing feedback
48	  - Report when PR is ready to merge
49	
50	To manually invoke shepherd later:
51	  /pr-shepherd 123
52	```
53	
54	When you see this output, **immediately invoke the pr-shepherd skill** with the PR number shown.
55	
56	## Announce at Start
57	
58	"I'm using the pr-shepherd skill to monitor this PR through to merge. I'll watch CI/CD, handle review comments, and fix issues as they arise."
59	
60	## For Orchestrators: Spawning Agents with PR Shepherding
61	
62	When spawning an agent to work in a worktree, include PR shepherding in the task prompt:
63	
64	```text
65	Work in worktree at /path/to/worktree on branch feature/xyz.
66	
67	Task: [describe the implementation task]
68	
69	After creating the PR:
70	1. Use the pr-shepherd skill to monitor it through to merge
71	2. Handle CI failures and review comments autonomously
72	3. Only escalate to orchestrator for complex issues requiring user input
73	4. Report back when PR is ready to merge or if blocked
74	
75	Run in background so I can continue other work.
76	```
77	
78	**Key principle**: The agent owns its PR lifecycle. The orchestrator spawns and forgets, checking back via `AgentOutputTool` when needed.
79	
80	## State Machine
81	
82	The agent operates in one of these states:
83	
84	```text
85	MONITORING → FIXING → MONITORING → WAITING_FOR_USER → FIXING → MONITORING → DONE
86	```
87	
88	| State              | What Happens                                | Exit When                                      |
89	| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
90	| `MONITORING`       | Poll CI and reviews every 60s in background | CI fails, new comments, all done, or need help |
91	| `FIXING`           | Fix issues using TDD, run local validation  | Local validation passes OR need user guidance  |
92	| `HANDLING_REVIEWS` | Invoke `handling-pr-comments` skill         | Comments handled OR need user input            |
93	| `WAITING_FOR_USER` | Present options, wait for user decision     | User responds                                  |
94	| `DONE`             | All CI green + all threads resolved         | Exit successfully                              |
95	
96	## Phase 1: Initialize
97	
98	```bash
99	# Get PR info
100	PR_NUMBER=$(gh pr view --json number -q .number 2>/dev/null)
101	OWNER=$(gh repo view --json owner -q .owner.login)
102	REPO=$(gh repo view --json name -q .name)
103	
104	# If no PR on current branch, check if number was provided
105	if [ -z "$PR_NUMBER" ]; then
106	  echo "No PR found for current branch. Provide PR number."
107	  exit 1
108	fi
109	
110	echo "Shepherding PR #$PR_NUMBER"
111	```
112	
113	## Phase 2: Monitoring Loop (Background)
114	
115	Run GTG every 60 seconds as the **single source of truth** for PR readiness:
116	
117	### Primary Check: GTG (Good-To-Go)
118	
119	GTG consolidates CI status, comment classification, and thread resolution into one call. Use it instead of separate API queries.
120	
121	```bash
122	# Primary readiness check — structured JSON output
123	GTG_RESULT=$(gtg $PR_NUMBER --repo "$OWNER/$REPO" --format json \
124	  --exclude-checks "Merge Ready (gtg)" \
125	  --exclude-checks "CodeRabbit" \
126	  --exclude-checks "Cursor Bugbot" \
127	  --exclude-checks "claude" 2>&1)
128	
129	STATUS=$(echo "$GTG_RESULT" | jq -r '.status')
130	ACTION_ITEMS=$(echo "$GTG_RESULT" | jq -r '.action_items[]?' 2>/dev/null)
131	CI_STATE=$(echo "$GTG_RESULT" | jq -r '.ci_status.state')
132	```
133	
134	**GTG statuses:**
135	
136	| Status               | Meaning                            | Agent Action                              |
137	| -------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
138	| `READY`              | All CI green, all threads resolved | → DONE                                    |
139	| `ACTION_REQUIRED`    | Actionable comments need fixes     | → HANDLING_REVIEWS (use `action_items`)   |
140	| `UNRESOLVED_THREADS` | Review threads still open          | → HANDLING_REVIEWS                        |
141	| `CI_FAILING`         | One or more CI checks failing      | → FIXING                                  |
142	| `ERROR`              | Couldn't fetch PR data             | Retry after 60s, escalate after 3 retries |
143	
144	**GTG reports, agents act**: GTG does not resolve threads or fix code — it only tells you what's blocking. After addressing feedback, you must resolve threads yourself using the GraphQL mutation in `handle-pr-comments.md` (Section 3). GTG will report `READY` on the next poll once threads are resolved.
145	
146	### Evaluate State Transitions
147	
148	```text
149	if STATUS == "READY":
150	  → DONE
151	
152	if STATUS == "CI_FAILING":
153	  → Parse action_items for specific failures
154	  → if is_simple_failure(failure): FIXING
155	  → else: WAITING_FOR_USER
156	
157	if STATUS == "ACTION_REQUIRED" or STATUS == "UNRESOLVED_THREADS":
158	  → HANDLING_REVIEWS (action_items tells you exactly what to fix)
159	
160	if STATUS == "ERROR":
161	  → Retry, then escalate
162	```
163	
164	### Fallback: Manual Checks
165	
166	If GTG is unavailable (e.g., not installed in environment), fall back to manual queries:
167	
168	```bash
169	# CI status
170	FAILED_CHECKS=$(gh pr checks $PR_NUMBER --json name,conclusion --jq '[.[] | select(.conclusion == "FAILURE")] | length')
171	
172	# Unresolved threads
173	UNRESOLVED=$(gh api graphql -f query='
174	  query($owner: String!, $repo: String!, $pr: Int!) {
175	    repository(owner: $owner, name: $repo) {
176	      pullRequest(number: $pr) {
177	        reviewThreads(first: 100) {
178	          nodes { isResolved }
179	        }
180	      }
181	    }
182	  }
183	' -f owner="$OWNER" -f repo="$REPO" -F pr="$PR_NUMBER" \
184	  --jq '[.data.repository.pullRequest.reviewThreads.nodes[] | select(.isResolved == false)] | length')
185	```
186	
187	### Re-triggering GTG CI Check
188	
189	When threads are resolved but the `Merge Ready (gtg)` GitHub Actions check is stale:
190	
191	```bash
192	gh workflow run gtg.yml -f pr_number=$PR_NUMBER
193	```
194	
195	## Phase 3: Fixing Issues
196	
197	### Simple Issues (Auto-fix)
198	
199	These can be fixed without user approval:
200	
201	- Lint failures → run linter
202	- Prettier failures → run formatter
203	- Type errors → fix the types
204	- Test failures in code YOU wrote → fix using TDD
205	
206	### Complex Issues (Need Approval)
207	
208	These require user input BEFORE fixing:
209	
210	- Test failures in code you didn't write
211	- Infrastructure/config failures
212	- Ambiguous errors
213	- Anything you're uncertain about
214	
215	### FIXING State Rules
216	
217	1. **Use TDD** - Invoke `superpowers:test-driven-development` for code changes
218	2. **Kill stale test runners** - Run `pkill -f vitest 2>/dev/null || true` before test runs
219	3. **Stay until green** - Don't leave FIXING until `pnpm lint && pnpm typecheck && pnpm test --run && pnpm test:coverage` all pass
220	4. **Only push when verified** - Never push code that fails local validation or coverage thresholds
221	5. **Return to MONITORING after push** - Let CI run, continue monitoring
222	
223	```bash
224	# Kill stale vitest processes before running tests
225	pkill -f vitest 2>/dev/null || true
226	
227	# After fixing, always validate locally (including coverage)
228	pnpm lint && pnpm typecheck && pnpm test --run && pnpm test:coverage
229	
230	# Only push if all pass (including coverage thresholds)
231	git add -A && git commit -m "fix: <description>" && git push
232	```
233	
234	## Phase 4: Handling Reviews
235	
236	When new review comments are detected:
237	
238	1. Invoke the `handling-pr-comments` skill
239	2. That skill handles categorization, fixes, responses, and thread resolution
240	3. **CRITICAL: The handling-pr-comments skill includes an iteration loop**
241	4. **ALL threads must be resolved** before returning to MONITORING
242	5. If a thread cannot be resolved (needs clarification from reviewer), query the comment author asking for follow-up
243	6. Return to MONITORING only when:
244	   - All threads are resolved, AND
245	   - Post-push verification confirms NO new comments appeared
246	
247	### Iteration Enforcement
248	
249	**THE #1 FAILURE MODE**: Returning to MONITORING after one pass without checking for new comments.
250	
251	The `handling-pr-comments` skill's Phase 7 (Post-Push Iteration Check) MUST complete successfully before exiting HANDLING_REVIEWS state. The skill will iterate automatically:
252	
253	```text
254	HANDLING_REVIEWS:
255	  → handling-pr-comments skill (Phases 1-7)
256	  → IF Phase 7 finds new comments: skill re-runs Phases 1-7
257	  → IF Phase 7 confirms no new comments: exit to MONITORING
258	```
259	
260	**DO NOT** manually override or skip Phase 7. If you find yourself tempted to skip iteration, you're about to make the #1 mistake.
261	
262	### Out-of-Scope Comments
263	
264	Reviewers may leave comments on code outside the PR diff. The `handling-pr-comments` skill handles these, but key points:
265	
266	- **Treat out-of-scope as IN SCOPE by default** - respect reviewer feedback
267	- Use **ultrathink** to evaluate if fixes are quick (< 30 min, < 3 files)
268	- If simple: fix immediately and note it was outside original scope
269	- If complex: create a GitHub issue and link it in the thread response
270	- **Always respond and resolve** - never leave out-of-scope threads hanging
271	
272	## Phase 5: Waiting for User
273	
274	When user input is needed, ALWAYS:
275	
276	1. **Present the situation clearly**
277	2. **Offer 2-4 options with pros/cons**
278	3. **State your recommendation**
279	4. **Allow user to choose OR provide their own approach**
280	
281	### Template
282	
283	```text
284	[Describe what happened]
285	
286	**Options:**
287	
288	1. **[Option name]** (Recommended)
289	   - [What it involves]
290	   - Pros: [benefits]
291	   - Cons: [drawbacks]
292	
293	2. **[Option name]**
294	   - [What it involves]
295	   - Pros: [benefits]
296	   - Cons: [drawbacks]
297	
298	3. **[Option name]**
299	   - [What it involves]
300	   - Pros: [benefits]
301	   - Cons: [drawbacks]
302	
303	Which approach would you like? (Or describe a different approach)
304	```
305	
306	### After User Responds
307	
308	- If user picks a numbered option → proceed with that approach → FIXING
309	- If user describes alternative → proceed with their approach → FIXING
310	
311	## Phase 6: Soft Timeout (4 Hours)
312	
313	At 4 hours elapsed, pause and checkpoint:
314	
315	```text
316	**PR Shepherd Checkpoint** (4 hours elapsed)
317	
318	Current status:
319	- CI: [status]
320	- Threads: [X] resolved, [Y] unresolved
321	- Commits: [N] fix commits pushed
322	
323	**Options:**
324	
325	1. **Keep monitoring** (Recommended)
326	   - Continue for another 4 hours
327	   - Pros: PR may get reviewed soon
328	   - Cons: Ties up agent resources
329	
330	2. **Exit with handoff**
331	   - Save status report, exit cleanly
332	   - Pros: Frees resources
333	   - Cons: Must manually re-invoke later
334	
335	3. **Set shorter check-in**
336	   - Check back in 1 hour instead of 4
337	   - Pros: More frequent checkpoints
338	   - Cons: More interruptions
339	
340	What would you like to do? (Or describe a different approach)
341	```
342	
343	## Exit Conditions
344	
345	### Success (DONE)
346	
347	Exit successfully when ALL are true:
348	
349	- All CI checks passing
350	- **Every single** code review comment has been addressed (fix or explanation -- NONE ignored)
351	- All review threads resolved (zero unresolved)
352	- No pending questions
353	- PR squash-merged to main (not just "ready to merge" -- actually merged)
354	
355	Report:
356	
357	```text
358	**PR #[number] Ready to Merge**
359	
360	- CI: All checks passing
361	- Reviews: All threads resolved
362	- Commits: [N] total ([M] fix commits)
363	
364	The PR is ready for final approval and merge.
365	```
366	
367	### Post-Completion RAM Cleanup
368	
369	After the PR is merged and knowledge extraction tasks are created, invoke automatic RAM cleanup to free resources:
370	
371	```text
372	/auto-ram-cleanup
373	```
374	
375	**Why**: Development processes (test runners, build watchers, language servers) accumulate during PR work. Cleaning up after merge frees memory for the next task.
376	
377	**What stays running**:
378	
379	- Docker containers (needed for database/services)
380	- Essential IDE processes
381	
382	**What gets cleaned**:
383	
384	- Orphaned test runners (vitest, jest)
385	- Build watchers no longer needed
386	- Duplicate language server instances
387	- Other development tool cruft
388	
389	## Phase 7: Post-Merge Verification & Fallback Knowledge Extraction
390	
391	**Primary path**: Self-reflect should have already run pre-PR (see orchestrated-execution section 8.5), with knowledge base changes committed as part of the PR. This phase verifies that happened and handles the fallback case.
392	
393	**Fallback**: If self-reflect was NOT run pre-PR (e.g., PR was created outside the orchestrated workflow), create a blocking task for knowledge extraction after merge.
394	
395	### When PR is Merged
396	
397	After detecting that the PR has been merged (or after user merges it):
398	
399	```bash
400	# Check if PR was merged
401	MERGED=$(gh pr view $PR_NUMBER --json merged -q .merged)
402	
403	if [ "$MERGED" = "true" ]; then
404	  # Create a task for knowledge curation
405	  # Use your project's task tracking system
406	  echo "Create task: Curate learnings from PR #$PR_NUMBER"
407	
408	  # If there's an associated epic, add this task as a blocker
409	  # (The epic can't close until learnings are extracted)
410	fi
411	```
412	
413	**Note**: Self-reflect (`/self-reflect`) should have already run BEFORE the PR was created (see orchestrated-execution skill, section 8.5). If it was skipped, run it now as a fallback — but the preferred time is pre-PR while implementation context is freshest.
414	
415	### Report to User
416	
417	When creating the curation task:
418	
419	````text
420	**PR #[number] Merged Successfully**
421	
422	Created blocking task: [CURATION_TASK_ID]
423	- Title: "Curate learnings from PR #[number]"
424	- Status: pending
425	- Blocker for: [epic if applicable]
426	
427	To extract learnings, invoke:
428	```
429	/curate-pr-learnings [number]
430	```
431	
432	The command will:
433	1. Fetch PR comments (deterministic script)
434	2. AI analyzes and extracts learnings (your job)
435	3. Store validated learnings (deterministic script)
436	
437	Then close the task.
438	````
439	
440	### Why This Matters
441	
442	1. **Security**: Webhook-triggered code execution is an attack surface. CLI/agent invocation is safer.
443	2. **Blocking Task**: The epic can't close until learnings are extracted, ensuring knowledge capture.
444	3. **Agent Autonomy**: An agent can pick up the curation task and process it.
445	4. **Human Oversight**: Human can also run curation manually via the CLI script.
446	
447	### For Epic Completions: Extract Conversation Learnings
448	
449	When this PR completes an **epic** (closes the last blocking task), you MUST also extract learnings from conversation history. Feature work often contains the richest architectural discussions.
450	
451	**Detect epic completion:**
452	
453	> **Note**: This pattern assumes single-epic workflows. If multiple epics are in-progress,
454	> `.[0]` selects the first one, which may not be the epic related to this PR.
455	> For multi-epic projects, correlate the PR's task to its blocking epic manually.
456	
457	```bash
458	# Check if this PR closes an epic (assumes single in-progress epic)
459	# Use your project's task tracking system to check epic status
460	```
461	
462	**If epic is completing:**
463	
464	1. Create a task for conversation extraction
465	2. Report the task to user:
466	
467	   ```text
468	   **Epic Completion Detected**
469	
470	   This PR completes the epic. Created conversation extraction task.
471	
472	   Before closing the epic, extract learnings from conversations.
473	   ```
474	
475	**Why extract from conversations?**
476	
477	- **Strategic insights**: Architectural decisions, trade-offs discussed
478	- **Debugging discoveries**: Root causes found after hours of investigation
479	- **Non-obvious behaviors**: "It turns out that..." moments
480	- **Integration quirks**: API behaviors that caused issues
481	
482	These learnings are often NOT in code review comments - they're in the back-and-forth conversation.
483	
484	### Timeout with Handoff
485	
486	If user chooses to exit at checkpoint:
487	
488	```text
489	**PR #[number] Shepherd Handoff**
490	
491	Status at exit:
492	- CI: [status]
493	- Threads: [X] resolved, [Y] unresolved
494	- Last activity: [timestamp]
495	
496	To resume: `/pr-shepherd [number]`
497	```
498	
499	## Skills Invoked
500	
501	| Situation           | Skill                                 |
502	| ------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
503	| New review comments | `handling-pr-comments`                |
504	| Code changes needed | `superpowers:test-driven-development` |
505	| Complex debugging   | `superpowers:systematic-debugging`    |
506	
507	## Mandatory Pre-Completion Check
508	
509	**BLOCKING: You MUST run this script and show its output before declaring ANY PR ready:**
510	
511	```bash
512	bin/pr-comments-check.sh <PR_NUMBER>
513	```
514	
515	This script:
516	
517	- Returns exit code 0 if all comments addressed
518	- Returns exit code 1 if ANY unaddressed comments exist
519	- Shows status for each comment
520	
521	**If the script shows ANY unaddressed comments, you are NOT done.** Address each unaddressed comment:
522	
523	For EACH top-level comment (where `in_reply_to_id` is null) without a reply:
524	
525	1. If actionable → Fix it and reply confirming the fix
526	2. If out-of-scope → Reply explaining deferral (create issue if needed)
527	3. If disagree → Reply with reasoning
528	4. **NEVER ignore silently**
529	
530	A PR is NOT ready until every top-level comment has been addressed with a reply.
531	
532	## Verification Checklist
533	
534	Before exiting DONE state:
535	
536	- [ ] All CI checks are green
537	- [ ] All review threads are resolved
538	- [ ] No pending user questions
539	- [ ] Final status reported to user
540	
541	After PR is merged (Phase 7):
542	
543	- [ ] Created task for knowledge curation
544	- [ ] Added task as blocker to epic (if applicable)
545	- [ ] Reported curation task ID to user
546	- [ ] Verified `/self-reflect` ran pre-PR (if not, run it now as fallback)
547	
548	After all post-merge tasks complete:
549	
550	- [ ] Ran `/auto-ram-cleanup` to free development resources
551	- [ ] Confirmed Docker containers still running (if needed)
552	
553	## Common Mistakes
554	
555	### #1 MISTAKE: Returning to MONITORING without checking for NEW comments
556	
557	- After pushing a fix and responding to threads, you MUST run Phase 7
558	- Automated reviewers (CodeRabbit, Cursor) analyze every commit
559	- NEW comments often appear within 1-2 minutes of your push
560	- If you skip Phase 7, you'll miss the new comments and declare complete prematurely
561	
562	**Pushing without local validation**
563	
564	- NEVER push code that hasn't passed `pnpm lint && pnpm typecheck && pnpm test --run && pnpm test:coverage`
565	
566	**Auto-fixing complex issues**
567	
568	- If uncertain, ASK. Always go through WAITING_FOR_USER for complex issues.
569	
570	**Forgetting to invoke handling-pr-comments**
571	
572	- When new comments arrive, delegate to that skill. Don't handle comments inline.
573	
574	**Not presenting options to user**
575	
576	- Always give 2-4 options with pros/cons. Never just ask "what should I do?"
577	
578	**Leaving FIXING state early**
579	
580	- Stay in FIXING until local validation passes. Don't assume a fix worked.
581	
582	**Skipping the handling-pr-comments iteration loop**
583	
584	- The skill has Phases 1-7 with an explicit iteration loop
585	- Phase 7 checks for new comments after your fix push
586	- If Phase 7 finds new comments, the skill loops back to Phase 1
587	- DO NOT exit early - let the skill complete its full iteration
