2	name: setup
3	description: Interactive project setup — detects your project, configures metaswarm, writes project-local files
4	---
5	
6	# Setup
7	
8	Interactive, Claude-guided setup for metaswarm. Detects your stack, asks targeted questions, writes project-local files, and creates command shims. Replaces both `npx metaswarm init` and the old `/metaswarm-setup` command.
9	
10	<CRITICAL-REQUIREMENTS>
11	Setup MUST produce these 3 mandatory outputs. A shell script handles them automatically — you MUST run it.
12	
13	After Phase 2 (user questions), determine the correct coverage command from the detection results, then run this Bash command:
14	
15	```bash
16	bash "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/lib/setup-mandatory-files.sh" "$(pwd)" <threshold> "<coverage-command>"
17	```
18	
19	Where:
20	- `<threshold>` is the user's chosen percentage (e.g., `100`)
21	- `<coverage-command>` is the enforcement command for their test runner:
22	  - pytest → `"pytest --cov --cov-fail-under=<threshold>"`
23	  - vitest/pnpm → `"pnpm vitest run --coverage"`
24	  - jest/npm → `"npx jest --coverage"`
25	  - go → `"go test -coverprofile=coverage.out ./..."`
26	  - cargo → `"cargo tarpaulin --fail-under <threshold>"`
27	
28	The script handles:
29	1. **CLAUDE.md** — appends metaswarm section (or writes new), skips if already present
30	2. **`.coverage-thresholds.json`** — writes at project root with correct thresholds and command
31	3. **6 shims in `.claude/commands/`** — writes `start-task.md`, `prime.md`, `review-design.md`, `self-reflect.md`, `pr-shepherd.md`, `brainstorm.md`
32	
33	The script outputs JSON with what was created/skipped/errored. Check that `"status": "ok"`.
34	
35	**If the script is not available or fails**, fall back to writing these files manually with the Write tool. Do NOT skip them.
36	</CRITICAL-REQUIREMENTS>
37	
38	## Pre-Flight
39	
40	### Existing Profile Check
41	
42	Use Glob to check if `.metaswarm/project-profile.json` exists.
43	
44	- **If it exists**: Read it, present the current configuration summary, and ask the user via AskUserQuestion: "You already have a metaswarm project profile. Re-run setup (overwrites choices) or skip?" Options: "Re-run setup" / "Skip". If the user skips, stop with: "Setup skipped. Existing configuration unchanged."
45	- **If it does not exist**: Continue to Project Detection.
46	
47	---
48	
49	## Phase 1: Project Detection
50	
51	Scan the project directory silently using Glob and Read. Do NOT ask the user for any of this information. Detect everything, then present results.
52	
53	### 1.1 Language
54	
55	Check for marker files at the project root:
56	
57	| Marker File | Language |
58	|---|---|
59	| `package.json` | Node.js / JavaScript |
60	| `tsconfig.json` | TypeScript (refines Node.js to TypeScript) |
61	| `pyproject.toml` OR `setup.py` OR `requirements.txt` | Python |
62	| `go.mod` | Go |
63	| `Cargo.toml` | Rust |
64	| `pom.xml` OR `build.gradle` OR `build.gradle.kts` | Java |
65	| `Gemfile` | Ruby |
66	| `Makefile` (alone, no other markers) | Unknown (ask user) |
67	
68	If `tsconfig.json` exists alongside `package.json`, the language is "TypeScript".
69	
70	If multiple languages are detected, note all of them but use the primary one (most infrastructure) for command generation.
71	
72	**If no language detected**: Use AskUserQuestion to ask the user what language/stack they are using before proceeding.
73	
74	### 1.2 Framework
75	
76	**Node.js/TypeScript** — Read `package.json` and check `dependencies` + `devDependencies`:
77	
78	| Dependency | Framework |
79	|---|---|
80	| `next` | Next.js |
81	| `nuxt` OR `nuxt3` | Nuxt |
82	| `@angular/core` | Angular |
83	| `svelte` OR `@sveltejs/kit` | SvelteKit |
84	| `react` (without next/nuxt) | React |
85	| `vue` (without nuxt) | Vue |
86	| `express` | Express |
87	| `fastify` | Fastify |
88	| `hono` | Hono |
89	| `@nestjs/core` | NestJS |
90	
91	**Python** — Check `pyproject.toml` or `requirements.txt` for: `fastapi`, `django`, `flask`.
92	
93	**Go** — Read `go.mod` for: `github.com/gin-gonic/gin` (Gin), `github.com/labstack/echo` (Echo), `github.com/gofiber/fiber` (Fiber).
94	
95	If no framework detected, set to `null`.
96	
97	### 1.3 Package Manager (Node.js only)
98	
99	| Lock File | Package Manager |
100	|---|---|
101	| `pnpm-lock.yaml` | pnpm |
102	| `yarn.lock` | yarn |
103	| `bun.lockb` | bun |
104	| `package-lock.json` | npm |
105	
106	Default to `npm` if no lock file found. For non-Node.js, set to `null`.
107	
108	### 1.4 Test Runner
109	
110	**Node.js/TypeScript** (first match wins):
111	1. Glob for `vitest.config.*` or `vitest` in devDependencies -> `vitest`
112	2. Glob for `jest.config.*` or `jest` in devDependencies -> `jest`
113	3. `mocha` in devDependencies -> `mocha`
114	
115	**Python**: Check for `[tool.pytest]` in `pyproject.toml` or `pytest` in dependencies -> `pytest` (default for Python).
116	
117	**Go**: `go test` (built-in). **Rust**: `cargo test` (built-in). **Java/Maven**: `mvn test`. **Java/Gradle**: `gradle test`.
118	
119	### 1.5 Linter
120	
121	| Marker | Linter |
122	|---|---|
123	| `.eslintrc*` OR `eslint.config.*` OR `eslint` in devDependencies | eslint |
124	| `biome.json` OR `@biomejs/biome` in devDependencies | biome |
125	| `[tool.ruff]` in `pyproject.toml` OR `ruff.toml` | ruff |
126	| `.golangci.yml` OR `.golangci.yaml` | golangci-lint |
127	| `.clippy.toml` or clippy in Cargo.toml | clippy |
128	
129	### 1.6 Formatter
130	
131	| Marker | Formatter |
132	|---|---|
133	| `.prettierrc*` OR `prettier` in devDependencies | prettier |
134	| `biome.json` (also formats) | biome |
135	| `black` in Python deps | black |
136	| `[tool.ruff.format]` in `pyproject.toml` | ruff format |
137	| `rustfmt.toml` OR `.rustfmt.toml` | rustfmt |

The shims you just created in `.claude/commands/` make the short names work. The short names are easier to type and remember.

408	
409	- If any Bash command fails, report the error and offer to skip that step or retry.
410	- If a file cannot be read, note it and continue with other detection.
411	- If AskUserQuestion is dismissed, use defaults: 100% coverage, no external tools, no visual review.
412	- Never leave the project half-configured. The pre-flight check allows re-running setup to completion.
413	- All template paths are hardcoded in this skill. Never construct file paths from user-provided input.

The command shims make the short names work. Do NOT recommend commands that don't exist (e.g., `/metaswarm:start`, `/metaswarm:status`, `/metaswarm:architect-agent`).

3	description: Delegate implementation and review tasks to external AI CLI tools (Codex, Gemini) with cross-model adversarial review

6	  - "use external tools"

10	---
11	
12	# External Tools Skill
13	
14	## Purpose
15	
16	This skill delegates implementation and review tasks to external AI CLI tools (OpenAI Codex CLI, Google Gemini CLI), enabling cost savings through cheaper models and cross-model adversarial review that eliminates single-model blind spots. Three core principles govern every interaction: **one job per invocation** (an external tool implements OR reviews, never self-validates), **minimal permissions** (sandboxed execution scoped to the task's working directory with only the tool's own API key), and **the orchestrator verifies independently** (external tools report facts only, the orchestrator judges pass/fail).
17	
18	---
19	
20	## Prerequisites
21	
22	### Required Tools
23	
24	| Tool | Install | Auth |
25	|------|---------|------|
26	| OpenAI Codex CLI | `npm i -g @openai/codex` | API key or ChatGPT subscription |
27	| Google Gemini CLI | `npm i -g @google/gemini-cli` | Google login (free 1K req/day) or API key |
28	
29	Neither tool is strictly required. The skill adapts based on what is available (see Escalation Model below).
30	
31	### Configuration
32	
33	Per-project config at `.metaswarm/external-tools.yaml` (optional -- if absent, external tools are not used):
34	
35	```yaml
36	adapters:
37	  codex:
38	    enabled: true
39	    model: "gpt-5.3-codex"
40	    timeout_seconds: 300
41	    sandbox: docker          # docker | platform | none
42	  gemini:
43	    enabled: true
44	    model: "pro"
45	    timeout_seconds: 300
46	    sandbox: docker
47	
48	routing:
49	  default_implementer: "cheapest-available"
50	  escalation_order: ["codex", "gemini", "claude"]
51	
52	budget:
53	  per_task_usd: 2.00        # circuit breaker per task
54	  per_session_usd: 20.00    # circuit breaker per session
55	```
56	
57	### Fallback Behavior
58	
59	- **Both tools available**: Full cross-model delegation and review
60	- **One tool available**: Reduced chain with mutual review between the tool and Claude
61	- **No tools available**: Existing metaswarm behavior unchanged; skill is a no-op
62	
63	---
64	
65	## Quick Reference
66	
67	### Health Check
68	
69	```bash
70	# Check if Codex is installed, authenticated, and reachable
71	adapters/codex.sh health
72	
73	# Check Gemini
74	adapters/gemini.sh health
75	```
76	
77	Returns JSON with `status: "ready|degraded|unavailable"`.
78	
79	### Implement
80	
81	```bash
82	adapters/codex.sh implement \
83	  --worktree "/tmp/ext-codex-task-42" \
84	  --prompt-file "/private/tmp/xt-abc123/prompt.md" \
85	  --attempt 1 \
86	  --timeout 300
87	```
88	
89	### Review
90	
91	```bash
92	adapters/gemini.sh review \
93	  --worktree "/tmp/ext-codex-task-42" \
94	  --rubric-file "./rubrics/external-tool-review-rubric.md" \
95	  --spec-file "/private/tmp/xt-abc123/spec.md" \
96	  --timeout 300
97	```
98	
99	All commands return a uniform JSON envelope with facts only (exit code, files changed, cost, duration). The orchestrator interprets results -- adapters never self-judge.
100	
101	---
102	
103	## Workflow: Routing & Dispatch
104	
105	### Phase 0: Tool Discovery (Health Checks)
106	
107	Before dispatching any task, the orchestrator runs health checks to determine which tools are available:
108	
109	```bash
110	# Run health checks for all configured adapters
111	for adapter in adapters/*.sh; do
112	  result=$("$adapter" health)
113	  # Parse status: ready | degraded | unavailable
114	done
115	```
116	
117	Health is checked **per task dispatch**, not just at session start. Auth tokens can expire mid-session, network conditions change, and rate limits reset. A tool that was `ready` five minutes ago may now be `unavailable`.
118	
119	Build the available tools list:
120	
121	```python
122	available_tools = [t for t in adapters if t.health() == "ready"]
123	```
124	
125	### Phase 1: IMPLEMENT (External Tool or Claude)
126	
127	If an external tool is selected as implementer:
128	
129	1. **Create worktree** -- `git worktree add "$WORKTREE_PATH" -b "external/$TOOL/$TASK_ID"` for isolation
130	2. **Package context** -- Gather relevant files, spec, acceptance criteria into a prompt file in a secure temp dir (mode 700, trap cleanup). Token-budget the context to fit the model's window.
131	3. **Invoke** -- `safe_invoke()` with timeout wrapper; capture JSON output
132	4. **Scope check** -- Verify all file changes are within the declared file scope; revert any out-of-scope changes
133	5. **Capture output** -- Facts only: exit code, files changed, diff stats, cost, duration
134	
135	If Claude is selected (escalation or no external tools), use the existing `Task()` mechanism unchanged.
136	
137	### Phase 2: VALIDATE (Always Orchestrator -- Unchanged)
138	
139	The orchestrator independently runs all quality gates:
140	
141	- `npm test` / `npx vitest run`
142	- `npx tsc --noEmit`
143	- `npx eslint <changed-files>`
144	- Coverage enforcement via `.coverage-thresholds.json`
145	- File scope verification via `git diff --name-only`
146	
147	This phase is identical whether the implementer was an external tool or Claude. **Never trust the implementer's self-report.**
148	
149	### Phase 3: ADVERSARIAL REVIEW (Cross-Model)
150	
151	The key advantage of external tools: the writer is always reviewed by a **different model**.
152	
153	| Implementer | Reviewer 1 | Reviewer 2 |
154	|-------------|-----------|-----------|
155	| Codex | Gemini (via adapter) | Claude (via Task) |
156	| Gemini | Codex (via adapter) | Claude (via Task) |
157	| Claude | Codex (via adapter) | Gemini (via adapter) |
158	
159	Review is invoked via the adapter's `review` command. The orchestrator reads the reviewer's raw log and evaluates it independently -- the adapter never returns a pass/fail verdict.
160	
161	### Phase 4: COMMIT (Unchanged)
162	
163	After passing adversarial review:
164	
165	1. Merge the worktree branch into the working branch
166	2. Clean up the worktree (`git worktree remove`)
167	3. Log the session for self-reflection
168	4. Commit with DoD verification in the commit message
169	
170	---
171	
172	## Escalation Model (Availability-Aware)
173	
174	The orchestrator adapts its escalation chain based on which tools passed their health checks.
175	
176	### Scenario 1: Two External Tools Available (5 attempts max)
177	
178	```
179	Model A implements (attempt 1)
180	  Orchestrator validates + cross-model review (B + Claude)
181	  FAIL -> feedback to Model A
182	
183	Model A implements (attempt 2, with review feedback)
184	  Orchestrator validates + cross-model review
185	  FAIL -> escalate to Model B
186	
187	Model B implements (attempt 1, with Model A's branch as reference)
188	  Orchestrator validates + cross-model review (A + Claude)
189	  FAIL -> feedback to Model B
190	
191	Model B implements (attempt 2, with review feedback)
192	  Orchestrator validates + cross-model review
193	  FAIL -> escalate to Claude
194	
195	Claude implements (with both branches as reference)
196	  Orchestrator validates + cross-model review (A + B)
197	  FAIL -> alert user with all branches, findings, CI results
198	```
199	
200	**Worst case: A(2) + B(2) + Claude(1) = 5 attempts before user alert.**
201	
202	### Scenario 2: One External Tool Available (3 attempts max)
203	
204	```
205	Model A implements (attempt 1)
206	  Orchestrator validates + review (Claude reviews)
207	  FAIL -> feedback to Model A
208	
209	Model A implements (attempt 2, with review feedback)
210	  Orchestrator validates + review (Claude reviews)
211	  FAIL -> escalate to Claude
212	
213	Claude implements (with Model A's branch as reference)
214	  Orchestrator validates + review (Model A reviews)
215	  FAIL -> alert user
216	```
217	
218	**Worst case: A(2) + Claude(1) = 3 attempts before user alert.**
219	
220	### Scenario 3: No External Tools Available
221	
222	```
223	Existing metaswarm behavior unchanged.
224	Claude implements via Task() mechanism.
225	Standard adversarial review (fresh Task() instance).
226	```
227	
228	No change to the current workflow. The skill is a no-op.
229	
230	---
231	
232	## Routing Logic
233	
234	The orchestrator selects the implementer for each task using cheapest-available selection:
235	
236	```python
237	available_tools = [t for t in adapters if t.health() == "ready"]
238	
239	if len(available_tools) == 2:
240	    implementer = cheapest(available_tools)
241	    reviewers = [other_tool, claude]
242	    escalation = [implementer, other_tool, claude, user]
243	elif len(available_tools) == 1:
244	    implementer = available_tools[0]
245	    reviewers = [implementer, claude]  # mutual review
246	    escalation = [implementer, claude, user]
247	else:
248	    # pure metaswarm, no change
249	    implementer = claude
250	    reviewers = [claude_fresh_task]
251	    escalation = [claude, user]
252	```
253	
254	**Selection criteria for `cheapest()`**:
255	
256	1. Pick the tool with lower estimated cost per invocation (based on model pricing and average token usage from session logs)
257	2. On tie, prefer the tool with higher historical success rate for this task type
258	3. If no historical data, prefer Gemini (free tier) over Codex
259	
260	---
261	
262	## Prompt File Format
263	
264	The prompt file is the key interface between the orchestrator and external tools. It must be **self-contained** -- the external tool has no access to BEADS, knowledge base, or conversation history.
265	
266	```markdown
267	# Task: [task title]
268	
269	## Acceptance Criteria
270	- [ ] criterion 1
271	- [ ] criterion 2
272	- [ ] criterion 3
273	
274	## Context
275	[relevant file contents, token-budgeted]
276	[prioritized: changed files > test files > imports > surrounding context]
277	
278	## Coding Standards
279	[extracted from project's CLAUDE.md / coding standards guide]
280	[language-specific rules, naming conventions, patterns]
281	
282	## Test Expectations
283	[what tests should pass after implementation]
284	[coverage requirements from .coverage-thresholds.json]
285	[test runner command: e.g., npx vitest run]
286	
287	## Previous Attempt (if retry)
288	[review feedback from last attempt -- what to fix]
289	[DO NOT include full prior output, only the actionable feedback summary]
290	
291	## Prior Model's Attempt (if escalation)
292	[diff summary from previous model's branch -- what was tried, why it failed]
293	[DO NOT include full branch, only the relevant diff summary]
294	```
295	
296	**Token budgeting rules** (handled by `package_context()` in `_common.sh`):
297	
298	- Estimate tokens per file using chars/4 heuristic
299	- Prioritize context: changed files > test files > imports > surrounding context
300	- Truncate to the model's context budget (configurable per adapter)
301	- On retry: include review feedback summary, not full prior output
302	- On escalation: include prior branch diff summary, not full branch
303	
304	---
305	
306	## Error Handling
307	
308	When an adapter returns `exit_code != 0`, the `error_type` field determines the orchestrator's response:
309	
310	| Error Type | Meaning | Orchestrator Action |
311	|---|---|---|
312	| `tool_not_installed` | Binary not found | Skip adapter, try next in escalation chain |
313	| `auth_expired` | API key invalid or expired | Escalate to user with setup instructions |
314	| `auth_missing` | No API key configured | Escalate to user with setup instructions |
315	| `network_error` | Cannot reach API | Retry with exponential backoff, then skip adapter |
316	| `rate_limited` | API rate limit hit | Wait and retry (respect Retry-After if provided) |
317	| `timeout` | Tool exceeded time limit | Retry once with increased timeout, then skip adapter |
318	| `context_too_large` | Input exceeds context window | Reduce context via `package_context()`, retry |
319	| `cost_limit_exceeded` | Would exceed budget | Skip adapter, alert user about budget status |
320	| `tool_crash` | Unexpected exit | Retry once, then skip adapter |
321	| `output_parse_error` | Malformed JSON output | Log raw output for debugging, treat as failure |
322	
323	**General rules:**
324	
325	- "Skip adapter" means move to the next tool in the escalation chain, not abort the task
326	- All errors are logged to `~/.claude/sessions/` for self-reflection
327	- Auth errors always escalate to the user -- the orchestrator cannot fix credentials
328	- Network and rate-limit errors get transient-failure treatment (backoff + retry)
329	
330	---
331	
332	## Budget Enforcement
333	
334	Two circuit breakers prevent runaway costs:
335	
336	### Per-Task Budget (`per_task_usd`)
337	
338	Before each adapter invocation, the orchestrator checks cumulative cost for the current task:
339	
340	```
341	if task_cost_so_far + estimated_cost > per_task_usd:
342	    error_type = "cost_limit_exceeded"
343	    skip adapter, try next in escalation chain (or Claude)
344	```
345	
346	Default: `$2.00` per task.
347	
348	### Per-Session Budget (`per_session_usd`)
349	
350	Before each adapter invocation, the orchestrator checks cumulative cost across all tasks in the session:
351	
352	```
353	if session_cost_so_far + estimated_cost > per_session_usd:
354	    error_type = "cost_limit_exceeded"
355	    alert user: "Session budget exhausted. External tools disabled for remaining tasks."
356	    fall back to Claude for all remaining work
357	```
358	
359	Default: `$20.00` per session.
360	
361	### Cost Tracking
362	
363	Cost is extracted post-execution from the adapter's JSON output (`cost.input_tokens`, `cost.output_tokens`) and converted to USD using known model pricing. Cost estimation is approximate (~15-30% variance due to different tokenizers per model). There is no pre-execution cost prediction in v1 -- cost is measured after execution, not capped before.
364	
365	---
366	
367	## Anti-Patterns
368	
369	| # | Anti-Pattern | Why It's Wrong | What to Do Instead |
370	|---|---|---|---|
371	| 1 | **Trusting external tool self-reports** -- tool says "all tests pass" and orchestrator believes it | External tools can hallucinate, skip steps, or misinterpret results; same reasoning as the no-self-certification rule for subagents | Orchestrator runs Phase 2 (VALIDATE) independently; adapter output contains facts only, never verdicts |
372	| 2 | **Skipping cross-model review** -- "the external tool's code looks fine, committing directly" | Single-model blind spots are the whole reason this skill exists; bypassing review defeats the purpose | Always run Phase 3 with a different model as reviewer; cross-model review is mandatory |
373	| 3 | **Running without timeout** -- invoking an adapter without `safe_invoke()` timeout wrapper | External CLIs can hang on rate limits, network issues, or infinite loops; an unmonitored invocation blocks the entire pipeline | Always use `safe_invoke()` with the configured `timeout_seconds`; handle timeout as a retryable error |
374	| 4 | **Passing the full environment** -- running the external tool with the orchestrator's full env vars | Leaks API keys, session tokens, and internal state to an external process that only needs its own credentials | Use `env -i` to pass only `HOME`, `PATH`, and the tool's own API key; see Minimal Environment in the design doc |
375	| 5 | **Working in the main repo** -- running external tools directly in the project working directory instead of a worktree | Concurrent invocations corrupt each other's state; failed attempts leave dirty files; no clean rollback path | Always create an isolated git worktree per invocation; merge only after all phases pass |
376	
377	---
378	
379	## Logging
380	
381	All adapter invocations produce structured session log entries in `~/.claude/sessions/`:
382	
383	```
384	Session log entry:
385	  - timestamp, tool, command, model, attempt
386	  - prompt content hash (what we asked)
387	  - output JSON (facts: exit code, files changed, cost)
388	  - CI gate results (from orchestrator's Phase 2)
389	  - review output (from cross-model Phase 3)
390	  - outcome: success | retry | escalated | user_alert
391	  - cost (tokens used, estimated USD)
392	```
393	
394	These logs feed the existing `/self-reflect` pipeline. Over time, the knowledge base accumulates routing intelligence:
395	
396	- **Patterns**: "Codex excels at single-file TypeScript implementations"
397	- **Gotchas**: "Gemini tends to skip error handling in async functions"
398	- **Routing decisions**: "Route database migration tasks directly to Claude (external tools fail 80%)"
399	
400	Future routing decisions improve automatically as the knowledge base grows from session log analysis.
401

2	name: create-issue
3	description: Create comprehensive GitHub issues with TDD plans, acceptance criteria, and agent instructions for autonomous PR lifecycle management
4	---
5	
6	# Create GitHub Issue Skill
7	
8	Create comprehensive, well-structured GitHub issues with embedded agent instructions for autonomous PR lifecycle management.
9	
10	## Overview
11	
12	This skill generates GitHub issues that are self-contained work packets. Each issue includes:
13	
14	1. **Problem/Feature specification** with clear scope
15	2. **Technical specification** with types, schemas, and API design
16	3. **TDD Implementation Plan** with test-first cycles
17	4. **Acceptance Criteria** checklist
18	5. **Agent Instructions** for complete PR lifecycle (implementation → review → merge)
19	
20	## Usage
21	
22	```
23	/create-issue
24	```
25	
26	## Interactive Flow
27	
28	### Step 1: Issue Type
29	
30	Ask the user:
31	
32	```
33	What type of issue is this?
34	1. Feature - New functionality
35	2. Bug - Something broken
36	3. Refactor - Improving existing code
37	```
38	
39	### Step 2: Brief Description
40	
41	```
42	Brief description of the problem or feature? (1-2 sentences)
43	```
44	
45	### Step 3: Complexity Assessment
46	
47	```
48	What's the estimated complexity?
49	1. Simple (< 1 day)
50	2. Medium (2-5 days)
51	3. Complex (> 1 week)
52	```
53	
54	### Step 4: Context Gathering
55	
56	```
57	Any related files, services, or issues I should reference?
58	(Or say "explore codebase" and I'll investigate)
59	```
60	
61	### Step 5: Generate Issue
62	
63	Based on inputs, generate the full issue using the appropriate template below.
64	
65	---
66	
67	## Issue Templates
68	
69	### Feature Template
70	
71	````markdown
72	## Summary
73	
74	[1-2 sentence description]
75	
76	**Complexity**: [simple|medium|complex]
77	**Estimated Effort**: [X days]
78	
79	---
80	
81	## Problem
82	
83	[What problem does this solve? Why is it needed?]
84	
85	### Evidence
86	
87	[Logs, metrics, user reports, or reasoning]
88	
89	### Scope
90	
91	- IN SCOPE: [what's included]
92	- OUT OF SCOPE: [what's explicitly NOT included]
93	
94	---
95	
96	## Architecture Decision
97	
98	**Decision**: [What approach will be taken]
99	**Rationale**: [Why this approach, why NOT alternatives]
100	
101	---
102	
103	## Technical Specification
104	
105	### Types/Interfaces
106	
107	```typescript
108	// Key type definitions
109	```
110	````
111	
112	### Zod Schemas (if applicable)
113	
114	```typescript
115	// Validation schemas
116	```
117	
118	### API Endpoints (if applicable)
119	
120	| Method | Path | Description |
121	| ------ | ---- | ----------- |
122	
123	### Database Changes (if applicable)
124	
125	[Schema changes, migrations needed]
126	
127	---
128	
129	## TDD Implementation Plan
130	
131	### Cycle 1: [Happy Path]
132	
133	```typescript
134	it("should [expected behavior]", async () => {
135	  // Test code
136	});
137	```
138	
139	### Cycle 2: [Error Handling]
140	
141	```typescript
142	it("should handle [error case]", async () => {
143	  // Test code
144	});
145	```
146	
147	### Cycle 3: [Edge Cases]
148	
149	### Cycle 4: [Integration]
150	
151	---
152	
153	## Error Handling
154	
155	| Scenario | Behavior | Logging |
156	| -------- | -------- | ------- |
157	
158	---
159	
160	## Implementation Phases
161	
162	### Phase 1: [Core] (High Priority)
163	
164	- [ ] Task 1
165	- [ ] Task 2
166	
167	### Phase 2: [Enhancement] (Medium Priority)
168	
169	- [ ] Task 3
170	
171	---
172	
173	## Files to Create/Modify
174	
175	- **NEW**: `path/to/new/file.ts`
176	- `path/to/existing/file.ts` - [what changes]
177	
178	---
179	
180	## Acceptance Criteria
181	
182	- [ ] All TDD cycles implemented with passing tests
183	- [ ] Zero TypeScript errors (`pnpm typecheck`)
184	- [ ] Zero lint warnings (`pnpm lint`)
185	- [ ] All tests pass (`pnpm test --run`)
186	- [ ] **JSDoc documentation** added to all new/modified services
187	- [ ] **`docs/SERVICE_INVENTORY.md`** updated (if new/modified service)
188	- [ ] **Mock factories** added/updated (if new service)
189	- [ ] PR created and all CI checks pass
190	- [ ] **ALL code review comments addressed** (see Agent Instructions)
191	- [ ] All review threads resolved
192	- [ ] PR approved and merged
193	
194	---
195	
196	## Agent Instructions
197	
198	**CRITICAL**: Follow this protocol when implementing this issue. A PR is NOT complete until ALL steps are done.
199	
200	### Phase 0: Research (BEFORE writing any code)
201	
202	**Read these files first to avoid reinventing the wheel:**
203	
204	1. **Service Inventory** - Check for existing services that might already do what you need:
205	
206	   ```bash
207	   # Read the service inventory
208	   cat docs/SERVICE_INVENTORY.md
209	   # Or search for specific functionality
210	   grep -i "embedding\|search\|cache" docs/SERVICE_INVENTORY.md
211	   ```
212	
213	2. **Mock Factories** - Review existing mocks to reuse patterns:
214	
215	   ```bash
216	   ls src/test-utils/factories/
217	   ```
218	
219	3. **Similar Services** - Find services with similar patterns:
220	   ```bash
221	   # Find orchestrator patterns
222	   ls src/lib/services/*-orchestrator*.ts
223	   # Find persistence patterns
224	   ls src/lib/services/*-persistence*.ts
225	   ```
226	
227	**Why this matters**: Reusing existing services and patterns saves time, maintains consistency, and prevents duplicate code.
228	
229	### Phase A: Implementation
230	
231	1. Create feature branch from main: `git checkout -b feat/[short-name]`
232	2. Follow TDD cycles exactly as specified above
233	3. **Add JSDoc documentation** to all new/modified services
234	4. Run local validation: `pnpm lint && pnpm typecheck && pnpm test --run`
235	5. **Update service inventory** (if new service)
236	6. Create PR with comprehensive description using `/create-pr`
237	
238	### Phase B: Code Review Iteration
239	
240	**Wait for code reviews before proceeding. Do NOT mark complete after pushing code.**
241	
242	For EACH code review iteration:
243	
244	1. **Fetch ALL comments** (including trivial, nitpicks, and out-of-scope):
245	
246	   ```bash
247	   PR_NUMBER=XXX
248	   OWNER=$(gh repo view --json owner -q .owner.login)
249	   REPO=$(gh repo view --json name -q .name)
250	   gh api "repos/$OWNER/$REPO/pulls/$PR_NUMBER/comments" --paginate
251	   ```
252	
253	2. **Address ALL comments by category**:
254	
255	   | Type                   | Action                                               |
256	   | ---------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
257	   | Critical/Major         | Fix immediately                                      |
258	   | Medium/Minor           | Fix before merge                                     |
259	   | **Trivial/Nitpick**    | **Fix these too!** Do NOT skip                       |
260	   | **Out-of-scope**       | **Investigate thoroughly** - often the BEST insights |
261	
262	3. **For each comment**:
263	   - **< 1 day of work** → Implement the fix in this PR
264	   - **> 1 day OR architectural** → Create a new GitHub issue, link in response
265	   - **Disagree** → Reply explaining why with technical reasoning
266	
267	4. **After making changes**:
268	
269	   ```bash
270	   pnpm lint && pnpm typecheck && pnpm test --run
271	   git add . && git commit -m "fix: address review feedback" && git push
272	   ```
273	
274	5. **Respond to EVERY comment thread individually**:
275	
276	   ```bash
277	   COMMENT_ID=XXX
278	   CURRENT_USER=$(gh api user -q '.login')
279	   gh api "/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/pulls/$PR_NUMBER/comments/$COMMENT_ID/replies" \
280	     -X POST \
281	     -f body="Fixed in commit $(git rev-parse --short HEAD). [explanation]
282	
283	   *(Response by Claude on behalf of @$CURRENT_USER)*"
284	   ```
285	
286	6. **Wait for next review cycle** - Do NOT resolve threads yourself
287	
288	7. **Repeat steps 1-6** until reviewer confirms satisfaction
289	
290	### Phase C: Thread Resolution
291	
292	Only AFTER reviewer approval:
293	
294	1. Re-fetch all threads to get current IDs
295	2. Resolve each thread:
296	
297	   ```bash
298	   THREAD_ID="PRRT_xxx"
299	   gh api graphql -f threadId="$THREAD_ID" -f query='
300	     mutation($threadId: ID!) {
301	       resolveReviewThread(input: {threadId: $threadId}) {
302	         thread { id isResolved }
303	       }
304	     }'
305	   ```
306	
307	3. Verify all threads resolved before proceeding
308	
309	### Phase D: Merge Readiness
310	
311	PR is ready for merge ONLY when ALL are true:
312	
313	- [ ] All CI checks pass (green)
314	- [ ] **EVERY** code review comment addressed (including trivial/out-of-scope)
315	- [ ] **EVERY** thread has individual response
316	- [ ] All threads are resolved (after reviewer approval)
317	- [ ] GitHub issues created for any deferred work (> 1 day)
318	- [ ] No pending reviewer comments awaiting response
319	
320	### Phase E: Squash Merge and Cleanup
321	
322	**IMPORTANT**: Always use squash merge, never regular merge.
323	
324	1. **Squash and merge** the PR (combines all commits into one clean commit):
325	
326	   ```bash
327	   # Via GitHub CLI
328	   gh pr merge $PR_NUMBER --squash --delete-branch
329	
330	   # Or via GitHub web UI:
331	   # Click "Squash and merge" (NOT "Merge" or "Rebase and merge")
332	   ```
333	
334	2. **Delete the feature branch** (automatically done with --delete-branch flag):
335	
336	   ```bash
337	   # If branch wasn't auto-deleted:
338	   git push origin --delete feat/[branch-name]
339	   git branch -d feat/[branch-name]
340	   ```
341	
342	3. **Update local main**:
343	
344	   ```bash
345	   git checkout main
346	   git pull origin main
347	   ```
348	
349	### Reference
350	
351	See the `/metaswarm:handle-pr-comments` command for detailed protocol.
352	
353	---
354	
355	## Related Issues
356	
357	- [Link to related issues]
358	
359	## References
360	
361	- [Link to relevant docs, specs, or PRs]
362	
363	````
364	
365	---
366	
367	### Bug Template
368	
369	Use the Feature template with these modifications:
370	
371	**Title format**: `fix: [brief description]`
372	
373	**Replace "Problem" section with**:
374	
375	```markdown
376	## Bug Report
377	
378	**Priority**: [P0-Critical|P1-High|P2-Medium|P3-Low]
379	**Affected Users**: [scope of impact]
380	**First Reported**: [date/source]
381	
382	### Expected Behavior
383	
384	[What should happen]
385	
386	### Actual Behavior
387	
388	[What actually happens]
389	
390	### Steps to Reproduce
391	
392	1. Step 1
393	2. Step 2
394	3. Step 3
395	
396	### Root Cause Analysis
397	
398	[If known, explain why this is happening]
399	
400	### Evidence
401	
402	[Logs, stack traces, screenshots]
403	````
404	
405	**Replace "TDD Implementation Plan" with**:
406	
407	````markdown
408	## Test Cases
409	
410	### Test 1: Verify fix for reported issue
411	
412	```typescript
413	it("should [correct behavior] when [condition]", async () => {
414	  // Reproduce the bug scenario
415	  // Verify it's now fixed
416	});
417	```
418	````
419	
420	### Test 2: Regression test
421	
422	```typescript
423	it("should not regress [related functionality]", async () => {
424	  // Ensure fix doesn't break other things
425	});
426	```
427	
428	````
429	
430	---
431	
432	### Refactor Template
433	
434	Use the Feature template with these modifications:
435	
436	**Title format**: `refactor: [brief description]`
437	
438	**Add after "Summary"**:
439	
440	```markdown
441	## Background
442	
443	[Why is this refactor needed? What triggered it?]
444	
445	### Review Feedback Being Addressed
446	
447	> [Quote the review comment that prompted this work]
448	
449	- Original PR: #XXX
450	- Comment by: @reviewer
451	````
452	
453	**Add to "Acceptance Criteria"**:
454	
455	```markdown
456	- [ ] No breaking changes to public APIs
457	- [ ] All existing tests still pass without modification
458	- [ ] Performance not degraded (if applicable)
459	```
460	
461	---
462	
463	## After Generation
464	
465	After generating the issue content:
466	
467	1. **Show preview** to user
468	2. **Ask for modifications** if needed
469	3. **Create issue** with appropriate labels:
470	
471	   ```bash
472	   # Feature
473	   gh issue create --title "feat: [title]" --body "[content]" \
474	     --label "enhancement" --label "complexity:[level]"
475	
476	   # Bug
477	   gh issue create --title "fix: [title]" --body "[content]" \
478	     --label "bug" --label "priority:[level]"
479	
480	   # Refactor
481	   gh issue create --title "refactor: [title]" --body "[content]" \
482	     --label "refactor" --label "complexity:[level]"
483	   ```
484	
485	4. **Report issue number** to user
486	
487	---
488	
489	## Key Principles
490	
491	1. **Self-contained**: Issues should have everything an agent needs to work autonomously
492	2. **TDD-first**: Always include test specifications before implementation
493	3. **Complete lifecycle**: Agent Instructions cover implementation through merge
494	4. **No shortcuts**: ALL review comments must be addressed, including trivial/nitpicks
495	5. **Out-of-scope = gold**: Comments marked out-of-scope often contain the best insights
496	6. **Iterate until done**: PR is not complete until ALL threads are resolved
