2	name: handling-pr-comments
3	description: Address PR review feedback systematically — fetch inline comments, review bodies, handle outside-diff-range comments, resolve threads with proper attribution
4	---
5	
6	# handling-pr-comments
7	
8	Use when addressing PR review feedback, after receiving review comments from CodeRabbit, Cursor, or human reviewers - ensures systematic responses to each comment thread with proper attribution and thread resolution.
9	
10	## When to Activate
11	
12	Activate this skill when ANY of these conditions are true:
13	
14	- User asks to "address PR comments" or "handle review feedback"
15	- User mentions CodeRabbit, Cursor bot, or reviewer comments
16	- User is working on fixes requested in a PR review
17	- User asks to "check PR comments" or "respond to reviewers"
18	- After making fixes to address review feedback
19	
20	## CRITICAL: The Complete Workflow
21	
22	**Most developers forget steps 4-6. This skill ensures they happen.**
23	
24	### Phase 1: Discover and Filter Comments
25	
26	Run the filtering script to identify actionable comments:
27	
28	```bash
29	# Filter actionable vs non-actionable comments
30	bin/pr-comments-filter.sh <PR_NUMBER>
31	```
32	
33	This script:
34	
35	- Filters out non-actionable comments (confirmations, acknowledgments, fingerprinting)
36	- Categorizes actionable comments by priority (Critical → Low)
37	- Shows comment IDs and details for processing
38	
39	### Phase 2: Triage Actionable Comments
40	
41	The filter script categorizes by priority:
42	
43	| Priority        | Marker                                                   | Action           |
44	| --------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------- |
45	| CRITICAL        | `_⚠️ Potential issue_ \| _🔴 Critical_`                  | Fix immediately  |
46	| HIGH            | `_⚠️ Potential issue_ \| _🟠 Major_`                     | Fix before merge |
47	| MEDIUM          | `_🟡 Minor_` or `_🛠️ Refactor suggestion_ \| _🟠 Major_` | Should fix       |
48	| LOW             | `_🔵 Trivial_` / `_🧹 Nitpick_`                          | Fix if quick     |
49	| HUMAN           | Non-bot comments                                         | Always process   |
50	
51	For each actionable comment, further categorize as:
52	
53	1. **Bug/Issue** - Must fix
54	2. **Enhancement** - Should fix
55	3. **Nitpick** - Nice to fix
56	4. **Question** - Needs clarification
57	5. **Intentional** - Decline with explanation
58	6. **Out-of-Scope** - See Phase 2b
59	
60	### Phase 2b: Extract "Outside Diff Range" Comments from Review Bodies (CRITICAL)
61	
62	**COMMONLY MISSED**: CodeRabbit posts "Outside diff range" comments in the **review body**, not as inline threads. These are actionable feedback that MUST be addressed.
63	
64	```bash
65	# Extract Outside diff range comments from review bodies
66	PR_NUMBER=<number>
67	OWNER=$(gh repo view --json owner -q .owner.login)
68	REPO_NAME=$(gh repo view --json name -q .name)
69	
70	echo "=== OUTSIDE DIFF RANGE COMMENTS ==="
71	gh api "repos/$OWNER/$REPO_NAME/pulls/$PR_NUMBER/reviews" --paginate | \
72	  jq -r '.[] | select(.body | test("Outside diff range"; "i")) |
73	    "Review ID: \(.id)\n\(.body)\n---"'
74	```
75	
76	**These comments are NOT in threads** - they cannot be replied to inline. You must:
77	
78	1. Parse the file paths and line numbers from the review body
79	2. Address the feedback in your code
80	3. Commit and push
81	4. Leave a general PR comment acknowledging you addressed them
82	
83	### Phase 2c: Handle Other Out-of-Scope Comments
84	
85	Run the out-of-scope detection script:
86	
87	```bash
88	# Detect out-of-scope comments
89	bin/pr-comments-out-of-scope.sh <PR_NUMBER>
90	```
91	
92	This script detects comments that:
93	
94	- Reference lines NOT in the PR diff
95	- Are marked "outdated" by GitHub (GraphQL `isOutdated` flag)
96	- Are general PR discussion comments
97	
98	**IMPORTANT**: Treat out-of-scope comments as **IN SCOPE** by default.
99	
100	#### Evaluation Process
101	
102	Use **ultrathink** to evaluate each out-of-scope comment:
103	
104	```text
105	ultrathink: Analyze this out-of-scope review comment:
106	- What is the reviewer asking for?
107	- How complex is this change? (lines of code, files affected)
108	- Does it require refactoring other systems?
109	- Can I complete this in under 30 minutes?
110	- Are there any risks or dependencies?
111	
112	Recommend: FIX_NOW or CREATE_ISSUE
113	```
114	
115	#### Decision Matrix
116	
117	| Criteria                                      | Action                                    |
118	| --------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
119	| Simple fix (< 30 min, < 3 files, no refactor) | **FIX_NOW** - Make the change immediately |
120	| Medium complexity (unclear scope)             | **ASK_USER** - Present options            |
121	| Major refactor (multiple systems, risky)      | **CREATE_ISSUE** - Document for follow-up |
122	
123	#### For FIX_NOW
124	
125	1. Make the fix
126	2. Commit with descriptive message
127	3. Push to the PR branch
128	4. Reply: "Fixed in commit <hash>. This was outside the original PR scope but straightforward to address."
129	5. Resolve the thread
130	
131	#### For CREATE_ISSUE
132	
133	1. Create GitHub issue with context
134	2. Reply: "Created issue #<number> to track this."
135	3. Resolve the thread
136	
137	### Phase 3: Make Fixes
138	
139	Fix the actual code issues. Commit and push.
140	
141	### Phase 4: RESPOND TO EACH THREAD (Often Forgotten!)
142	
143	**After pushing fixes, respond to EACH comment thread individually:**
144	
145	```bash
146	PR_NUMBER=<number>
147	OWNER=$(gh repo view --json owner -q .owner.login)
148	REPO_NAME=$(gh repo view --json name -q .name)
149	CURRENT_USER=$(gh api user -q '.login')
150	COMMENT_ID=<id-from-filter-script>
151	
152	gh api "/repos/$OWNER/$REPO_NAME/pulls/$PR_NUMBER/comments/$COMMENT_ID/replies" \
153	  -X POST \
154	  -f body="Fixed in commit $(git rev-parse --short HEAD).
155	
156	*(Response by Claude on behalf of @$CURRENT_USER)*"
157	```
158	
159	### Phase 5: Resolve ALL Threads
160	
161	**Every thread must be resolved after responding.** Use GraphQL to resolve:
162	
163	```bash
164	THREAD_ID="PRRT_kwDOK-xA485..."  # From GraphQL query
165	
166	gh api graphql -f query='mutation {
167	  resolveReviewThread(input: {threadId: "'"$THREAD_ID"'"}) {
168	    thread { id isResolved }
169	  }
170	}'
171	```
172	
173	### Phase 6: Handle Threads That Can't Be Resolved
174	
175	1. **Query the comment author** asking for specific follow-up
176	2. **Do NOT leave unresolved** - either resolve after responding, or ask for clarification
177	3. If waiting for author response, mark as needing user input
178	
179	### Phase 7: Post-Push Iteration Check (MANDATORY)
180	
181	**THE #1 WORKFLOW FAILURE: Stopping after Phase 5-6 without checking for NEW comments.**
182	
183	Automated reviewers (CodeRabbit, Cursor) analyze EVERY commit you push. They post NEW comments during/after their check runs.
184	
185	```bash
186	# STEP 1: Wait for ALL CI/CD checks to complete
187	PR_NUMBER=<number>
188	echo "Waiting for CI/CD checks to complete..."
189	gh pr checks $PR_NUMBER --watch
190	
191	# STEP 2: Check for NEW comments since your last response
192	OWNER=$(gh repo view --json owner -q .owner.login)
193	REPO_NAME=$(gh repo view --json name -q .name)
194	CURRENT_USER=$(gh api user -q '.login')
195	
196	# Get timestamp of your last reply
197	LAST_REPLY=$(gh api "repos/$OWNER/$REPO_NAME/pulls/$PR_NUMBER/comments" --paginate | \
198	  jq -r "[.[] | select(.user.login == \"$CURRENT_USER\") | select(.in_reply_to_id)] | sort_by(.created_at) | last | .created_at")
199	
200	# Handle case where user has no previous replies
201	if [ -z "$LAST_REPLY" ] || [ "$LAST_REPLY" = "null" ]; then
202	  echo "No previous replies found - checking all comments as new"
203	  LAST_REPLY="1970-01-01T00:00:00Z"  # Unix epoch - treat all comments as new
204	fi
205	
206	# Check for new comments after that time
207	NEW_COUNT=$(gh api "repos/$OWNER/$REPO_NAME/pulls/$PR_NUMBER/comments" --paginate | \
208	  jq -r --arg time "$LAST_REPLY" '[.[] | select(.in_reply_to_id == null) | select(.created_at > $time)] | length')
209	
210	# STEP 3: Also check review bodies for new "Outside diff range" comments
211	NEW_REVIEWS=$(gh api "repos/$OWNER/$REPO_NAME/pulls/$PR_NUMBER/reviews" --paginate | \
212	  jq -r --arg time "$LAST_REPLY" '[.[] | select(.submitted_at > $time) | select(.body | test("Outside diff range"; "i"))] | length')
213	
214	TOTAL_NEW=$((NEW_COUNT + NEW_REVIEWS))
215	
216	if [ "$TOTAL_NEW" -gt 0 ]; then
217	  echo "$NEW_COUNT NEW INLINE COMMENT(S) + $NEW_REVIEWS NEW REVIEW BODY COMMENT(S) DETECTED"
218	  echo "ACTION: Return to Phase 1 and iterate"
219	else
220	  echo "No new comments - safe to proceed to verification"
221	fi
222	```
223	
224	**If NEW comments found**: Return to Phase 1. DO NOT proceed to verification.
225	
226	**Iteration Loop**:
227	
228	```text
229	REPEAT:
230	  Phase 1: Discover comments
231	  Phase 2: Triage
232	  Phase 3: Fix
233	  Phase 4: Respond
234	  Phase 5: Resolve threads
235	  Phase 6: Handle unclear threads
236	  Phase 7: Check for NEW comments after push
237	
238	  IF new comments found → GO TO Phase 1
239	  IF no new comments → proceed to verification
240	```
241	
242	## Response Templates
243	
244	### For Fixes Made
245	
246	```text
247	Fixed in commit <hash>.
248	
249	*(Response by Claude on behalf of @username)*
250	```
251	
252	### For Acknowledged Nitpicks
253	
254	```text
255	Acknowledged - this is a valid suggestion. Deferring to a future cleanup PR to keep this PR focused.
256	
257	*(Response by Claude on behalf of @username)*
258	```
259	
260	### For Intentional Decisions
261	
262	```text
263	This is intentional because [reason]. The [thing] is designed to [explanation].
264	
265	*(Response by Claude on behalf of @username)*
266	```
267	
268	## Mandatory Pre-Completion Check
269	
270	**BLOCKING: You MUST run this script and show its output before declaring ANY PR ready:**
271	
272	```bash
273	bin/pr-comments-check.sh <PR_NUMBER>
274	```
275	
276	This script:
277	
278	- Returns exit code 0 if all comments addressed
279	- Returns exit code 1 if ANY unaddressed comments exist
280	- Shows status for each comment
281	
282	**If the script shows ANY unaddressed comments, you are NOT done.** Address each unaddressed comment:
283	
284	1. If actionable → Fix it and reply confirming the fix
285	2. If out-of-scope → Reply explaining deferral (create issue if needed)
286	3. If disagree → Reply with reasoning
287	4. **NEVER ignore silently**
288	
289	**You must show the script output in your response** as proof that all comments are addressed. Example:
290	
291	```
292	Checking PR #908 for unaddressed comments...
293	
294	=== Inline Code Review Comments ===
295	Comment 123 by cursor[bot] - 1 reply(s) [OK]
296	Comment 456 by coderabbitai[bot] - 1 reply(s) [OK]
297	
298	=== General PR Discussion Comments ===
299	coderabbitai[bot]: <!-- summary -->...
300	
301	All inline review comments have been addressed
302	```
303	
304	A PR is NOT ready until this script returns success.
305	
306	## Verification Checklist
307	
308	Before declaring PR comments handled:
309	
310	- [ ] Ran `bin/pr-comments-filter.sh <PR>` to identify actionable comments
311	- [ ] **CRITICAL**: Extracted "Outside diff range" comments from review bodies (Phase 2b)
312	- [ ] Ran `bin/pr-comments-out-of-scope.sh <PR>` to find other out-of-scope feedback
313	- [ ] Code fixes have been made and pushed
314	- [ ] Each comment thread has a response posted
315	- [ ] "Outside diff range" comments addressed with a general PR comment
316	- [ ] **POST-PUSH CHECK**: Waited for CI/CD to complete, checked for NEW comments
317	- [ ] **NO new comments found** after the post-push check (iterate if found)
318	- [ ] **ALL threads have been resolved** (no unresolved threads remaining)
319	- [ ] All responses include proper attribution
320	- [ ] Out-of-scope comments have been either fixed OR have GitHub issues created
321	
322	**DO NOT skip the "Outside diff range" check (Phase 2b) - this is the #2 cause of incomplete PR handling.**
323	
324	## Reference
325	
326	For the complete detailed workflow with all edge cases and troubleshooting, see:
327	the `/metaswarm:handle-pr-comments` command
328

1	---
2	name: visual-review
3	description: Take screenshots of web pages and UI using Playwright for visual review and iteration
4	auto_activate: false
5	triggers:
6	  - "visual review"
7	  - "screenshot"
8	  - "take a screenshot"
9	  - "review visually"
10	  - "check how it looks"
11	  - "review the UI"
12	  - "review the slides"
13	---
14	
15	# Visual Review
16	
17	## Purpose
18	
19	Agents cannot see rendered web pages, presentations, or UI. This skill bridges that gap by using Playwright to capture screenshots of web pages (local files, localhost servers, or deployed URLs) so agents can visually inspect their work, identify layout issues, and iterate.
20	
21	This is especially useful for:
22	- **Reveal.js presentations** — capture each slide individually
23	- **Web UIs** — verify layout, styling, and responsiveness
24	- **Landing pages** — check visual design matches intent
25	- **Email templates** — verify rendering
26	
27	## Prerequisites
28	
29	This skill requires **Playwright** to be available. Before first use, check and install if needed:
30	
31	```bash
32	# Check if Playwright is available
33	npx playwright --version 2>/dev/null
34	
35	# If not available, install it (one-time setup)
36	npx playwright install chromium
37	```
38	
39	**Important:** Only the `chromium` browser is needed. Do not install all browsers — it wastes disk space and time.
40	
41	If `npx playwright` fails entirely, the user may need to install it:
42	```bash
43	npm install -g playwright
44	npx playwright install chromium
45	```
46	
47	## Workflow
48	
49	### Phase 1: Setup
50	
51	1. **Verify Playwright is available** — run `npx playwright --version`
52	2. **If not installed** — run `npx playwright install chromium` and inform the user
53	3. **Create output directory** — `mkdir -p /tmp/visual-review`
54	4. **Determine the target** — local file path, localhost URL, or deployed URL
55	
56	### Phase 2: Capture Screenshots
57	
58	#### For a single page:
59	
60	```bash
61	npx playwright screenshot \
62	  --browser chromium \
63	  --viewport-size "1456,816" \
64	  --wait-for-timeout 3000 \
65	  "<URL_OR_FILE_PATH>" \
66	  /tmp/visual-review/page.png
67	```
68	
69	**Parameters:**
70	- `--viewport-size "1456,816"` — 16:9 aspect ratio, good for presentations
71	- `--wait-for-timeout 3000` — wait 3 seconds for fonts, images, and animations to settle
72	- Use `file:///absolute/path/to/file.html` for local files
73	- Use full URLs for deployed sites
74	
75	#### For Reveal.js presentations (multiple slides):
76	
77	Capture each slide by appending the slide hash to the URL:
78	
79	```bash
80	# Capture all slides (adjust count as needed)
81	for i in $(seq 0 15); do
82	  npx playwright screenshot \
83	    --browser chromium \
84	    --viewport-size "1456,816" \
85	    --wait-for-timeout 2000 \
86	    "<BASE_URL>#/$i" \
87	    "/tmp/visual-review/slide-$i.png"
88	done
89	```
90	
91	**Tip:** To capture slides with all fragments visible, add `?fragments=true` or use JavaScript injection via a Playwright script. The simpler approach is to accept that fragment-heavy slides will appear in their initial (pre-click) state.
92	
93	#### For responsive testing:
94	
95	```bash
96	# Mobile
97	npx playwright screenshot --viewport-size "390,844" "<URL>" /tmp/visual-review/mobile.png
98	
99	# Tablet
100	npx playwright screenshot --viewport-size "768,1024" "<URL>" /tmp/visual-review/tablet.png
101	
102	# Desktop wide
103	npx playwright screenshot --viewport-size "1920,1080" "<URL>" /tmp/visual-review/desktop.png
104	```
105	
106	### Phase 3: Show and Review Screenshots
107	
108	Use the Read tool to analyze each screenshot:
109	
110	```
111	Read /tmp/visual-review/slide-0.png
112	Read /tmp/visual-review/slide-1.png
113	...
114	```
115	
116	**Showing screenshots to the user:** The agent can see screenshots via Read, but the user cannot. When the user wants to see what you see — or when you want to discuss a visual issue together — there are two approaches depending on the environment:
117	
118	**Local machine (has a display):**
119	
120	```bash
121	# Open a specific screenshot
122	open /tmp/visual-review/slide-2.png
123	
124	# Open all screenshots at once
125	open /tmp/visual-review/slide-*.png
126	```
127	
128	**Remote/headless machine (SSH, tmux, etc.):**
129	
130	Serve the screenshots directory over HTTP so the user can view them in their local browser:
131	
132	```bash
133	# Start a simple file server (runs in background)
134	# Use a high port (>10000) to avoid conflicts with dev tools
135	python3 -m http.server 18080 --directory /tmp/visual-review &
136	
137	# User can now browse to:
138	#   http://<hostname>:18080/
139	#   http://<hostname>:18080/slide-2.png
140	#   etc.
141	```
142	
143	Pick any open high-numbered port (18080, 19090, etc.) — low ports like 8080 are often taken by dev tools. This serves the entire `/tmp/visual-review/` directory with an auto-generated file listing. The user can click through all screenshots in their browser. Stop the server when done:
144	
145	```bash
146	# Stop the background server
147	kill %1
148	# Or find and kill by port
149	lsof -ti:18080 | xargs kill
150	```
151	
152	Use whichever approach fits the environment. This is useful when collaborating on visual issues, when the user asks what something looks like, or when you want confirmation on a subjective design choice.
153	
154	For each screenshot, evaluate:
155	- **Layout** — Is content centered/aligned as intended?
156	- **Typography** — Are headings, body text, and code readable?
157	- **Colors** — Do accent colors, backgrounds, and contrasts look right?
158	- **Spacing** — Is there enough whitespace? Any cramped areas?
159	- **Content** — Is all expected content visible? (Note: fragment content may be hidden)
160	- **Responsiveness** — Does it work at the target viewport size?
161	
162	### Phase 4: Document Findings
163	
164	Create a findings summary:
165	
166	```markdown
167	## Visual Review — [Project Name]
168	
169	### Reviewed: [date]
170	
171	| Slide/Page | Status | Notes |
172	|-----------|--------|-------|
173	| slide-0   | Good   | Title centered, accent color correct |
174	| slide-1   | Issue  | Text overflows right edge on mobile |
175	| ...       | ...    | ... |
176	
177	### Issues to Fix
178	1. [Description of issue + which slide/page]
179	2. [...]
180	
181	### Looks Good
182	- [Things that are working well]
183	```
184	
185	### Phase 5: Fix and Re-capture
186	
187	After making fixes:
188	1. Re-capture only the affected slides/pages
189	2. Review the new screenshots
190	3. Repeat until satisfied
191	
192	## Reveal.js-Specific Tips
193	
194	- **Slide numbering**: Reveal.js uses `#/0` for the first slide, `#/1` for the second, etc.
195	- **Vertical slides**: Use `#/horizontal/vertical` format (e.g., `#/2/1`)
196	- **Fragments**: Content with `class="fragment"` is hidden until clicked. Screenshots show the initial state. This is expected behavior, not a bug.
197	- **Speaker notes**: Not visible in screenshots (they appear in a separate window with `S` key)
198	- **Theme loading**: Use `--wait-for-timeout 3000` to ensure CDN-loaded themes and fonts are ready
199	
200	## Common Mistakes
201	
202	| Mistake | Why It's Wrong | Do This Instead |
203	|---------|---------------|-----------------|
204	| Not waiting for page load | Fonts/images may not render | Use `--wait-for-timeout 3000` |
205	| Using `http://` for local files | File URLs need `file:///` protocol | Use `file:///absolute/path` |
206	| Installing all Playwright browsers | Wastes time and disk | Only install `chromium` |
207	| Treating fragment-hidden content as a bug | Fragments are designed to reveal on click | Note it as expected |
208	| Forgetting to create output directory | Screenshots fail silently | Always `mkdir -p /tmp/visual-review` first |
209	| Taking too many screenshots at once | Floods context window | Review in batches of 4-6 |
210	| Not offering to `open` when discussing visuals | User can't see what you're describing | Use `open` when collaborating on visual issues |
211	
212	## Integration Points
213	
214	**Upstream:** Any skill that produces visual output (presentations, web UIs, email templates)
215	
216	**Downstream:** Fixes feed back into the source files, then re-capture to verify
217	
218	## Verification Checklist
219	
220	- [ ] Playwright is installed and working
221	- [ ] Screenshots captured at correct viewport size
222	- [ ] All pages/slides reviewed
223	- [ ] Issues documented with specific descriptions
224	- [ ] Fixes verified with re-captured screenshots
225

1	# Testing Patterns Guide
2	
3	This is the canonical guide for how and why we write tests in projects using the metaswarm framework. It covers philosophy, practical patterns, infrastructure, and anti-patterns. Every agent and developer writing tests should read this document.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Table of Contents
8	
9	- [Philosophy](#philosophy)
10	  - [Why 100% Coverage?](#why-100-coverage)
11	  - [Why TDD?](#why-tdd)
12	  - [Tests Must Test Results, Not Presence](#tests-must-test-results-not-presence)
13	  - [Never Lobotomize a Test](#never-lobotomize-a-test)
14	  - [Dependency Injection Is Non-Negotiable](#dependency-injection-is-non-negotiable)
15	  - [No Live Services in Tests](#no-live-services-in-tests)
16	- [Practical Patterns](#practical-patterns)
17	  - [Test File Location and Naming](#test-file-location-and-naming)
18	  - [Mock Infrastructure](#mock-infrastructure)
19	  - [Service Testing Pattern](#service-testing-pattern)
20	  - [Route/Handler Testing Pattern](#routehandler-testing-pattern)
21	  - [Testing Error Paths](#testing-error-paths)
22	  - [Testing Time-Dependent Code](#testing-time-dependent-code)
23	  - [Testing Compound Boolean Logic (MC/DC)](#testing-compound-boolean-logic-mcdc)
24	  - [Using and Creating Mock Factories](#using-and-creating-mock-factories)
25	- [Test Boundaries](#test-boundaries)
26	  - [Unit Tests](#unit-tests)
27	  - [Integration Tests](#integration-tests)
28	  - [End-to-End Tests](#end-to-end-tests)
29	- [Type Safety in Tests](#type-safety-in-tests)
30	- [Coverage Configuration](#coverage-configuration)
31	- [Anti-Patterns](#anti-patterns)
32	- [CI Integration](#ci-integration)
33	- [Checklist: Before Submitting Tests](#checklist-before-submitting-tests)
34	- [Commands](#commands)
35	
36	---
37	
38	## Philosophy
39	
40	### Why 100% Coverage?
41	
42	We enforce 100% statement, branch, function, and line coverage. Thresholds are defined in `.coverage-thresholds.json` at the repo root (portable across projects). Orchestrator agents **must** read that file and run `npm run test:coverage` before marking any task complete. Here is why:
43	
44	1. **Coverage is a floor, not a ceiling.** 100% coverage does not mean the code is correct -- but anything less than 100% _guarantees_ untested code paths. Untested code is code we do not understand.
45	
46	2. **It forces design decisions.** If code is hard to test, it is usually hard to maintain. The coverage requirement pushes toward constructor DI, pure functions, and clean separation of concerns. Code that is easy to test is easy to reason about.
47	
48	3. **It eliminates the "that line doesn't matter" excuse.** Every `if` branch, every error handler, every fallback exists because someone thought it was necessary. If it is worth writing, it is worth testing. If it is not worth testing, delete it.
49	
50	4. **It catches regressions in edge cases.** The bugs that reach production live in the code paths nobody thought to test -- the error handler that silently swallows, the fallback that returns stale data, the branch that was "obvious."
51	
52	Files that are genuinely untestable in isolation (generated code, server bootstrap, UI primitives) should be excluded in your test configuration. Everything else gets covered.
53	
54	### Why TDD?
55	
56	We require Test-Driven Development (Red-Green-Refactor) for all new services:
57	
58	1. **RED**: Write a failing test that describes desired behavior.
59	2. **GREEN**: Write the minimum code to make it pass.
60	3. **REFACTOR**: Improve code while keeping tests green.
61	
62	TDD is not bureaucracy -- it is a design tool. Writing the test first forces you to think about the interface before the implementation. It ensures every line of code exists to satisfy a requirement, not a guess.
63	
64	### Tests Must Test Results, Not Presence
65	
66	The most important rule: **every assertion must verify that the code produces the correct result, not merely that something exists or was called.**
67	
68	```typescript
69	// BAD -- tests presence, not correctness
70	expect(result).toBeDefined();
71	expect(mockFn).toHaveBeenCalled();
72	expect(output.length).toBeGreaterThan(0);
73	
74	// GOOD -- tests actual results
75	expect(result).toEqual({ id: "item_123", status: "active" });
76	expect(mockFn).toHaveBeenCalledWith({
77	  id: "item_123",
78	  amount: 500,
79	  type: "GRANT",
80	});
81	expect(output).toEqual(["item_abc", "item_def"]);
82	```
83	
84	A test that passes when the code returns the wrong value is worse than no test at all -- it gives false confidence.
85	
86	### Never Lobotomize a Test
87	
88	When a test fails, the fix is **never** to weaken the assertion. Fix the code or fix the mock infrastructure. The short version:
89	
90	- Never replace specific matchers with `expect.any()`
91	- Never comment out or remove assertions
92	- Never use `.skip()` or `.todo()` to silence failures
93	- Never reduce assertion specificity to make a test pass
94	
95	If fixing one test breaks another, you are removing functionality, not fixing a bug. Stop and investigate the root cause.
96	
97	### Dependency Injection Is Non-Negotiable
98	
99	Every service takes its dependencies through the constructor. This is not just for testability -- it is for clarity. When you read a constructor, you see exactly what the service depends on. When you write a test, you control exactly what it receives.
100	
101	```typescript
102	// Production
103	const service = new OrderService(database, paymentClient, logger);
104	
105	// Test
106	const service = new OrderService(mockDatabase, mockPaymentClient, mockLogger);
107	```
108	
109	No global imports, no module-level singletons, no hidden dependencies. If a service needs something, it asks for it in the constructor.
110	
111	### No Live Services in Tests
112	
113	Unit and integration tests must **never** make real API calls. All external services (databases, third-party APIs, message queues, caches) are mocked. This ensures tests are:
114	
115	- **Fast**: Milliseconds, not seconds
116	- **Deterministic**: Same input produces same output, every time
117	- **Free**: No API costs from test runs
118	- **Independent**: No network, no credentials, no external state
119	
120	---
121	
122	## Practical Patterns
123	
124	### Test File Location and Naming
125	
126	```text
127	src/services/order.service.ts
128	src/services/__tests__/order.service.test.ts
129	
130	src/api/routes/webhooks/payment.ts
131	src/api/routes/webhooks/__tests__/payment.test.ts
132	
133	scripts/process-queue.ts
134	scripts/__tests__/process-queue.test.ts
135	```
136	
137	Conventions:
138	
139	- Test files use `.test.ts` (or `.test.tsx` for components) and live in a `__tests__/` directory adjacent to the code they test.
140	- Mirror the source file name: `foo.service.ts` -> `foo.service.test.ts`
141	- One test file per source file as a baseline; split into multiple files only when a single test file exceeds ~300 lines.
142	
143	### Mock Infrastructure
144	
145	#### Database Mock
146	
147	```typescript
148	import { createMockDatabaseClient } from "@/test-utils/mocks/database";
149	
150	const mockDb = createMockDatabaseClient();
151	// Provides mock stubs for all models/tables your ORM exposes
152	```
153	
154	#### ID Factories
155	
156	```typescript
157	import { testId } from "@/test-utils/factories/ids";
158	
159	const orderId = testId("order", "abc"); // "order_test_abc"
160	const userId = testId("user", "def"); // "user_test_def"
161	```
162	
163	#### Finding Existing Mocks
164	
165	Before creating a new mock, check:
166	
167	1. `src/test-utils/mocks/` -- Shared mock factories
168	2. `src/test-utils/factories/` -- Shared data factories
169	3. Existing `__tests__/` directories near the code you are testing -- look for local mock patterns
170	4. Your project's service inventory documentation
171	
172	### Service Testing Pattern
173	
174	```typescript
175	import { describe, it, expect, vi, beforeEach } from "vitest";
176	import { createMockDatabaseClient } from "@/test-utils/mocks/database";
177	import { OrderService } from "../order.service";
178	
179	describe("OrderService", () => {
180	  let service: OrderService;
181	  let mockDb: ReturnType<typeof createMockDatabaseClient>;
182	  let mockPaymentClient: {
183	    charge: ReturnType<typeof vi.fn>;
184	    refund: ReturnType<typeof vi.fn>;
185	  };
186	  let mockLogger: {
187	    info: ReturnType<typeof vi.fn>;
188	    warn: ReturnType<typeof vi.fn>;
189	    error: ReturnType<typeof vi.fn>;
190	  };
191	
192	  beforeEach(() => {
193	    mockDb = createMockDatabaseClient();
194	    mockPaymentClient = {
195	      charge: vi.fn().mockResolvedValue({}),
196	      refund: vi.fn().mockResolvedValue({}),
197	    };
198	    mockLogger = { info: vi.fn(), warn: vi.fn(), error: vi.fn() };
199	
200	    service = new OrderService({
201	      db: mockDb,
202	      paymentClient: mockPaymentClient,
203	      logger: mockLogger,
204	    } as never);
205	  });
206	
207	  it("creates an order with correct total", async () => {
208	    mockDb.order.create.mockResolvedValue({
209	      id: "order_123",
210	      total: 2500,
211	      status: "PENDING",
212	    });
213	
214	    const result = await service.createOrder({
215	      items: [{ productId: "prod_1", quantity: 2, unitPrice: 1250 }],
216	    });
217	
218	    expect(result.total).toBe(2500);
219	    expect(result.status).toBe("PENDING");
220	    expect(mockDb.order.create).toHaveBeenCalledWith(
221	      expect.objectContaining({ total: 2500 })
222	    );
223	  });
224	});
225	```
226	
227	Key points:
228	
229	- `beforeEach` creates fresh mocks (no state leaks between tests)
230	- Constructor DI injects all dependencies
231	- Assertions verify **specific values**, not just "was called"
232	
233	### Route/Handler Testing Pattern
234	
235	```typescript
236	function buildApp(mockService: MockOrderService) {
237	  // Build your application/router with mock dependencies injected
238	  const app = createApp();
239	  app.route("/api/orders", createOrderRoutes(mockService));
240	  return app;
241	}
242	
243	it("returns 201 on successful order creation", async () => {
244	  const app = buildApp(mockService);
245	  const res = await app.request("/api/orders", {
246	    method: "POST",
247	    headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
248	    body: JSON.stringify({ items: [{ productId: "prod_1", quantity: 1 }] }),
249	  });
250	
251	  expect(res.status).toBe(201);
252	  const body = await res.json();
253	  expect(body).toEqual(
254	    expect.objectContaining({ id: expect.any(String), status: "PENDING" })
255	  );
256	});
257	```
258	
259	### Testing Error Paths
260	
261	Every `if` branch and `catch` block needs a test. Error paths are where production bugs hide.
262	
263	```typescript
264	it("throws NotFoundError when order does not exist", async () => {
265	  mockDb.order.findUnique.mockResolvedValue(null);
266	
267	  await expect(service.getOrder("order_nonexistent")).rejects.toThrow(
268	    NotFoundError
269	  );
270	});
271	
272	it("returns failed entry when single payment charge fails", async () => {
273	  mockPaymentClient.charge.mockRejectedValue(new Error("Gateway timeout"));
274	
275	  const result = await service.processPayments(["order_123"]);
276	
277	  expect(result.failed).toEqual([
278	    { orderId: "order_123", error: "Gateway timeout" },
279	  ]);
280	});
281	```
282	
283	### Testing Time-Dependent Code
284	
285	```typescript
286	import { vi, beforeEach, afterEach } from "vitest";
287	
288	beforeEach(() => {
289	  vi.useFakeTimers();
290	  vi.setSystemTime(new Date("2025-01-15T00:00:00Z"));
291	});
292	
293	afterEach(() => {
294	  vi.useRealTimers();
295	});
296	
297	it("calculates days remaining correctly", async () => {
298	  mockDb.subscription.findUnique.mockResolvedValue({
299	    expiresAt: new Date("2025-01-25T00:00:00Z"),
300	    status: "ACTIVE",
301	  });
302	
303	  const status = await service.getSubscriptionStatus("sub_123");
304	
305	  expect(status.daysRemaining).toBe(10);
306	  expect(status.isActive).toBe(true);
307	});
308	```
309	
310	### Testing Compound Boolean Logic (MC/DC)
311	
312	When a function contains a compound boolean expression (2+ conditions joined by `&&` or `||`), standard branch coverage only requires two tests: one where the whole expression is true, one where it is false. This means individual conditions can be wrong, missing, or using the wrong operator without any test noticing.
313	
314	**MC/DC (Modified Condition/Decision Coverage)** requires that each condition is proven to independently affect the outcome. For `n` conditions, you need `n + 1` tests minimum.
315	
316	#### When to Apply MC/DC
317	
318	Apply MC/DC to compound boolean expressions in:
319	
320	- **Authorization/RBAC guards** -- conditions that gate access to operations
321	- **Eligibility/business rules** -- status checks, feature flags, tier checks
322	- **Validation logic** -- multi-field input validation
323	- **State machine transitions** -- compound conditions that determine next state
324	
325	Do **not** apply MC/DC to simple single-condition checks. Branch coverage is sufficient for those.
326	
327	#### The Pattern: Baseline + Toggle Each Condition
328	
329	```typescript
330	describe("canPerformAction -- MC/DC", () => {
331	  // Baseline: all conditions true -> allowed
332	  it("allows when all conditions met", () => {
333	    const result = canPerformAction(
334	      createMockUser({ role: "ADMIN" }),
335	      createMockOrganization({ tier: "PRO", suspended: false })
336	    );
337	    expect(result).toBe(true);
338	  });
339	
340	  // Toggle role alone -> denied
341	  it("denies when non-admin (other conditions true)", () => {
342	    const result = canPerformAction(
343	      createMockUser({ role: "MEMBER" }),
344	      createMockOrganization({ tier: "PRO", suspended: false })
345	    );
346	    expect(result).toBe(false);
347	  });
348	
349	  // Toggle tier alone -> denied
350	  it("denies when free tier (other conditions true)", () => {
351	    const result = canPerformAction(
352	      createMockUser({ role: "ADMIN" }),
353	      createMockOrganization({ tier: "FREE", suspended: false })
354	    );
355	    expect(result).toBe(false);
356	  });
357	
358	  // Toggle suspended alone -> denied
359	  it("denies when suspended (other conditions true)", () => {
360	    const result = canPerformAction(
361	      createMockUser({ role: "ADMIN" }),
362	      createMockOrganization({ tier: "PRO", suspended: true })
363	    );
364	    expect(result).toBe(false);
365	  });
366	});
367	```
368	
369	What MC/DC catches that branch coverage misses:
370	
371	- Wrong operator: `&&` where `||` was intended (or vice versa)
372	- Missing condition: a guard deleted but branch coverage still passes
373	- Dead condition: a condition that can never be false due to upstream logic
374	- Masked condition: short-circuit evaluation prevents a condition from being evaluated
375	
376	### Using and Creating Mock Factories
377	
378	Check your project's existing factory infrastructure before creating new ones. Do not duplicate factories that already exist.
379	
380	```typescript
381	// GOOD -- import the shared factory
382	import { createMockOrganization } from "@/test-utils/factories";
383	const org = createMockOrganization({ tier: "ENTERPRISE" });
384	
385	// BAD -- re-creating a factory that already exists
386	function makeOrg(overrides = {}) {
387	  return { id: "org_1", ...overrides };
388	}
389	
390	// BAD -- inline object with incomplete fields
391	const org = { id: "org_1", name: "Test" }; // missing tier, status, dates, etc.
392	```
393	
394	**When to create a local helper**: Only for test-specific _combinations_ of shared factories:
395	
396	```typescript
397	// GOOD -- composes shared factory, does not replace it
398	function createTrialOrg() {
399	  return createMockOrganization({ tier: "FREE", trialEndsAt: futureDate });
400	}
401	```
402	
403	**When to create a new shared factory**: When you add a new model/entity. Add the factory to `src/test-utils/factories/`, export it from `index.ts`, and update your service inventory.
404	
405	Never construct mock objects inline with incomplete fields. Never duplicate a factory or record type that already exists.
406	
407	---
408	
409	## Test Boundaries
410	
411	### Unit Tests
412	
413	**Scope**: Single function, class, or module in isolation.
414	
415	**Characteristics**:
416	- All dependencies are mocked
417	- No I/O (no database, no network, no filesystem)
418	- Run in milliseconds
419	- Deterministic -- no randomness, no real clocks
420	
421	**When to use**: Business logic, utility functions, transformations, validations, domain objects.
422	
423	```typescript
424	describe("calculateDiscount", () => {
425	  it("applies 10% discount for orders over $100", () => {
426	    expect(calculateDiscount(15000)).toBe(1500); // cents
427	  });
428	
429	  it("applies no discount for orders under $100", () => {
430	    expect(calculateDiscount(9999)).toBe(0);
431	  });
432	});
433	```
434	
435	### Integration Tests
436	
437	**Scope**: Multiple components working together, typically at an I/O boundary.
438	
439	**Characteristics**:
440	- Test real interactions between components
441	- May use test doubles for external services (test containers, in-memory databases)
442	- Run in seconds
443	- Test the contract between your code and external systems
444	
445	**When to use**: Database queries, API routes, middleware chains, service compositions.
446	
447	```typescript
448	describe("OrderAPI (integration)", () => {
449	  let app: Application;
450	  let testDb: TestDatabase;
451	
452	  beforeAll(async () => {
453	    testDb = await createTestDatabase();
454	    app = buildApp({ db: testDb.client });
455	  });
456	
457	  afterAll(async () => {
458	    await testDb.cleanup();
459	  });
460	
461	  it("creates order and persists to database", async () => {
462	    const res = await app.request("/api/orders", {
463	      method: "POST",
464	      body: JSON.stringify({ items: [{ productId: "prod_1", quantity: 1 }] }),
465	    });
466	
467	    expect(res.status).toBe(201);
468	
469	    const order = await testDb.client.order.findFirst();
470	    expect(order).not.toBeNull();
471	    expect(order!.status).toBe("PENDING");
472	  });
473	});
474	```
475	
476	### End-to-End Tests
477	
478	**Scope**: The entire system from external input to external output.
479	
480	**Characteristics**:
481	- Test the system as a user would interact with it
482	- Use real (or near-real) infrastructure
483	- Run in seconds to minutes
484	- Validate complete user workflows
485	
486	**When to use**: Critical user journeys, smoke tests, deployment verification.
487	
488	**Guidelines**:
489	- Keep the E2E suite small and focused on high-value paths
490	- Use dedicated test environments
491	- Accept slightly lower reliability (network, timing) in exchange for higher confidence
492	- Never use E2E tests as a substitute for unit/integration tests
493	
494	---
495	
496	## Type Safety in Tests
497	
498	### Mock DI Wiring: Use `as never`
499	
500	When injecting mock deps into service constructors, use `as never`:
501	
502	```typescript
503	// CORRECT -- concise, honest about the cast
504	const service = new MyService(deps as never);
505	
506	// WRONG -- verbose, no additional safety over `as never`
507	const service = new MyService(
508	  mockDb as unknown as ConstructorParameters<typeof MyService>[0],
509	  mockClient as unknown as ConstructorParameters<typeof MyService>[1]
510	);
511	
512	// WRONG -- disables all type checking
513	const service = new MyService(deps as any);
514	```
515	
516	Why `as never` over `as unknown as X`: both bypass the type checker at the cast point. `as never` is honest -- it says "trust me, this is test wiring." The verbose form gives a false impression of safety while being equally unchecked.
517	
518	### Other Type Patterns
519	
520	- Use `ReturnType<typeof vi.fn>` for mock function types
521	- Use `Partial<Type>` for partial mock objects
522	- **Never** use `as any` in tests (or anywhere else)
523	
524	---
525	
526	## Coverage Configuration
527	
528	Coverage thresholds are defined in `.coverage-thresholds.json` at the repository root:
529	
530	```json
531	{
532	  "statements": 100,
533	  "branches": 100,
534	  "functions": 100,
535	  "lines": 100
536	}
537	```
538	
539	### Configuring Your Test Runner
540	
541	#### Vitest Example
542	
543	```typescript
544	// vitest.config.ts
545	import { defineConfig } from "vitest/config";
546	import thresholds from "./.coverage-thresholds.json";
547	
548	export default defineConfig({
549	  test: {
550	    coverage: {
551	      provider: "v8",
552	      thresholds: {
553	        statements: thresholds.statements,
554	        branches: thresholds.branches,
555	        functions: thresholds.functions,
556	        lines: thresholds.lines,
557	      },
558	      exclude: [
559	        "**/*.config.*",
560	        "**/test-utils/**",
561	        "**/generated/**",
562	        "**/node_modules/**",
563	      ],
564	    },
565	  },
566	});
567	```
568	
569	#### Jest Example
570	
571	```javascript
572	// jest.config.js
573	const thresholds = require("./.coverage-thresholds.json");
574	
575	module.exports = {
576	  coverageThreshold: {
577	    global: {
578	      statements: thresholds.statements,
579	      branches: thresholds.branches,
580	      functions: thresholds.functions,
581	      lines: thresholds.lines,
582	    },
583	  },
584	  collectCoverageFrom: [
585	    "src/**/*.{ts,tsx}",
586	    "!src/**/*.config.*",
587	    "!src/test-utils/**",
588	    "!src/generated/**",
589	  ],
590	};
591	```
592	
593	### Exclusion Policy
594	
595	Files excluded from coverage MUST be genuinely untestable in isolation:
596	
597	- Generated code (ORM clients, GraphQL types, route manifests)
598	- Server bootstrap/entry points
599	- Configuration files
600	- UI primitive re-exports (barrel files)
601	- Type-only files (`.d.ts`)
602	
603	Everything else gets covered. If you find yourself wanting to exclude a file, ask: "Can I refactor this to be testable?" The answer is almost always yes.
604	
605	---
606	
607	## Anti-Patterns
608	
609	### 1. Over-Mocking
610	
611	```typescript
612	// BAD -- mocks everything, tests nothing
613	it("processes order", async () => {
614	  mockService.processOrder.mockResolvedValue({ success: true });
615	  const result = await controller.handleOrder(mockRequest);
616	  expect(mockService.processOrder).toHaveBeenCalled();
617	});
618	```
619	
620	This test verifies that your mock was called. It does not verify that your code works. If you deleted the implementation, this test would still pass.
621	
622	### 2. Testing Implementation Details
623	
624	```typescript
625	// BAD -- tests internal method call order
626	expect(service.validate).toHaveBeenCalledBefore(service.save);
627	
628	// GOOD -- tests the observable result
629	expect(result.status).toBe("SAVED");
630	expect(result.validationErrors).toEqual([]);
631	```
632	
633	Test the contract (inputs and outputs), not the internal execution path. Implementation details change during refactoring; contracts do not.
634	
635	### 3. Shared Mutable State
636	
637	```typescript
638	// BAD -- tests depend on order
639	let counter = 0;
640	it("first test", () => {
641	  counter++;
642	  expect(counter).toBe(1);
643	});
644	it("second test", () => {
645	  expect(counter).toBe(1); // Fails if first test doesn't run
646	});
647	```
648	
649	Every test must set up its own world. Use `beforeEach` for fresh state. Never rely on test execution order.
650	
651	### 4. Flaky Tests
652	
653	Common causes:
654	
655	- **Real timers**: Use `vi.useFakeTimers()` instead of `setTimeout`
656	- **Non-deterministic data**: Use factories with fixed seeds, not `Math.random()`
657	- **Race conditions**: Await all promises; do not fire-and-forget
658	- **External dependencies**: Mock all I/O; no real network calls in unit tests
659	- **Date sensitivity**: Mock `Date.now()` and `new Date()`
660	
661	If a test fails intermittently, it is a bug. Fix it immediately -- do not re-run until it passes.
662	
663	### 5. Assertion-Free Tests
664	
665	```typescript
666	// BAD -- no assertion, test always passes
667	it("handles error", async () => {
668	  await service.handleError(new Error("boom"));
669	});
670	```
671	
672	Every test MUST contain at least one assertion. A test without assertions is not a test.
673	
674	### 6. Catch-and-Ignore in Tests
675	
676	```typescript
677	// BAD -- swallows the error, test passes incorrectly
678	it("rejects invalid input", async () => {
679	  try {
680	    await service.process(invalidInput);
681	  } catch {
682	    // test passes
683	  }
684	});
685	
686	// GOOD -- explicitly asserts the error
687	it("rejects invalid input", async () => {
688	  await expect(service.process(invalidInput)).rejects.toThrow(
689	    ValidationError
690	  );
691	});
692	```
693	
694	### 7. Snapshot Abuse
695	
696	Snapshots are for capturing complex output that is tedious to assert manually (rendered UI trees, serialized configs). They are NOT a substitute for specific assertions on business logic.
697	
698	```typescript
699	// BAD -- snapshot on business logic
700	expect(calculateTotal(items)).toMatchSnapshot();
701	
702	// GOOD -- specific assertion
703	expect(calculateTotal(items)).toBe(4250);
704	```
705	
706	---
707	
708	## CI Integration
709	
710	### Coverage Gate in CI
711	
712	Coverage must be enforced in CI as a required check. If coverage drops below threshold, the build fails.
713	
714	```yaml
715	# Example GitHub Actions workflow
716	name: CI
717	on: [push, pull_request]
718	
719	jobs:
720	  test:
721	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
722	    steps:
723	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
724	      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
725	        with:
726	          node-version: "20"
727	      - run: npm ci
728	      - run: npm run test:coverage
729	      # Coverage thresholds enforced by test runner config
730	      # Build fails automatically if thresholds are not met
731	```
732	
733	### Required CI Checks for PRs
734	
735	A PR should not be mergeable until:
736	
737	1. All tests pass
738	2. Coverage thresholds are met
739	3. Type checking passes
740	4. Linting passes with zero warnings
741	
742	Configure branch protection rules to enforce these checks.
743	
744	---
745	
746	## Checklist: Before Submitting Tests
747	
748	1. **Every assertion tests a result**, not just presence (`toBeDefined`, `toHaveBeenCalled` alone are insufficient)
749	2. **Every error path has a test** -- `catch` blocks, `if` guards, validation failures
750	3. **Every branch has a test** -- `if/else`, ternaries, `switch` cases, default values. For compound booleans: each condition independently tested via MC/DC
751	4. **Mock infrastructure is correct** -- mocks return the right shapes, not just `undefined`
752	5. **No weakened assertions** -- no `expect.any()` where specifics are possible
753	6. **No skipped tests** -- no `.skip()`, `.todo()`, or commented-out assertions
754	7. **Fresh state per test** -- `beforeEach` recreates mocks, no shared mutable state
755	8. **Tests run in isolation** -- order-independent, no reliance on prior test side effects
756	9. **Types are clean** -- no `as any`, proper use of `as never` for DI wiring
757	10. **Coverage is 100%** -- run `npm run test:coverage` and verify all thresholds
758	
759	---
760	
761	## Commands
762	
763	Adapt these commands to your project's package manager and test runner:
764	
765	| Command                            | Purpose                            |
766	| ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
767	| `npm test -- --run`                | Run all tests and exit             |
768	| `npm run test:coverage`            | Run with coverage enforcement      |
769	| `npx vitest run path/__tests__/`   | Run specific test directory        |
770	| `npx vitest run path/file.test.ts` | Run specific test file             |
771	| `npm test -- --watch`              | Watch mode during development      |
772	
773	---
774	
775	## See Also
776	
777	- [Coding Standards Guide](./coding-standards.md) -- TypeScript discipline and design patterns
778	- [Build Validation Guide](./build-validation.md) -- Pre-commit validation and CI workflows
779	- [Git Workflow Guide](./git-workflow.md) -- Commit and PR conventions
780

content.content:
1	# Git Workflow Guide
2	
3	This guide covers Git best practices, branch management, commit conventions, and PR workflows for projects using the metaswarm framework. It is framework-level guidance applicable to any codebase.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Table of Contents
8	
9	- [Current Branch Awareness](#current-branch-awareness)
10	- [Branch Naming Conventions](#branch-naming-conventions)

23	
24	## Current Branch Awareness
25	
26	**CRITICAL**: Always maintain awareness of your current branch to prevent wrong-branch operations.
27	
28	Before ANY git operation (add, commit, push, checkout), you MUST:
29	
30	```bash
31	# 1. Check current branch
32	CURRENT_BRANCH=$(git branch --show-current)
33	echo "Current branch: $CURRENT_BRANCH"
34	
35	# 2. Verify it matches your intention
36	# If working on a PR, ensure branch name matches the PR
37	
38	# 3. Check if PR exists for this branch
39	gh pr list --head "$CURRENT_BRANCH" --state open
40	```
41	
42	Common branch confusion scenarios to avoid:
43	
44	- Pushing to main instead of a feature branch
45	- Creating a PR from the wrong branch
46	- Committing fixes to an unrelated branch
47	- Losing track of context after multiple git operations
48	
49	### Detecting the Main Branch
50	
51	Not all repositories use `main`. Detect the default branch programmatically:
52	
53	```bash
54	# Get the default branch name
55	DEFAULT_BRANCH=$(git symbolic-ref refs/remotes/origin/HEAD 2>/dev/null | sed 's@^refs/remotes/origin/@@')
56	
57	# Fallback if not set
58	if [ -z "$DEFAULT_BRANCH" ]; then
59	  DEFAULT_BRANCH=$(git remote show origin | grep 'HEAD branch' | awk '{print $NF}')
60	fi
61	
62	echo "Default branch: $DEFAULT_BRANCH"
63	```
64	
65	### Checking Remote Tracking
66	
67	```bash
68	# Check if current branch tracks a remote
69	git rev-parse --abbrev-ref --symbolic-full-name @{u} 2>/dev/null || echo "No upstream set"
70	
71	# Set upstream when pushing for the first time
72	git push -u origin "$(git branch --show-current)"
73	```
74	
75	---
76	
77	## Branch Naming Conventions
78	
79	Use prefixed branch names that communicate intent:
80	
81	| Prefix      | Purpose                          | Example                          |
82	| ----------- | -------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
83	| `feature/`  | New functionality                | `feature/user-authentication`    |
84	| `feat/`     | Short form of feature            | `feat/add-search`                |
85	| `fix/`      | Bug fixes                        | `fix/null-reference-in-loader`   |
86	| `chore/`    | Maintenance, deps, config        | `chore/update-dependencies`      |
87	| `refactor/` | Code restructuring (no behavior change) | `refactor/extract-service` |
88	| `docs/`     | Documentation changes            | `docs/api-reference`             |
89	| `test/`     | Test additions or fixes          | `test/add-integration-tests`     |
90	| `ci/`       | CI/CD pipeline changes           | `ci/add-coverage-gate`           |
91	| `hotfix/`   | Urgent production fixes          | `hotfix/security-patch`          |
92	
93	Rules:
94	
95	- Use lowercase with hyphens (kebab-case)
96	- Keep names concise but descriptive
97	- Include issue numbers when applicable: `fix/issue-123-login-error`
98	- Never work directly on main/master
99	
100	---
101	
102	## Commit Guidelines
103	
104	### Conventional Commit Format
105	
106	Use the [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) specification:
107	
108	```
109	<type>(<optional scope>): <description>
110	
111	<optional body>
112	
113	<optional footer>
114	```
115	
116	Types:
117	
118	| Type         | When to Use                                  |
119	| ------------ | -------------------------------------------- |
120	| `feat`       | New feature                                  |
121	| `fix`        | Bug fix                                      |
122	| `chore`      | Maintenance (deps, config, build)            |
123	| `refactor`   | Code change that neither fixes nor adds      |
124	| `test`       | Adding or correcting tests                   |
125	| `docs`       | Documentation only                           |
126	| `style`      | Formatting, whitespace (no logic change)     |
127	| `perf`       | Performance improvement                      |
128	| `ci`         | CI/CD configuration changes                  |
129	| `revert`     | Reverting a previous commit                  |
130	
131	### AI Attribution
132	
133	When commits are generated with AI assistance:
134	
135	```
136	feat: add rate limiting middleware
137	
138	- Implement token bucket algorithm
139	- Add per-endpoint configuration
140	- Include bypass for health checks
141	
142	Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
143	```
144	
145	Guidelines:
146	
147	- **DO**: Include `Co-Authored-By` for attribution
148	- **DO**: Use conventional commit format
149	- **DO**: Group related changes into logical commits
150	- **DON'T**: Create commits without running validation first
151	- **DON'T**: Write vague messages like "fix stuff" or "updates"
152	
153	### Commit Message Quality
154	
155	The subject line should:
156	
157	- Be under 72 characters
158	- Use imperative mood ("add" not "added")
159	- Not end with a period
160	- Explain the "why" when not obvious from the diff
161	
162	The body (when included) should:
163	
164	- Be separated from the subject by a blank line
165	- Wrap at 72 characters
166	- Explain what changed and why
167	
168	---
169	
170	## Pre-Commit Verification
171	
172	Before ANY git operations:
173	
174	1. **Verify current branch**: `git branch --show-current`
175	2. **Check for uncommitted changes**: `git status`
176	3. **Review changes**: `git diff` (unstaged) or `git diff --cached` (staged)
177	4. **Run validation on modified files**:
178	
179	```bash
180	# Get list of modified TypeScript/JavaScript files
181	MODIFIED_FILES=$(git diff --name-only --diff-filter=ACM | grep -E '\.(ts|tsx|js|jsx)$')
182	
183	# Run linter
184	if [ -n "$MODIFIED_FILES" ]; then
185	  echo "$MODIFIED_FILES" | xargs npx eslint --max-warnings 0
186	fi
187	
188	# Run type checker
189	npx tsc --noEmit
190	
191	# Kill stale test processes before running tests
192	pkill -f vitest 2>/dev/null || true
193	pkill -f jest 2>/dev/null || true
194	
195	# Run tests if any test files were modified
196	if echo "$MODIFIED_FILES" | grep -q '\.test\.' || echo "$MODIFIED_FILES" | grep -q '\.spec\.'; then
197	  npm test -- --run  # Adjust for your test runner
198	fi
199	
200	# Run coverage check (all thresholds must be met before push)
201	npm run test:coverage  # Adjust for your package manager
202	```
203	
204	### Validation Order
205	
206	Always validate in this order (fail fast):
207	
208	1. **Type check** -- catches structural errors
209	2. **Lint** -- catches style and correctness issues
210	3. **Test** -- catches behavioral regressions
211	4. **Coverage** -- ensures no untested code paths
212	
213	---
214	
215	## PR Creation Workflow
216	
217	1. **Verify branch**: `git branch --show-current`
218	2. **Push branch**: `git push -u origin <branch-name>`
219	3. **Create PR with comprehensive description**:
220	
221	```bash
222	gh pr create --title "feat: clear description" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
223	## Summary
224	
225	Brief description of what this PR does and why.
226	
227	## Changes
228	
229	- Change 1
230	- Change 2
231	- Change 3
232	
233	## Testing
234	
235	- [ ] Unit tests added/updated
236	- [ ] Integration tests pass
237	- [ ] Coverage thresholds met
238	- [ ] Manual testing completed (if applicable)
239	
240	## Review Focus
241	
242	Areas that need careful review.
243	
244	## Related Issues
245	
246	Closes #123
247	EOF
248	)"
249	```
250	
251	4. Include in PR description:
252	   - Summary with context on the "why"
253	   - Detailed list of changes
254	   - Testing confirmation
255	   - Review focus areas
256	   - Related issue references
257	
258	---
259	
260	## PR Comment Monitoring
261	
262	### When to Check for Comments
263	
264	Check for PR comments at these key moments:
265	
266	1. **After completing significant work on an existing PR, before committing** -- "Before we commit, check for any new comments."
267	2. **After pushing updates to an existing PR** -- "After push, check for new feedback."
268	3. **Before switching away from a PR branch** -- "Before switching branches, check for pending feedback."
269	4. **Before starting new tasks** -- If you have open PRs, check for pending feedback first.
270	
271	### When This Does NOT Apply
272	
273	- Working on initial feature development before creating a PR
274	- Making changes on a branch that has not been pushed yet
275	- Working on a branch without an associated PR
276	
277	### Checking for Comments
278	
279	```bash
280	# List your open PRs
281	gh pr list --author @me --state open
282	
283	# Quick check for new comments
284	gh pr view <pr-number> --json comments,reviews | jq -r '.comments | length'
285	
286	# View all review comments
287	gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/<pr-number>/comments
288	
289	# Check inline code comments
290	gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/<pr-number>/comments \
291	  | jq -r '.[] | "\(.path):\(.line) - \(.body)"'
292	```
293	
294	### Resolving Comments
295	
296	- Only check and address comments on PRs that you authored
297	- Never modify other people's PRs unless explicitly asked
298	- When addressing feedback: fix the code, push, and reply to the comment
299	- Resolve threads only after the feedback has been addressed
300	
301	---
302	
303	## Multi-File Commit Strategy
304	
305	### Strategy 1: Single Comprehensive Commit
306	
307	Best for: Related changes across multiple files.
308	
309	```bash
310	git add src/services/ src/utils/
311	git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
312	feat: implement rate limiting
313	
314	- Add token bucket service
315	- Add rate limit middleware
316	- Add configuration utilities
317	
318	Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
319	EOF
320	)"
321	```
322	
323	### Strategy 2: Logical Commit Grouping
324	
325	Best for: Large features with distinct components.
326	
327	```bash
328	# Core logic
329	git add src/services/ src/lib/
330	git commit -m "feat(core): add rate limiting service"
331	
332	# API integration
333	git add src/api/ src/middleware/
334	git commit -m "feat(api): integrate rate limiting middleware"
335	
336	# Tests
337	git add **/*.test.ts
338	git commit -m "test: add rate limiting tests"
339	```
340	
341	### Strategy 3: File-by-File Commits
342	
343	Best for: Unrelated changes or debugging.
344	
345	```bash
346	git add src/services/auth.ts
347	git commit -m "fix: resolve token validation edge case"
348	
349	git add src/utils/logger.ts
350	git commit -m "refactor: improve log formatting"
351	```
352	
353	---
354	
355	## Pipeline Orchestration Pattern
356	
357	When multiple agents work in parallel (e.g., across worktrees), use a **pipeline pattern** instead of batching.
358	
359	### Core Rule: Pipeline, Don't Batch
360	
361	As soon as an agent finishes, immediately push, create a PR, and launch a shepherd. Do not wait for other agents to complete.
362	
363	```text
364	Timeline (correct - pipeline):
365	  Agent A finishes -> push + PR + shepherd (immediately)
366	  Agent B still running...
367	  Agent C finishes -> push + PR + shepherd (immediately)
368	  Agent A's PR merges (shepherd handled CI + reviews)
369	  Agent B finishes -> push + PR + shepherd (immediately)
370	
371	Timeline (wrong - batched):
372	  Wait for A, B, C to all finish -> push all -> create all PRs -> shepherd all
373	```
374	
375	### Per-Agent Timing
376	
377	Track timing for each agent to identify bottlenecks:
378	
379	| Metric              | Calculation                | What It Reveals               |
380	| ------------------- | -------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
381	| Implementation time | impl-done - impl-start     | Code complexity / agent speed |
382	| PR turnaround       | pr-merged - pr-created     | CI + review bottlenecks       |
383	| Shepherd overhead   | pr-merged - shepherd-start | Review/CI iteration cost      |
384	| Pipeline gap        | pr-created - impl-done     | Orchestration latency         |
385	| Total cycle time    | pr-merged - impl-start     | End-to-end per story          |
386	
387	**Pipeline gap should be near zero.** If it is consistently high, the orchestrator is batching instead of pipelining.
388	
389	---
390	
391	## Tag and Release Conventions
392	
393	### Semantic Versioning
394	
395	Use [SemVer](https://semver.org/) for releases: `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`
396	
397	- **MAJOR**: Breaking changes
398	- **MINOR**: New features (backward compatible)
399	- **PATCH**: Bug fixes (backward compatible)
400	
401	### Tagging Releases
402	
403	```bash
404	# Create annotated tag
405	git tag -a v1.2.0 -m "Release v1.2.0: add rate limiting and improve auth"
406	
407	# Push tags to remote
408	git push origin v1.2.0
409	
410	# Push all tags
411	git push origin --tags
412	
413	# List tags
414	git tag -l "v1.*"
415	```
416	
417	### Release Workflow
418	
419	```bash
420	# Create release on GitHub with auto-generated notes
421	gh release create v1.2.0 --generate-notes --title "v1.2.0"
422	
423	# Create pre-release
424	gh release create v1.2.0-rc.1 --prerelease --title "v1.2.0 Release Candidate 1"
425	```
426	
427	---
428	
429	## Conflict Resolution
430	
431	### Prevention
432	
433	- Rebase feature branches on main regularly
434	- Keep PRs small and focused
435	- Communicate when modifying shared interfaces or contracts
436	- Use feature flags for long-running work
437	
438	### Resolution Process
439	
440	```bash
441	# Update your branch with latest main
442	git fetch origin
443	git rebase origin/main
444	
445	# If conflicts occur during rebase:
446	# 1. Resolve conflicts in each file
447	# 2. Stage resolved files
448	git add <resolved-files>
449	
450	# 3. Continue rebase
451	git rebase --continue
452	
453	# If the rebase becomes too complex, abort and try merge instead
454	git rebase --abort
455	git merge origin/main
456	```
457	
458	### Conflict Resolution Rules
459	
460	- Never blindly accept "ours" or "theirs" -- understand the intent of both changes
461	- If the conflict involves code you did not write, consult the author
462	- After resolving, run the full validation suite to ensure nothing broke
463	- For worktree conflicts across parallel agents, see the [Worktree Development Guide](./worktree-development.md)
464

466	
467	## Common Git Commands
468	
469	### Working with Branches
470	
471	```bash
472	# Create and switch to new branch
473	git checkout -b feature/branch-name
474	
475	# List all branches
476	git branch -a
477	
478	# Delete local branch
479	git branch -d branch-name
480	
481	# Update branch from main
482	git fetch origin
483	git rebase origin/main
484	```
485	
486	### Stashing Changes
487	
488	```bash
489	# Save current changes
490	git stash
491	
492	# List stashes
493	git stash list
494	
495	# Apply most recent stash
496	git stash pop
497	
498	# Apply specific stash
499	git stash apply stash@{2}
500	```
501	
502	### Undoing Changes
503	
504	```bash
505	# Undo last commit (keep changes staged)
506	git reset --soft HEAD~1
507	
508	# Undo last commit (keep changes unstaged)
509	git reset HEAD~1
510	
511	# Amend last commit message
512	git commit --amend
513	```
514	
515	---
516	
517	## Best Practices
518	
519	1. **Commit frequently**: Make small, logical commits that each represent one coherent change.
520	2. **Write clear messages**: Use conventional commit format. Future you (and your agents) will thank you.
521	3. **Review before push**: Always review `git diff` before pushing. Catch mistakes early.
522	4. **Keep branches updated**: Regularly sync with the default branch to minimize conflicts.
523	5. **Clean up**: Delete merged branches locally and remotely.
524	6. **Never force-push to shared branches**: Force-pushing to main or shared branches destroys history for others.
525	7. **Use PRs for all changes**: Even small fixes benefit from the review workflow.

23	
24	## Command Timeout Guidelines
25	
26	**IMPORTANT**: Claude Code has a default 2-minute timeout for Bash commands. For long-running commands, always specify extended timeouts using the `timeout` parameter in milliseconds.
27	
28	### Recommended Timeouts
29	
30	| Command                      | Timeout (ms) | Duration   | Usage                         |
31	| ---------------------------- | ------------ | ---------- | ----------------------------- |
32	| Full build                   | 300000       | 5 minutes  | Complete build with typechecking |
33	| Test suite                   | 240000       | 4 minutes  | Full test execution           |
34	| Test coverage                | 300000       | 5 minutes  | Tests + coverage report       |
35	| Type checking                | 120000       | 2 minutes  | TypeScript compilation check  |
36	| Linting                      | 120000       | 2 minutes  | Full ESLint pass              |
37	| Database migrations          | 180000       | 3 minutes  | Large schema changes          |
38	| Container builds             | 600000       | 10 minutes | Docker image builds           |
39	| Large file processing        | 300000       | 5 minutes  | Bulk data operations          |
40	
41	**Note**: Maximum timeout is 600000ms (10 minutes). For operations longer than 10 minutes, break them into smaller steps.
42	
43	---
44	
45	## Build Process Overview
46	
47	A standard build pipeline for a TypeScript project includes these stages, executed in order:
48	
49	```text
50	1. Type Check     (tsc --noEmit)         -- Structural correctness
51	2. Lint           (eslint)                -- Style and correctness rules
52	3. Test           (vitest/jest --run)     -- Behavioral correctness
53	4. Coverage       (test:coverage)         -- Coverage threshold enforcement
54	5. Build          (bundler build)         -- Production artifact generation
55	```
56	
57	### Validation Order (Fail Fast)
58	
59	Always validate in this order to fail fast on the cheapest checks:
60	
61	1. **Type check** -- catches structural errors in seconds
62	2. **Lint** -- catches style and correctness issues
63	3. **Test** -- catches behavioral regressions
64	4. **Coverage** -- ensures no untested code paths
65	5. **Build** -- generates production artifacts
66	
67	If any step fails, stop and fix before proceeding. Do not skip steps.
68	
69	---
70	
71	## TypeScript Checking Strategies
72	
73	### Quick Check Commands
74	
75	| Approach            | Time     | Purpose                              |
76	| ------------------- | -------- | ------------------------------------ |
77	| `tsc --noEmit`      | 5-15s    | Check TypeScript errors only         |
78	| Watch mode          | Instant  | Continuous monitoring during dev     |
79	| Lint changed files  | 1-3s     | Lint only modified files             |
80	| Parallel check      | 15-20s   | Lint + typecheck + tests in parallel |
81	| Full build          | 1-5 min  | Complete production build            |
82	
83	### When to Use Each
84	
85	1. **During development**: Use watch mode for instant feedback
86	   ```bash
87	   npx tsc --noEmit --watch
88	   ```
89	
90	2. **Before committing**: Quick typecheck + lint changed files
91	   ```bash
92	   npx tsc --noEmit && npx eslint $(git diff --name-only --diff-filter=ACM | grep -E '\.(ts|tsx)$')
93	   ```
94	
95	3. **Before creating a PR**: Full validation suite
96	   ```bash
97	   npx tsc --noEmit && npm run lint && npm run test:coverage && npm run build
98	   ```
99	
100	4. **Full production build**: Only when you need actual build artifacts
101	   ```bash
102	   npm run build  # Use 300000ms timeout
103	   ```
104	
105	### Incremental Builds
106	
107	For large projects, use TypeScript project references to enable incremental builds:
108	
109	```json
110	// tsconfig.json
111	{
112	  "compilerOptions": {
113	    "composite": true,
114	    "incremental": true,
115	    "tsBuildInfoFile": "./dist/.tsbuildinfo"
116	  }
117	}
118	```
119	
120	```bash
121	# Incremental build (only recompiles changed files)
122	npx tsc --build
123	
124	# Clean incremental cache
125	npx tsc --build --clean
126	```
127	
128	### Project References
129	
130	For monorepos or large projects, split into sub-projects:
131	
132	```json
133	// tsconfig.json (root)
134	{
135	  "references": [
136	    { "path": "./packages/core" },
137	    { "path": "./packages/api" },
138	    { "path": "./packages/web" }
139	  ]
140	}
141	```
142	
143	```bash
144	# Build all projects in dependency order
145	npx tsc --build --verbose
146	```
147	
148	---
149	
150	## Critical Validation Workflow
151	
152	**Before marking ANY task complete**, you MUST validate your changes.
153	
154	### Incremental Checks (During Development)
155	
156	```bash
157	# 1. TypeScript
158	npx tsc --noEmit
159	
160	# 2. ESLint on modified files only
161	npx eslint $(git diff --name-only --diff-filter=ACM | grep -E '\.(ts|tsx|js|jsx)$')
162	
163	# 3. Prettier on modified files
164	npx prettier --check $(git diff --name-only --diff-filter=ACM | grep -E '\.(ts|tsx|js|jsx)$')
165	```
166	
167	### Full Validation (Before Completion)
168	
169	**IMPORTANT**: While incremental checks on modified files are useful during development, you MUST run the full validation suite before declaring any task complete:
170	
171	```bash
172	# MANDATORY before marking task complete:
173	npm run lint          # Full ESLint check -- must pass with 0 warnings
174	npx tsc --noEmit      # Full TypeScript check -- must pass with 0 errors
175	npm run test:coverage # Full test suite with coverage -- must meet thresholds
176	npm run build         # Full build -- must succeed with no errors
177	```
178	
179	**Why**: Your changes may affect other files indirectly through imports, type definitions, or shared utilities. Only a full validation ensures the entire codebase remains healthy.
180	
181	### Validation Sequence (Repeat Until ALL Pass)
182	
183	1. Fix all TypeScript errors: `npx tsc --noEmit`
184	2. Fix all ESLint warnings: `npm run lint`
185	3. Run tests: `npm test -- --run`
186	4. Verify coverage: `npm run test:coverage`
187	5. Verify build: `npm run build`
188	6. If ANY step fails, return to step 1
189	
190	**Error Continuation Rule**: If you encounter 10 errors, fix ALL 10, not just the first 3.
191	
192	**Success Criteria**: Only mark task complete when all commands return exit code 0 with zero errors and zero warnings.
193	
194	---
195	
196	## File Tracking for Targeted Validation
197	
198	Track all files you modify during a session for efficient validation:
199	
200	```bash
201	# Before starting work, check clean state
202	git status
203	
204	# During work, periodically check modifications
205	git status --porcelain
206	
207	# Before validation, get list of modified files
208	MODIFIED_FILES=$(git diff --name-only --diff-filter=ACM | grep -E '\.(ts|tsx|js|jsx)$')
209	
210	# Run validation only on your changes
211	if [ -n "$MODIFIED_FILES" ]; then
212	  echo "$MODIFIED_FILES" | xargs npx eslint --max-warnings 0
213	  echo "$MODIFIED_FILES" | xargs npx prettier --check
214	else
215	  echo "No modified JS/TS files detected -- skipping lint/format."
216	fi
217	```
218	
219	**Why targeted validation matters**:
220	
221	- Running linters on the entire codebase creates out-of-scope work
222	- Users expect changes only to files related to the task
223	- Targeted validation is faster and more focused
224	- Prevents introducing issues in unrelated code
225	
226	---
227	
228	## Coverage Enforcement
229	
230	Coverage thresholds are defined in `.coverage-thresholds.json` at the repo root:
231	
232	```json
233	{
234	  "statements": 100,
235	  "branches": 100,
236	  "functions": 100,
237	  "lines": 100
238	}
239	```
240	
241	Orchestrator agents **must** read that file and run `npm run test:coverage` before marking any task complete. If any threshold is not met, the task fails.
242	
243	For detailed coverage configuration, see the [Testing Patterns Guide](./testing-patterns.md#coverage-configuration).
244	
245	---
246	
247	## Build Error Resolution
248	
249	### DO NOT Stop Prematurely
250	
251	- If build errors exist, CONTINUE fixing them
252	- If tests are failing, CONTINUE debugging
253	- If ESLint has warnings, CONTINUE resolving them
254	- The task is NOT complete until ALL validation steps pass
255	
256	### Systematic Error Fixing Process
257	
258	1. **Identify ALL errors first**: Run full build and list every error
259	2. **Group errors by type**: TypeScript, ESLint, test failures
260	3. **Fix errors systematically**: Start with TypeScript (often cascading), then ESLint, then tests
261	4. **Verify each fix**: Re-run the relevant check after each batch of fixes
262	5. **Never skip errors**: Every single error must be resolved
263	
264	### Common Error Patterns
265	
266	When encountering build errors:
267	
268	- **NEVER use `as any` or `@ts-ignore`** -- these mask problems instead of solving them
269	- Do not stop at the first error -- use `npx tsc --noEmit 2>&1 | head -100` to see all errors
270	- After fixing errors, always re-run the check to verify
271	
272	Common TypeScript patterns to remember:
273	
274	- Use proper null checks instead of non-null assertion (`!`)
275	- Extract nullable values before comparisons
276	- Create proper interfaces instead of using `any`
277	- Check property paths carefully in nested objects
278	- Use discriminated unions for complex type narrowing
279	
280	### Build Failure Triage
281	
282	| Error Type             | First Action                                    | Common Cause                         |
283	| ---------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
284	| TypeScript errors      | `npx tsc --noEmit`                              | Type mismatches, missing properties  |
285	| ESLint errors          | `npx eslint <file> --max-warnings 0`            | Unused imports, style violations     |
286	| Test failures          | `npx vitest run <file>` (or jest)               | Logic bugs, stale mocks             |
287	| Coverage drops         | `npm run test:coverage`                         | Untested branches or new code        |
288	| Build/bundle errors    | `npm run build`                                 | Import resolution, missing exports   |
289	| Missing dependencies   | `npm install`                                   | Package not installed                |
290
