292	
293	## CI/CD Pipeline Configuration
294	
295	### GitHub Actions Example
296	
297	```yaml
298	name: CI
299	on:
300	  push:
301	    branches: [main]
302	  pull_request:
303	    branches: [main]
304	
305	jobs:
306	  validate:
307	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
308	    steps:
309	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
310	
311	      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
312	        with:
313	          node-version: "20"
314	          cache: "npm"
315	
316	      - name: Install dependencies
317	        run: npm ci
318	
319	      - name: Type check
320	        run: npx tsc --noEmit
321	
322	      - name: Lint
323	        run: npm run lint
324	
325	      - name: Test with coverage
326	        run: npm run test:coverage
327	
328	      - name: Build
329	        run: npm run build
330	```
331	
332	### Branch Protection Rules
333	
334	Configure these as required status checks:
335	
336	- Type checking must pass
337	- Linting must pass with zero warnings
338	- All tests must pass
339	- Coverage thresholds must be met
340	- Build must succeed
341	
342	### Caching for Faster CI
343	
344	```yaml
345	- uses: actions/cache@v4
346	  with:
347	    path: |
348	      node_modules
349	      ~/.npm
350	      dist/.tsbuildinfo
351	    key: ${{ runner.os }}-node-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}
352	    restore-keys: |
353	      ${{ runner.os }}-node-
354	```
355	
356	---
357	
358	## Production vs Development Builds
359	
360	### Overview
361	
362	| Environment           | Type Checking | Optimization | Use Case                     |
363	| --------------------- | ------------- | ------------ | ---------------------------- |
364	| **Local Development** | Full          | None         | Active development           |
365	| **CI/CD**             | Full          | Full         | Pre-deployment validation    |
366	| **Production Deploy** | Skipped       | Full         | Fast deploys after CI passed |
367	
368	### Why Skip Type Checking in Production?
369	
370	Type checking in production builds is redundant when CI/CD has already validated types. Skipping it saves 30-60 seconds per deployment.
371	
372	```bash
373	# Development/CI: full validation
374	npm run build
375	
376	# Production: skip type checking (CI already validated)
377	SKIP_TYPE_CHECK=true npm run build
378	```
379	
380	### When NOT to Skip Type Checking
381	
382	- Local development builds
383	- CI/CD pipeline builds
384	- Before creating PRs
385	- When testing type changes
386	
387	---
388	
389	## Pre-Push Hooks
390	
391	Use git hooks to prevent pushing broken code:
392	
393	### Using Husky
394	
395	```bash
396	npm install --save-dev husky
397	npx husky init
398	```
399	
400	### Pre-Push Hook
401	
402	```bash
403	#!/usr/bin/env bash
404	# .husky/pre-push
405	
406	echo "Running pre-push validation..."
407	
408	# Type check
409	npx tsc --noEmit || {
410	  echo "TypeScript check failed. Push aborted."
411	  exit 1
412	}
413	
414	# Lint
415	npm run lint || {
416	  echo "Lint check failed. Push aborted."
417	  exit 1
418	}
419	
420	# Tests
421	npm test -- --run || {
422	  echo "Tests failed. Push aborted."
423	  exit 1
424	}
425	
426	echo "All checks passed. Pushing..."
427	```
428	
429	### Pre-Commit Hook (Staged Files Only)
430	
431	```bash
432	#!/usr/bin/env bash
433	# .husky/pre-commit
434	
435	STAGED_FILES=$(git diff --cached --name-only --diff-filter=ACM | grep -E '\.(ts|tsx|js|jsx)$')
436	
437	if [ -n "$STAGED_FILES" ]; then
438	  echo "$STAGED_FILES" | xargs npx eslint --max-warnings 0 || {
439	    echo "Lint errors in staged files. Commit aborted."
440	    exit 1
441	  }
442	fi
443	```
444	
445	---
446	
447	## Build Performance Optimization
448	
449	### General Strategies
450	
451	1. **Incremental TypeScript builds**: Use `--incremental` and project references
452	2. **Parallel execution**: Run independent checks in parallel
453	3. **Targeted validation**: Check only modified files during development
454	4. **Caching**: Use build caches (`.tsbuildinfo`, bundler caches, CI caches)
455	5. **Watch mode**: Use watch mode during active development instead of repeated full builds
456	
457	### Parallel Validation Script
458	
459	```bash
460	#!/usr/bin/env bash
461	# scripts/check-fast.sh -- Run validation checks in parallel
462	
463	set -euo pipefail
464	
465	echo "Starting parallel validation..."
466	
467	# Run checks in parallel
468	npx tsc --noEmit &
469	PID_TSC=$!
470	
471	npm run lint &
472	PID_LINT=$!
473	
474	npm test -- --run &
475	PID_TEST=$!
476	
477	# Wait for all and capture exit codes
478	FAIL=0
479	wait $PID_TSC || { echo "TypeScript check FAILED"; FAIL=1; }
480	wait $PID_LINT || { echo "Lint check FAILED"; FAIL=1; }
481	wait $PID_TEST || { echo "Tests FAILED"; FAIL=1; }
482	
483	if [ $FAIL -ne 0 ]; then
484	  echo "Validation FAILED"
485	  exit 1
486	fi
487	
488	echo "All checks passed."
489	```
490	
491	### Large Project Strategies
492	
493	For projects with slow builds:
494	
495	- **Split into packages**: Use workspaces/project references so only changed packages rebuild
496	- **Use `swc` or `esbuild`**: Faster transpilation for development builds (keep `tsc` for type checking)
497	- **Prune test suites**: Run only tests related to changed files during development
498	  ```bash
499	  npx vitest --changed  # Run tests related to uncommitted changes
500	  ```
501	- **Parallelize CI**: Split test suites across multiple CI runners
502

504	
505	## Container Build Patterns
506	
507	### Multi-Stage Dockerfile
508	
509	```dockerfile
510	# Stage 1: Install dependencies
511	FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
512	WORKDIR /app
513	COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
514	RUN npm ci --production=false
515	
516	# Stage 2: Build
517	FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
518	WORKDIR /app
519	COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
520	COPY . .
521	RUN npm run build
522	
523	# Stage 3: Production image
524	FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
525	WORKDIR /app
526	ENV NODE_ENV=production
527	
528	COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
529	COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules
530	COPY package.json ./
531	
532	USER node
533	CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"]
534	```
535	
536	### Build Cache Optimization
537	
538	```dockerfile
539	# Copy package files first (rarely change) for better layer caching
540	COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
541	RUN npm ci
542	
543	# Then copy source (frequently changes)
544	COPY . .
545	RUN npm run build
546	```
547	
548	### Health Checks
549	
550	```dockerfile
551	HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=5s --retries=3 \
552	  CMD node -e "require('http').get('http://localhost:${PORT}/health', (r) => { process.exit(r.statusCode === 200 ? 0 : 1); })"
553	```

1	# Worktree Development Guide
2	
3	This guide covers git worktree usage patterns for parallel development in projects using the metaswarm framework. Worktrees enable multiple agents (or developers) to work on different features simultaneously without interfering with each other.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Table of Contents
8	
9	- [When to Use Worktrees](#when-to-use-worktrees)
10	- [Worktree Setup](#worktree-setup)
11	- [Environment Isolation](#environment-isolation)
12	- [Workflow Patterns](#workflow-patterns)
13	- [Hub-and-Spoke Pattern](#hub-and-spoke-pattern)
14	- [Agent Full-Lifecycle Ownership](#agent-full-lifecycle-ownership)
15	- [Layered Merge Pipeline](#layered-merge-pipeline)
16	- [Handoff Documents](#handoff-documents)
17	- [Conflict Detection and Resolution](#conflict-detection-and-resolution)
18	- [Worktree Cleanup and Maintenance](#worktree-cleanup-and-maintenance)
19	- [Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting)
20	- [Quick Reference](#quick-reference)
21	
22	---
23	
24	## When to Use Worktrees
25	
26	### Good Use Cases
27	
28	| Scenario                               | Why Worktrees Help                                            |
29	| -------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
30	| **Multiple PRs in review**             | Work on new features while PRs await review                   |
31	| **Parallel feature development**       | Multiple agents work on different features simultaneously     |
32	| **Testing against different branches** | Compare behavior across branches without stashing             |
33	| **Long-running tasks**                 | Do not block other work while waiting for builds/tests        |
34	| **Code review reference**              | Keep PR code open while working on something else             |
35	
36	### When NOT to Use Worktrees
37	
38	- **Simple, quick tasks** -- Overhead is not worth it for 5-minute fixes
39	- **Tight integration work** -- When changes need to see each other immediately
40	- **Schema migrations** -- Shared databases require careful coordination
41	- **Single-branch workflow** -- If you are only working on one thing
42	
43	---
44	
45	## Worktree Setup
46	
47	### Creating a Worktree
48	
49	From the **main repository** (not from another worktree):
50	
51	```bash
52	# Create a worktree with a new branch
53	git worktree add ~/Developer/<project>-worktrees/<agent-name> -b feature/<feature-name>
54	
55	# Create a worktree from an existing branch
56	git worktree add ~/Developer/<project>-worktrees/<agent-name> feature/<existing-branch>
57	
58	# Create a worktree from a specific commit (detached HEAD)
59	git worktree add ~/Developer/<project>-worktrees/<agent-name> <commit-sha>
60	```
61	
62	### Directory Structure
63	
64	Establish a consistent directory layout:
65	
66	```text
67	~/Developer/
68	+-- my-project/                        # Main repository (hub)
69	|   +-- .claude/
70	|   |   +-- handoffs/                  # Handoff documents between agents
71	|   +-- guides/                        # Project guides
72	|   +-- src/                           # Source code
73	+-- my-project-worktrees/             # All worktrees live here
74	    +-- agent-1/                       # Worktree for agent 1
75	    |   +-- .claude/
76	    |   |   +-- handoffs/
77	    |   +-- src/
78	    +-- agent-2/                       # Worktree for agent 2
79	    +-- hotfix/                        # Worktree for hotfix
80	```
81	
82	Rules:
83	
84	- All worktrees live in a sibling directory named `<project>-worktrees/`
85	- Each worktree gets a descriptive name (agent name, feature name, or purpose)
86	- The main repository is always the orchestration hub
87	
88	### Post-Setup
89	
90	After creating a worktree, install dependencies:
91	
92	```bash
93	cd ~/Developer/<project>-worktrees/<agent-name>
94	npm install  # or pnpm install, yarn install
95	```
96	
97	---
98	
99	## Environment Isolation
100	
101	Each worktree must be isolated to prevent resource conflicts. Use environment variables or configuration to ensure separation:
102	
103	### Port Isolation
104	
105	Each worktree should use a unique port to avoid `EADDRINUSE` errors:
106	
107	| Resource       | Main Repo | Worktree 1 | Worktree 2 |
108	| -------------- | --------- | ----------- | ----------- |
109	| App Server     | Base port | Base + 1    | Base + 2    |
110	| Debug Port     | Base + 10 | Base + 11   | Base + 12   |
111	| Dev Server     | Base + 20 | Base + 21   | Base + 22   |
112	
113	### Environment Variables
114	
115	Set these per-worktree to prevent collisions:
116	
117	```bash
118	# In each worktree's .env or shell environment
119	PORT=<unique-port>
120	CACHE_KEY_PREFIX="<worktree-name>:"
121	WORKTREE_ID="<worktree-name>"
122	DATABASE_URL="<shared-or-isolated-db-url>"
123	```
124	
125	### Automated Setup Script
126	
127	Create a setup script that configures isolation automatically:
128	
129	```bash
130	#!/usr/bin/env bash
131	# worktree-setup.sh <worktree-name> <branch-name>
132	set -euo pipefail
133	
134	WORKTREE_NAME="$1"
135	BRANCH_NAME="$2"
136	PROJECT_DIR="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)"
137	PROJECT_NAME="$(basename "$PROJECT_DIR")"
138	WORKTREE_BASE="$(dirname "$PROJECT_DIR")/${PROJECT_NAME}-worktrees"
139	WORKTREE_PATH="${WORKTREE_BASE}/${WORKTREE_NAME}"
140	
141	# Create worktree directory
142	mkdir -p "$WORKTREE_BASE"
143	git worktree add "$WORKTREE_PATH" -b "$BRANCH_NAME"
144	
145	# Install dependencies
146	cd "$WORKTREE_PATH"
147	npm install
148	
149	# Configure environment isolation
150	if [ -f .env ]; then
151	  # Calculate unique port offset from worktree name hash
152	  OFFSET=$(echo "$WORKTREE_NAME" | cksum | awk '{print ($1 % 100) + 1}')
153	  echo "" >> .env
154	  echo "# Worktree isolation" >> .env
155	  echo "WORKTREE_ID=${WORKTREE_NAME}" >> .env
156	  echo "CACHE_KEY_PREFIX=${WORKTREE_NAME}:" >> .env
157	fi
158	
159	echo "Worktree ready at: $WORKTREE_PATH"
160	```
161	
162	---
163	
164	## Workflow Patterns
165	
166	### Starting Work in a Worktree
167	
168	1. **Create the worktree** from the main repository:
169	
170	   ```bash
171	   git worktree add ~/Developer/<project>-worktrees/<name> -b feature/<feature>
172	   ```
173	
174	2. **Navigate and set up**:
175	
176	   ```bash
177	   cd ~/Developer/<project>-worktrees/<name>
178	   npm install
179	   ```
180	
181	3. **Start development**:
182	
183	   ```bash
184	   npm run dev
185	   ```
186	
187	### Referencing Code from Other Branches
188	
189	View files from other branches without switching context:
190	
191	```bash
192	# View file content from another branch
193	git show main:src/services/order.service.ts
194	
195	# Diff against main
196	git diff main -- src/services/
197	
198	# Diff between two branches
199	git diff feature/auth...feature/orders -- src/shared/
200	```
201	
202	### Coordinating Shared Resources
203	
204	**CRITICAL**: All worktrees share the same git repository and potentially the same database.
205	
206	Before any schema or migration change:
207	
208	1. Check active worktrees: `git worktree list`
209	2. Coordinate with other developers/agents
210	3. Run migrations from the main repository only
211	4. Other worktrees regenerate clients after migration
212	
213	---
214	
215	## Hub-and-Spoke Pattern
216	
217	The hub-and-spoke pattern is the recommended approach for complex features with parallel work streams.
218	
219	### Architecture
220	
221	```text
222	Main Repository (Hub / Orchestrator)
223	+-- Creates worktrees
224	+-- Spawns agents with Task tool (run_in_background: true)
225	+-- Continues other work while agents run
226	+-- Checks agent status periodically
227	+-- Merges completed features
228	+-- Coordinates shared resources (database, config)
229	
230	Worktree: feat-auth (Spoke / Worker Agent)
231	+-- Agent works in isolated directory
232	+-- Creates PR when implementation is done
233	+-- Monitors CI and handles failures
234	+-- Responds to review comments autonomously
235	+-- Reports back when PR is ready to merge OR blocked
236	
237	Worktree: feat-orders (Spoke / Worker Agent)
238	+-- Parallel agent works independently
239	+-- Owns its own PR lifecycle
240	+-- Handles CI failures and reviews
241	+-- Reports back when ready or blocked
242	```
243	
244	### Spawning Agents
245	
246	Instead of manually navigating to worktrees, orchestrate agents directly:
247	
248	```typescript
249	// From main repository, spawn a background agent
250	Task({
251	  description: "Implement feature X in worktree",
252	  subagent_type: "general-purpose",
253	  run_in_background: true,
254	  prompt: `
255	You are working in a git worktree at /path/to/<project>-worktrees/feat-x
256	on branch feature/x.
257	
258	IMPORTANT: Change to the worktree directory first:
259	cd /path/to/<project>-worktrees/feat-x
260	
261	Your task: [Detailed task description]
262	
263	After implementation:
264	1. Run validation (lint, typecheck, test, coverage)
265	2. Create PR
266	3. Monitor CI and handle failures autonomously
267	4. Respond to review comments
268	5. Report back when PR is ready to merge or if blocked
269	
270	Run autonomously - the orchestrator will check in periodically.
271	`,
272	});
273	```
274	
275	### Key Principles
276	
277	- **Agents own their PR lifecycle**: The orchestrator spawns and moves on, checking back periodically
278	- **Orchestrator remains free**: Not blocked polling CI for any single agent
279	- **Agents handle their own failures**: CI failures, review comments, merge conflicts
280	- **Parallel execution**: Multiple agents work simultaneously in separate worktrees
281	
282	---
283	
284	## Agent Full-Lifecycle Ownership
285	
286	Each agent owns the complete lifecycle of its story:
287	
288	```text
289	1. Implement (TDD, in worktree)
290	2. Run validation (lint, typecheck, test, coverage)
291	3. Commit changes
292	4. Push branch
293	5. Create PR (gh pr create)
294	6. Shepherd PR through CI
295	7. Address EVERY code review comment (fix or explain)
296	8. Resolve ALL review threads
297	9. Squash merge when all checks pass
298	```
299	
300	**The orchestrator does NOT batch these steps.** Each agent pipelines its own output through to merge independently.
301	
302	### Agent Self-Shepherding
303	
304	After creating a PR, the agent monitors and maintains it:
305	
306	```bash
307	# Create PR
308	git push -u origin feature/my-feature
309	PR_URL=$(gh pr create --title "feat: ..." --body "...")
310	PR_NUMBER=$(echo "$PR_URL" | grep -o '[0-9]*$')
311	
312	# Monitor CI status
313	gh pr checks "$PR_NUMBER" --watch
314	
315	# If CI fails, fix and push
316	# If reviews come in, address them
317	# When all checks pass and threads are resolved, report ready
318	```
319	
320	---
321	
322	## Layered Merge Pipeline
323	
324	When implementing multiple stories that have dependency chains, use a layered merge pipeline to ensure each layer builds on merged, validated code.
325	
326	### Core Principle
327	
328	**Never build dependent code on unmerged branches.** Stories in layer N+1 must only be implemented after layer N is squash-merged to the default branch. This prevents stub duplication and ensures agents import real services instead of recreating them.
329	
330	### Layer Classification
331	
332	```text
333	Layer 1: Stories with NO dependencies on other unmerged stories
334	Layer 2: Stories that depend on Layer 1 code
335	Layer 3: Stories that depend on Layer 2 code
336	...
337	```
338	
339	### Pipeline Pattern
340	
341	Pipeline each agent's output immediately -- do not wait for all agents to finish:
342	
343	```text
344	Timeline (correct):
345	  Agent A finishes -> push + PR + shepherd (immediately)
346	  Agent B finishes -> push + PR + shepherd (immediately)
347	  Agent C still running...
348	  Agent A's PR merges
349	  Agent C finishes -> push + PR + shepherd (immediately)
350	  Agent B's PR merges
351	  Agent C's PR merges
352	  -> Pull main, create layer N+1 worktrees, launch next wave
353	
354	Anti-pattern (wasteful):
355	  Wait for ALL agents -> batch push -> batch PR -> batch shepherd
356	```
357	
358	### Orchestration Steps
359	
360	1. **Identify layers**: Group stories by dependency. Independent stories = Layer 1.
361	2. **Create worktrees from current main**: `git worktree add <path> -b feat/<story> main`
362	3. **Launch agents in parallel** with explicit context about what services exist on main.
363	4. **As each agent completes**: Push, create PR, begin shepherding immediately.
364	5. **Shepherd agents handle CI and reviews** autonomously.
365	6. **Layer N+1 worktree creation can overlap**: While layer N shepherds are running, create N+1 worktrees for stories that have no dependency on unmerged layer N code. Implementation agents for N+1 still wait for the phase gate.
366	7. **PHASE GATE**: Do NOT start layer N+1 implementation until ALL layer N PRs are squash-merged to main. No exceptions.
367	8. **After all layer N PRs merge**: Pull main, clean up worktrees/branches, launch layer N+1 agents.
368	9. **Inform layer N+1 agents**: Enumerate every new service/utility now on main. Agents must `import` and use them, NOT recreate stubs.
369	
370	### Phase Gate Checklist
371	
372	Before starting any new layer, the orchestrator MUST verify:
373	
374	- [ ] ALL PRs from previous layer show state=MERGED
375	- [ ] `git pull origin main` reflects all merged commits
376	- [ ] No open PRs remain from the previous layer
377	- [ ] Task tracking for previous layer is closed
378	
379	```bash
380	# Verify all previous layer PRs are merged
381	for pr in <pr_numbers>; do
382	  state=$(gh pr view $pr --json state -q .state)
383	  if [ "$state" != "MERGED" ]; then
384	    echo "BLOCKED: PR #$pr is $state, not MERGED"
385	    exit 1
386	  fi
387	done
388	
389	# Pull latest main
390	git checkout main && git pull origin main
391	```
392	
393	### Informing Agents About Existing Code
394	
395	Each implementation agent prompt MUST include:
396	
397	```text
398	## CRITICAL: Services already on main -- DO NOT recreate these
399	- `src/services/auth.service.ts` -- AuthService (use for authentication)
400	- `src/lib/cache.ts` -- CacheManager (use for caching patterns)
401	- [... enumerate all relevant services ...]
402	
403	**You MUST import and use existing code. Do NOT create stubs or duplicates.**
404	```
405	
406	---
407	
408	## Handoff Documents
409	
410	When passing work between agents or sessions, use structured handoff documents.
411	
412	### Handoff Contract
413	
414	Every handoff must include:
415	
416	1. **What was accomplished** -- Completed tasks with specific outcomes
417	2. **What remains** -- Specific next steps (not vague goals)
418	3. **Key decisions** -- Why certain approaches were chosen
419	4. **Gotchas** -- Things the next agent should know
420	5. **Test status** -- Did tests pass? What coverage? What needs testing?
421	
422	### Handoff Document Template
423	
424	```markdown
425	# Handoff: <Feature/Task Name>
426	
427	## Date: <YYYY-MM-DD>
428	
429	## From: <source agent/session>
430	
431	## To: <target agent/session>
432	
433	## Completed
434	
435	- [x] Implemented OrderService with create/update/delete
436	- [x] Added unit tests (100% coverage)
437	- [x] Integrated with existing AuthService
438	
439	## Remaining
440	
441	- [ ] Add integration tests for API routes
442	- [ ] Update API documentation
443	- [ ] Add rate limiting to create endpoint
444	
445	## Key Decisions
446	
447	- Used repository pattern for data access (consistent with existing services)
448	- Chose optimistic locking for concurrent updates (discussed in Issue #45)
449	
450	**Tip:** Use `bd decision "<decision>: <rationale>"` to record decisions persistently in the beads database — these survive compaction and are available across sessions.
451	
452	## Gotchas
453	
454	- The OrderStatus enum is defined in shared types -- do NOT redefine it
455	- Database migrations have NOT been run yet -- run from main repo only
456	- The mock factory for Orders is in `src/test-utils/factories/order.ts`
457	
458	## Test Status
459	
460	- Unit tests: 47 passing, 100% coverage
461	- Integration tests: Not yet written
462	- Linting: Clean (0 warnings)
463	- Type check: Clean (0 errors)
464	```
465	
466	### Storage Location
467	
468	Store handoff documents in `.claude/handoffs/`:
469	
470	```text
471	.claude/handoffs/
472	+-- 20250115-1430-order-service-handoff.md
473	+-- 20250116-0900-auth-refactor-handoff.md
474	```
475	
476	---
477	
478	## Conflict Detection and Resolution
479	
480	### Prevention Strategies
481	
482	| Shared Resource          | How to Coordinate                                        |
483	| ------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------- |
484	| **Database schema**      | Never migrate without checking other worktrees           |
485	| **Package dependencies** | Commit lock files; let package manager resolve           |
486	| **Shared interfaces**    | Use interfaces; do not modify contracts without coordination |
487	| **Test data**            | Use unique identifiers per worktree                      |
488	| **Configuration**        | Keep environment-specific config in `.env` (not tracked) |
489	
490	### Detecting Conflicts Early
491	
492	```bash
493	# Check if your branch has diverged from main
494	git fetch origin
495	git log --oneline origin/main..HEAD  # Your commits not on main
496	git log --oneline HEAD..origin/main  # Main commits not on your branch
497	
498	# Check for potential conflicts before merging
499	git merge-tree $(git merge-base HEAD origin/main) HEAD origin/main
500	```
501	
502	### Resolving Conflicts Across Worktrees
503	
504	When two worktrees modify the same files:
505	
506	1. **Merge the simpler/smaller change first**
507	2. **Rebase the remaining worktree on updated main**:
508	   ```bash
509	   cd ~/Developer/<project>-worktrees/<remaining-agent>
510	   git fetch origin
511	   git rebase origin/main
512	   # Resolve conflicts
513	   git add <resolved-files>
514	   git rebase --continue
515	   ```
516	3. **Re-run full validation** after resolving conflicts
517	4. **Update the PR** with force-push if rebased: `git push --force-with-lease`
518	
519	### Shared Interface Changes
520	
521	If one agent needs to change a shared interface:
522	
523	1. Create the interface change as a **separate, small PR**
524	2. Merge it first (Layer 0)
525	3. All other agents rebase onto the updated main
526	4. Continue with their feature work
527	
528	---
529	
530	## Worktree Cleanup and Maintenance
531	
532	### Routine Cleanup
533	
534	After a PR is merged:
535	
536	```bash
537	# Remove the worktree
538	git worktree remove ~/Developer/<project>-worktrees/<agent-name>
539	
540	# Delete the feature branch
541	git branch -d feature/<feature-name>
542	
543	# Prune stale worktree references
544	git worktree prune
545	```
546	
547	### Lifecycle Per Layer
548	
549	```bash
550	# 1. Pull latest main after previous layer merges
551	git checkout main && git pull origin main
552	
553	# 2. Clean up old worktrees
554	git worktree remove ~/Developer/<project>-worktrees/<old-agent>
555	git branch -d <old-branch>
556	
557	# 3. Create fresh worktrees for new layer
558	git worktree add ~/Developer/<project>-worktrees/<new-agent> -b feat/<new-story> main
559	
560	# 4. Launch agents, pipeline PRs as they complete
561	
562	# 5. After all merge, repeat for next layer
563	```
564	
565	### Listing and Auditing Worktrees
566	
567	```bash
568	# List all active worktrees
569	git worktree list
570	
571	# Show detailed info (including prunable entries)
572	git worktree list --verbose
573	
574	# Remove all stale references
575	git worktree prune --verbose
576	```
577	
578	---
579	
580	## Troubleshooting
581	
582	### Port Conflicts
583	
584	**Symptom**: `Error: listen EADDRINUSE: address already in use`
585	
586	**Solution**:
587	
588	```bash
589	# Find what is using the port
590	lsof -i :<port>
591	
592	# Kill the process or use a different port
593	PORT=<new-port> npm run dev
594	```
595	
596	### Cache Key Collisions
597	
598	**Symptom**: Unexpected cache data or overwrites between worktrees
599	
600	**Solution**: Ensure `CACHE_KEY_PREFIX` is set per worktree:
601	
602	```bash
603	export CACHE_KEY_PREFIX="$(basename $PWD):"
604	```
605	
606	### Build Cache Problems
607	
608	**Symptom**: Stale builds, incorrect types after switching branches
609	
610	**Solution**:
611	
612	```bash
613	# Clear build caches
614	rm -rf dist node_modules/.cache node_modules/.vite
615	
616	# Reinstall dependencies
617	npm install
618	
619	# Rebuild
620	npm run build
621	```
622	
623	### Worktree Creation Fails
624	
625	**Symptom**: `fatal: 'branch-name' is already checked out at...`
626	
627	**Cause**: The branch is checked out in another worktree.
628	
629	**Solution**:
630	
631	```bash
632	# Create a new branch for this worktree
633	git worktree add ~/Developer/<project>-worktrees/<name> -b <new-branch-name>
634	
635	# Or check out a different branch in the existing worktree first
636	```
637	
638	### Stale Worktrees
639	
640	**Symptom**: Old worktrees taking up space or causing confusion.
641	
642	**Solution**:
643	
644	```bash
645	# List all worktrees
646	git worktree list
647	
648	# Remove a stale worktree
649	git worktree remove ~/Developer/<project>-worktrees/<old-name>
650	
651	# Prune worktree references for manually deleted directories
652	git worktree prune
653	```
654	
655	---
656	
657	## Quick Reference
658	
659	| Operation                 | Command                                                         |
660	| ------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
661	| Create worktree           | `git worktree add <path> -b <branch> [start-point]`            |
662	| List worktrees            | `git worktree list`                                             |
663	| Remove worktree           | `git worktree remove <path>`                                    |
664	| Prune stale references    | `git worktree prune`                                            |
665	| View file from branch     | `git show <branch>:<filepath>`                                  |
666	| Diff against main         | `git diff main -- <path>`                                       |
667	| Check divergence          | `git log --oneline origin/main..HEAD`                           |
668	| Fetch + rebase on main    | `git fetch origin && git rebase origin/main`                    |
669	| Force push after rebase   | `git push --force-with-lease`                                   |
670	
671	---
672

1	# Coding Standards
2	
3	When reviewing or writing code in a metaswarm-managed project, enforce the following standards. Flag violations clearly and suggest specific fixes. Be direct and specific.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## TypeScript Discipline
8	
9	Use TypeScript's type inference wherever it produces clear, unambiguous types. Do not annotate what the compiler already knows.
10	
11	Explicitly annotate function signatures (parameters and return types), exported interfaces, and public API boundaries -- these are contracts, not suggestions.
12	
13	Never use `any`. If you are reaching for `any`, you have not understood the data shape yet. Use `unknown` and narrow, or define the type properly.
14	
15	Prefer `interface` for object shapes and `type` for unions, intersections, and computed types. Do not mix them randomly.
16	
17	Use discriminated unions instead of optional fields and type guards instead of type assertions (`as`).
18	
19	Generics should clarify, not obscure. If a generic makes the code harder to read, you have over-abstracted.
20	
21	### Strict Mode
22	
23	All projects must use `"strict": true` in `tsconfig.json`. This enables:
24	
25	- `strictNullChecks` -- no implicit null/undefined
26	- `noImplicitAny` -- no inferred `any`
27	- `strictFunctionTypes` -- correct variance checking
28	- `strictPropertyInitialization` -- class fields must be initialized
29	
30	### Type Cast Hierarchy
31	
32	When you must cast (e.g., in tests), use the least permissive option:
33	
34	1. **Type narrowing** (best) -- `if ('field' in obj)`, `typeof`, `instanceof`
35	2. **Generic constraints** -- `function process<T extends Base>(item: T)`
36	3. **`as Type`** -- only when narrowing is not possible and you can prove correctness
37	4. **`as never`** -- test DI wiring only
38	5. **`as unknown as Type`** -- rare, for genuinely incompatible types
39	6. **`as any`** -- NEVER. Not in production code. Not in tests. Not in config.
40	
41	---
42	
43	## SOLID Principles (Non-Negotiable)
44	
45	**Single Responsibility**: Every module, class, and function does one thing. If you need the word "and" to describe what it does, split it.
46	
47	**Open/Closed**: Extend behavior through composition, strategy, or decoration -- not by modifying existing code. New features should not require editing working code.
48	
49	**Liskov Substitution**: Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types without breaking callers. If your subclass throws where the parent does not, or ignores parent behavior, it is broken.
50	
51	**Interface Segregation**: Do not force consumers to depend on methods they do not use. Prefer small, focused interfaces over fat ones.
52	
53	**Dependency Inversion**: Depend on abstractions (interfaces), not concretions. High-level modules should never import low-level implementation details directly.
54	
55	---
56	
57	## Dependency Injection
58	
59	Inject dependencies through constructors or factory functions. Never instantiate dependencies inline (e.g., `new EmailService()` inside a handler).
60	
61	Use DI for anything with side effects: database access, HTTP clients, email/notification services, file system, logging, time/clock.
62	
63	This is not optional -- it is the foundation of testable code. If a module cannot be tested without hitting a real database or API, the architecture is wrong.
64	
65	```typescript
66	// GOOD -- dependencies are explicit and injectable
67	class OrderService {
68	  constructor(
69	    private readonly db: DatabaseClient,
70	    private readonly paymentGateway: PaymentGateway,
71	    private readonly logger: Logger
72	  ) {}
73	}
74	
75	// BAD -- hidden, untestable dependencies
76	class OrderService {
77	  private db = new DatabaseClient(process.env.DB_URL!);
78	  private gateway = new PaymentClient(process.env.PAYMENT_KEY!);
79	}
80	```
81	
82	---
83	
84	## Design Patterns (Use When They Solve Real Problems)
85	
86	Apply patterns where they reduce complexity. Do not use them to show off.
87	
88	**Strategy**: When behavior varies by context. Replace conditional chains (`if/else`, `switch`) that select between algorithms with injectable strategy objects.
89	
90	**Factory / Abstract Factory**: When object creation logic is complex or context-dependent. Centralize creation, keep business logic clean.
91	
92	**Observer / Event Emitter**: When components need to react to changes without tight coupling. Prefer typed event systems.
93	
94	**Decorator**: When you need to layer behavior (logging, caching, retry, auth) without modifying the underlying implementation. Middleware stacks are decorators.
95	
96	**Adapter**: When integrating external APIs or legacy code whose interface does not match yours. Wrap it, do not leak it.
97	
98	**Command**: When operations need to be queued, undone, logged, or retried. Encapsulate the operation as an object.
99	
100	**Repository**: Separate data access from business logic. Business logic never writes raw queries.
101	
102	**Avoid Singleton** unless absolutely necessary (and it almost never is). If you think you need one, you probably need DI with a shared instance instead.
103	
104	---
105	
106	## Error Handling
107	
108	Use typed, domain-specific error classes -- not raw `throw new Error("something broke")`.
109	
110	```typescript
111	// GOOD -- typed errors with context
112	class OrderNotFoundError extends Error {
113	  constructor(public readonly orderId: string) {
114	    super(`Order not found: ${orderId}`);
115	    this.name = "OrderNotFoundError";
116	  }
117	}
118	
119	// GOOD -- Result type for expected failures
120	type Result<T, E = Error> =
121	  | { success: true; data: T }
122	  | { success: false; error: E };
123	```
124	
125	Rules:
126	
127	- Never swallow errors silently. If you catch, either handle it meaningfully, transform it, or rethrow with context.
128	- Prefer result types (`{ success, data } | { success, error }`) for expected failure cases. Reserve exceptions for truly exceptional situations.
129	- Every error should be traceable. Include enough context (what operation, what input, what state) to debug without reproducing.
130	- Never `catch (e: any)`. Narrow your catch, type your errors.
131	
132	---
133	
134	## Naming Conventions
135	
136	### Files
137	
138	- Services: `order.service.ts`
139	- Utilities: `string-utils.ts`
140	- Types/interfaces: `order.types.ts`
141	- Constants: `config.ts` or `constants.ts`
142	- Tests: `order.service.test.ts` (in `__tests__/` directory)
143	- Use kebab-case for file names
144	
145	### Classes, Interfaces, Types
146	
147	- PascalCase: `OrderService`, `PaymentGateway`, `CreateOrderInput`
148	- Prefix interfaces with intent, not `I`: `PaymentGateway` not `IPaymentGateway`
149	
150	### Functions and Variables
151	
152	- camelCase: `calculateDiscount`, `orderTotal`
153	- Boolean: `isValid`, `hasPermission`, `shouldRetry` -- not `valid`, `check`, `flag`
154	- Descriptive: `normalizeAddresses()` not `processData()`
155	- No abbreviations unless universally understood (`id`, `url`, `config` are fine; `usr`, `mgr`, `proc` are not)
156	
157	### Constants
158	
159	- UPPER_SNAKE_CASE for true constants: `MAX_RETRY_COUNT`, `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS`
160	
161	---
162	
163	## Import Organization
164	
165	Organize imports in consistent groups, separated by blank lines:
166	
167	```typescript
168	// 1. Node built-ins
169	import { readFile } from "node:fs/promises";
170	import path from "node:path";
171	
172	// 2. External packages
173	import { z } from "zod";
174	
175	// 3. Internal aliases (@/ or ~/)
176	import { OrderService } from "@/services/order.service";
177	import { Logger } from "@/lib/logger";
178	
179	// 4. Relative imports
180	import { validateInput } from "./validators";
181	import type { OrderInput } from "./types";
182	```
183	
184	Rules:
185	
186	- Use `import type` for type-only imports
187	- No circular dependencies -- if A imports B and B imports A, extract shared code to C
188	- No barrel file abuse -- only use `index.ts` re-exports when the module boundary is intentional
189	
190	---
191	
192	## Function and Module Design
193	
194	Functions should be short and do one thing. If it is over 30 lines, it probably does too much.
195	
196	Max 3-4 parameters. If you need more, use an options/config object.
197	
198	Prefer pure functions where possible. Side effects should be pushed to the edges of the system.
199	
200	Never mutate function arguments. Return new values.
201	
202	Avoid deeply nested code. Early returns, guard clauses, and extraction into helper functions keep nesting flat.
203	
204	No magic numbers or strings. Use named constants or enums.
205	
206	---
207	
208	## Code Organization
209	
210	Organize by feature/domain, not by technical layer. `users/user.service.ts` not `services/user-service.ts`.
211	
212	Keep related code close together. If you always change two files in tandem, they should be in the same module.
213	
214	Explicit exports. Do not use barrel files (`index.ts` re-exporting everything) unless the module boundary is intentional and well-defined.
215	
216	Circular dependencies are architecture bugs. If A depends on B and B depends on A, extract the shared concept into C.
217	
218	---
219	
220	## Async and Concurrency
221	
222	Never fire-and-forget a Promise. Every promise must be awaited, returned, or explicitly handled with `.catch()`.
223	
224	Use `Promise.all` / `Promise.allSettled` for parallel work. Sequential `await` in a loop is usually a performance bug.
225	
226	Handle race conditions explicitly. If two operations can interleave, the code must account for it.
227	
228	Timeouts on all external calls. No unbounded waits.
229	
230	---
231	
232	## Security and Data Handling
233	
234	Validate and sanitize all external input at the boundary. Never trust user input, API responses, or file contents.
235	
236	Never log sensitive data (tokens, passwords, PII). Redact before logging.
237	
238	Use parameterized queries. No string concatenation for SQL or any query language.
239	
240	Secrets come from environment variables or a secrets manager, never from code or config files committed to the repo.
241	
242	---
243	
244	## Performance Awareness
245	
246	Do not optimize prematurely, but do not be negligent either. N+1 queries, unbounded list operations, and missing indexes are bugs, not optimization tasks.
247	
248	Pagination for any list endpoint. No unbounded queries.
249	
250	Cache intentionally with clear invalidation strategies. Ad-hoc caching creates stale data bugs.
251	
252	Be conscious of memory: do not load entire datasets into memory when you can stream or paginate.
253	
254	---
255	
256	## See Also
257	
258	- [Testing Patterns Guide](./testing-patterns.md) -- Test quality and coverage standards
259	- [Build Validation Guide](./build-validation.md) -- Validation workflows
260	- [Git Workflow Guide](./git-workflow.md) -- Commit and PR conventions
261

1	# Security Review Rubric
2	
3	**Used By**: Security Auditor Agent
4	**Purpose**: Identify security vulnerabilities before code reaches production
5	**Version**: 1.0
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Overview
10	
11	This rubric ensures code changes don't introduce security vulnerabilities. Based on OWASP Top 10 and security best practices. Any CRITICAL finding blocks the PR.
12	
13	---
14	
15	## Severity Levels
16	
17	| Level        | Description                                    | Action                           |
18	| ------------ | ---------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
19	| **CRITICAL** | Exploitable vulnerability, data breach risk    | MUST fix immediately, blocks PR  |
20	| **HIGH**     | Security weakness, defense-in-depth gap        | MUST fix before merge            |
21	| **MEDIUM**   | Hardening opportunity, best practice violation | SHOULD fix, document if deferred |
22	| **LOW**      | Minor improvement, future consideration        | OPTIONAL, note for backlog       |
23	
24	---
25	
26	## OWASP Top 10 Checklist
27	
28	### A01:2021 - Broken Access Control
29	
30	| Check                 | Severity | What to Look For                                     |
31	| --------------------- | -------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
32	| Missing auth checks   | CRITICAL | Routes without auth middleware                       |
33	| Missing org scoping   | CRITICAL | Database queries without `where: { organizationId }` |
34	| IDOR vulnerabilities  | CRITICAL | User-supplied IDs used without ownership check       |
35	| Privilege escalation  | CRITICAL | Role checks missing or bypassable                    |
36	| CORS misconfiguration | HIGH     | Overly permissive origins                            |
37	
38	**Code Patterns to Flag**:
39	
40	```typescript
41	// CRITICAL - No auth check
42	app.get("/api/users", async c => {
43	  const users = await prisma.user.findMany(); // No auth middleware!
44	});
45	
46	// CRITICAL - No org scoping (data leak)
47	const accounts = await prisma.emailAccount.findMany({
48	  where: { email: searchEmail }, // Missing organizationId!
49	});
50	
51	// CRITICAL - IDOR vulnerability
52	const account = await prisma.emailAccount.findUnique({
53	  where: { id: params.accountId }, // No org ownership verification!
54	});
55	```
56	
57	---
58	
59	### A02:2021 - Cryptographic Failures
60	
61	| Check           | Severity | What to Look For                      |
62	| --------------- | -------- | ------------------------------------- |
63	| Secrets in code | CRITICAL | API keys, passwords, tokens hardcoded |
64	| Secrets in logs | CRITICAL | Logging sensitive data                |
65	| Weak encryption | HIGH     | MD5, SHA1 for passwords               |
66	| Missing HTTPS   | HIGH     | HTTP URLs for APIs                    |
67	| Insecure random | MEDIUM   | Math.random() for security            |
68	
69	**Code Patterns to Flag**:
70	
71	```typescript
72	// CRITICAL - Hardcoded secret
73	const API_KEY = "sk_live_abc123...";
74	
75	// CRITICAL - Secret in logs
76	logger.info({ apiKey, token }, "Making request");
77	
78	// HIGH - Weak hashing
79	const hash = crypto.createHash("md5").update(password);
80	```
81	
82	---
83	
84	### A03:2021 - Injection
85	
86	| Check             | Severity | What to Look For                |
87	| ----------------- | -------- | ------------------------------- |
88	| SQL injection     | CRITICAL | String concatenation in queries |
89	| NoSQL injection   | CRITICAL | Unvalidated objects in queries  |
90	| Command injection | CRITICAL | User input in shell commands    |
91	| LDAP injection    | CRITICAL | User input in LDAP queries      |
92	| XPath injection   | HIGH     | User input in XPath             |
93	
94	**Code Patterns to Flag**:
95	
96	```typescript
97	// CRITICAL - SQL injection
98	const query = `SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '${userName}'`;
99	
100	// CRITICAL - Command injection
101	exec(`convert ${userFilename} output.png`);
102	
103	// CRITICAL - NoSQL injection
104	prisma.user.findMany({ where: JSON.parse(userInput) });
105	```
106	
107	---
108	
109	### A04:2021 - Insecure Design
110	
111	| Check                             | Severity | What to Look For                |
112	| --------------------------------- | -------- | ------------------------------- |
113	| Missing rate limiting             | HIGH     | APIs without throttling         |
114	| No CAPTCHA on sensitive ops       | MEDIUM   | Registration, password reset    |
115	| Excessive data exposure           | HIGH     | Returning more data than needed |
116	| Missing business logic validation | HIGH     | Assuming client-side validation |
117	
118	---
119	
120	### A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration
121	
122	| Check                    | Severity | What to Look For             |
123	| ------------------------ | -------- | ---------------------------- |
124	| Debug mode in prod       | HIGH     | Development settings exposed |
125	| Default credentials      | CRITICAL | Unchanged passwords          |
126	| Unnecessary features     | MEDIUM   | Unused endpoints, features   |
127	| Missing security headers | MEDIUM   | CSP, X-Frame-Options, etc.   |
128	| Error details exposed    | HIGH     | Stack traces to users        |
129	
130	---
131	
132	### A06:2021 - Vulnerable Components
133	
134	| Check                  | Severity      | What to Look For                |
135	| ---------------------- | ------------- | ------------------------------- |
136	| Known CVEs             | CRITICAL/HIGH | Outdated dependencies with CVEs |
137	| Unmaintained packages  | MEDIUM        | No updates in 2+ years          |
138	| Excessive dependencies | LOW           | Large attack surface            |
139	
140	**Check Command**:
141	
142	```bash
143	pnpm audit
144	```
145	
146	---
147	
148	### A07:2021 - Auth/Session Failures
149	
150	| Check                    | Severity | What to Look For               |
151	| ------------------------ | -------- | ------------------------------ |
152	| Weak password policy     | MEDIUM   | No complexity requirements     |
153	| Session fixation         | HIGH     | Session ID not rotated on auth |
154	| Missing MFA              | MEDIUM   | Sensitive ops without 2FA      |
155	| Insecure session storage | HIGH     | Tokens in localStorage         |
156	| No session timeout       | MEDIUM   | Long-lived sessions            |
157	
158	---
159	
160	### A08:2021 - Software/Data Integrity
161	
162	| Check                    | Severity | What to Look For                     |
163	| ------------------------ | -------- | ------------------------------------ |
164	| Unsigned updates         | HIGH     | CI/CD without verification           |
165	| Deserialization attacks  | CRITICAL | Unvalidated JSON.parse of user input |
166	| Missing integrity checks | MEDIUM   | External resources without SRI       |
167	
168	---
169	
170	### A09:2021 - Logging/Monitoring Failures
171	
172	| Check              | Severity | What to Look For       |
173	| ------------------ | -------- | ---------------------- |
174	| Missing audit logs | MEDIUM   | Auth events not logged |
175	| PII in logs        | HIGH     | Logging personal data  |
176	| No alerting        | MEDIUM   | Failures not monitored |
177	
178	---
179	
180	### A10:2021 - SSRF
181	
182	| Check                   | Severity | What to Look For                       |
183	| ----------------------- | -------- | -------------------------------------- |
184	| Unvalidated URLs        | CRITICAL | User-supplied URLs fetched server-side |
185	| Internal network access | CRITICAL | Fetching localhost, internal IPs       |
186	| Protocol bypass         | HIGH     | file://, gopher://, etc.               |
187	
188	**Code Patterns to Flag**:
189	
190	```typescript
191	// CRITICAL - SSRF vulnerability
192	const response = await fetch(userProvidedUrl); // No validation!
193	
194	// CRITICAL - Internal network access
195	if (url.includes("localhost") || url.includes("127.0.0.1")) {
196	  // This check is bypassable!
197	}
198	```
199

319	
320	## Approval Criteria
321	
322	**APPROVED** when:
323	
324	- Zero CRITICAL findings
325	- Zero HIGH findings (or accepted with documented justification)
326	- All OWASP categories checked
327	
328	**BLOCKED** when:
329	
330	- Any CRITICAL finding exists
331	- Multiple HIGH findings without mitigation plan
332	
333	---
334	
335	## Common Vulnerability Patterns
336	
337	### Authentication Bypass
338	
339	```typescript
340	// WRONG - Trusting client-side check
341	if (req.headers['x-admin'] === 'true') { ... }
342	
343	// RIGHT - Server-side auth via middleware
344	// Middleware verifies JWT and provides auth context
345	const { userId, orgId } = c.get("auth");
346	if (!userId) { throw new UnauthorizedError(); }
347	```
348	
349	### Authorization Bypass
350	
351	```typescript
352	// WRONG - No org scoping
353	const account = await prisma.emailAccount.findUnique({
354	  where: { id: accountId },
355	});
356	
357	// RIGHT - Organization-scoped query
358	const account = await prisma.emailAccount.findUnique({
359	  where: { id: accountId, organizationId: auth.orgId },
360	});
361	```
362	
363	### Input Validation
364	
365	```typescript
366	// WRONG - Trusting user input
367	const data = JSON.parse(req.body);
368	await prisma.user.update({ where: { id: data.id }, data });
369	
370	// RIGHT - Schema validation
371	const schema = z.object({
372	  id: z.string().uuid(),
373	  name: z.string().max(100),
374	});
375	const data = schema.parse(await req.json());
376	```
377

1	# Architecture Rubric
2	
3	**Used By**: Architect Agent
4	**Purpose**: Ensure implementation plans follow codebase architecture and patterns
5	**Version**: 1.0
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Overview
10	
11	This rubric evaluates implementation plans for architectural fit. It ensures new code follows established patterns, uses correct service layers, and maintains codebase consistency.
12	
13	---
14	
15	## Key Reference Documents
16	
17	| Document                                | Purpose                                 |
18	| --------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
19	| `CLAUDE.md`                             | Architecture overview, coding standards |
20	| `./guides/testing-patterns.md`    | Testing philosophy, mock factories, TDD |
21	| `test-quality-anti-patterns.md (project-specific)` | Common testing mistakes to avoid        |
22	| `docs/SERVICE_INVENTORY.md`             | Existing services & factory catalog     |
23	| TypeScript strict mode                  | Follow conventions from tsconfig.json and ESLint configuration |
24	
25	---
26	
27	## Evaluation Categories
28	
29	### 1. Service Layer Placement
30	
31	| Layer                     | Purpose                         | Location                             |
32	| ------------------------- | ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
33	| **API Routes**            | HTTP handling only              | `src/api/routes/`                    |
34	| **Pure Services**         | Business logic, no side effects | `src/lib/services/pure-*.ts`         |
35	| **Persistence Services**  | Database operations             | `src/lib/services/*-persistence.ts`  |
36	| **Orchestrator Services** | Coordinate multiple services    | `src/lib/services/*-orchestrator.ts` |
37	| **Adapters**              | External API wrappers           | `src/lib/services/*-adapter.ts`      |
38	
39	**Check**: Is the proposed code in the correct layer?
40	
41	```typescript
42	// WRONG - Business logic in API route
43	export async function POST(req) {
44	  const data = await req.json();
45	  // Complex business logic here...
46	  await prisma.user.create({ ... });
47	}
48	
49	// RIGHT - API route delegates to service
50	export async function POST(req) {
51	  const data = await req.json();
52	  const result = await userService.createUser(data);
53	  return c.json(result);
54	}
55	```
56	
57	---
58	
59	### 2. Dependency Injection
60	
61	| Criterion                    | Pass                | Fail                         |
62	| ---------------------------- | ------------------- | ---------------------------- |
63	| Dependencies via constructor | All deps injected   | Direct imports of singletons |
64	| Interfaces for external deps | Abstract boundaries | Concrete implementations     |
65	| No hidden dependencies       | All deps visible    | Global state, implicit deps  |
66	
67	**Check**: Are dependencies properly injected?
68	
69	```typescript
70	// WRONG - Hidden dependency
71	export class UserService {
72	  async getUser(id: string) {
73	    const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { id } }); // prisma is global!
74	  }
75	}
76	
77	// RIGHT - Injected dependency
78	export class UserService {
79	  constructor(private readonly prisma: PrismaClient) {}
80	
81	  async getUser(id: string) {
82	    return this.prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { id } });
83	  }
84	}
85	```
86	
87	---
88	
89	### 3. Pattern Consistency
90	
91	| Pattern             | When to Use                    | Example                          |
92	| ------------------- | ------------------------------ | -------------------------------- |
93	| **Strategy**        | Multiple algorithms            | AI providers, payment processors |
94	| **Template Method** | Common workflow, varying steps | Pipeline processing              |
95	| **Factory**         | Complex object creation        | Mock factories                   |
96	| **Repository**      | Data access abstraction        | Database operations              |
97	| **Adapter**         | External API integration       | Gmail, Stripe, PostHog           |
98	
99	**Check**: Does the plan use patterns consistent with similar features?
100	
101	---
102	
103	### 4. Naming Conventions
104	
105	| Type          | Convention                       | Example                          |
106	| ------------- | -------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
107	| Pure services | `pure-*.service.ts`              | `pure-scoring.service.ts`        |
108	| Persistence   | `*-persistence.service.ts`       | `contact-persistence.service.ts` |
109	| Orchestrators | `*-orchestrator.service.ts`      | `draft-orchestrator.service.ts`  |
110	| Adapters      | `*-adapter.ts` or `*.adapter.ts` | `gmail-adapter.ts`               |
111	| Types         | `*.types.ts` or in `types.ts`    | `contact.types.ts`               |
112	| Schemas       | `src/lib/schemas/[domain]/`      | `src/lib/schemas/beads/`         |
113	
114	**Check**: Do file and class names follow conventions?
115	
116	---
117	
118	### 5. Data Flow
119	
120	| Principle        | Description                                    |
121	| ---------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
122	| Unidirectional   | Data flows one direction through layers        |
123	| No circular deps | Services don't depend on each other circularly |
124	| Clear boundaries | Each layer has defined responsibilities        |
125	
126	```
127	API Route → Orchestrator → Pure Service → Persistence → Database
128	                ↓
129	            Adapter → External API
130	```
131	
132	**Check**: Does data flow correctly through layers?
133	
134	---
135	
136	### 6. Error Handling Strategy
137	
138	| Layer         | Error Handling                       |
139	| ------------- | ------------------------------------ |
140	| API Routes    | Catch, log, return HTTP status       |
141	| Orchestrators | Coordinate error handling, may retry |
142	| Pure Services | Throw typed errors, no side effects  |
143	| Persistence   | Wrap database errors                 |
144	| Adapters      | Wrap external API errors             |
145	
146	**Check**: Is error handling appropriate for each layer?
147	
148	---
149	
150	### 7. Database Considerations
151	
152	| Check          | Requirement               |
153	| -------------- | ------------------------- |
154	| Schema changes | Migration required?       |
155	| Indexes        | New queries need indexes? |
156	| Relations      | Foreign keys correct?     |
157	| Soft deletes   | Using deletedAt pattern?  |
158	| Multi-tenancy  | userId on all user data?  |
159	
160	**Check**: Are database operations properly planned?
161	
162	---
163	
164	## Review Checklist
165	
166	```markdown
167	### Service Placement
168	
169	- [ ] Code is in correct layer (API/Service/Persistence)
170	- [ ] No business logic in API routes
171	- [ ] No HTTP concerns in services
172	
173	### Dependency Injection
174	
175	- [ ] All dependencies via constructor
176	- [ ] No global singletons imported
177	- [ ] Testable with mock injection
178	
179	### Pattern Consistency
180	
181	- [ ] Uses same patterns as similar features
182	- [ ] No unnecessary new patterns
183	- [ ] Patterns match complexity level
184	
185	### Naming Conventions
186	
187	- [ ] File names follow conventions
188	- [ ] Class/function names are descriptive
189	- [ ] Matches existing codebase style
190	
191	### Data Flow
192	
193	- [ ] Clear layer separation
194	- [ ] No circular dependencies
195	- [ ] Appropriate abstraction levels
196	
197	### Error Handling
198	
199	- [ ] Errors handled at correct layer
200	- [ ] Typed errors used
201	- [ ] User-facing errors are clear
202	
203	### Database
204	
205	- [ ] Migrations planned if needed
206	- [ ] Indexes for new queries
207	- [ ] userId filter on user data
208	```
209	
210	---
211	
212	## Output Format
213	
214	```markdown
215	## Architecture Review: <task-id>
216	
217	### Verdict: APPROVED | NEEDS REVISION
218	
219	### Summary
220	
221	<Brief assessment of architectural fit>
222	
223	---

226	
227	**Status**: Correct | Needs Adjustment
228	
229	<Analysis of where code should live>
230

232
